According to:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg20/4.3.1

"DLL support is available for applications running under z/OS in batch or in

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg20/4.3.4

does "confuse" me a bit as to what you do to get from the DLL program-id to the
PGM= name.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program name length limitations was Re: GOBACK

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T01:25:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40aab409.115473674@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405161205.41d6aab3@posting.google.com> <40a7e618.491317038@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405162345.2977d4fb@posting.google.com> <40a9021b.4370945@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171810.4c44d8a7@posting.google.com> <40a9732c.33320929@news.optonline.net> <c8cu5u$90i$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <ocuqc.997$be.130@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>environments."

A Unix DLL is called an 'so' (shared object). A single linker/binder program
named 'ld' does both static and dynamic binding. In the case of dynamic, it is
called by the operating system. In the case of static, it is called by the
compiler. It can be executed as a separate step, but seldom is. 

The default and norm is dynamic binding.

As with Microsoft, getting rid of a loaded 'so' is impossible or a PITA. This
makes repeated testing during development somewhat .. challenging. The most
common technique involves 'version control' appending a number -- .1, .2 ... --
to the file name and using a symbolic link (without the version number) to point
to the most recent.  That's why files in /lib directories so often have version
numbers at the end.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<10al95dr1892ke0@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c8cu5u$90i$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405161205.41d6aab3@posting.google.com> <40a7e618.491317038@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405162345.2977d4fb@posting.google.com> <40a9021b.4370945@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171810.4c44d8a7@posting.google.com> <40a9732c.33320929@news.optonline.net> <c8cu5u$90i$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`

```
Clark F. Morris, Jr. wrote:

> It depends on how the program handling is done by the operating system. 
>  We still have the 8 byte limitation on member names in z/OS (IBM 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ex-Burroughs and ex-Univac mainframe series.

Entry point names are limited to 24 characters on the 2200 (old 
Univac/Sperry).  You can specify up to 30 characters on the Program-ID 
clause, but only the first 24 will be visible if you're using it as a 
subprogram.  (I'm not 100% on that second part, but it seems that I had 
an entry point get truncated when I was doing some "research 
programming"...  I know the 30-character Program-ID compiled.)
```

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T21:11:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-0ADF9E.21111420052004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405161205.41d6aab3@posting.google.com> <40a7e618.491317038@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405162345.2977d4fb@posting.google.com> <40a9021b.4370945@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171810.4c44d8a7@posting.google.com> <40a9732c.33320929@news.optonline.net> <c8cu5u$90i$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`

```
In article <c8cu5u$90i$1@news.eusc.inter.net>,
 "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

> It depends on how the program handling is done by the operating system. 
>   We still have the 8 byte limitation on member names in z/OS (IBM 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ex-Burroughs and ex-Univac mainframe series.

You can use the long name option on any type of program object.  The 
only limitation is, and I may be wrong, you cannot use names greater 
than 8 chars in the old-style (obsolete) PDS format.  In the new library 
format, PDSE, you can use long names.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program name length limitations was Re: GOBACK

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T08:22:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8ksan$qut$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40a9021b.4370945@news.optonline.net> <c8krdc$ade$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`

```
In article <c8krdc$ade$1@news.eusc.inter.net>,
Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
>Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

[snip]

>> You can use the long name option on any type of program object.  The 
>> only limitation is, and I may be wrong, you cannot use names greater 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>etc.) with names longer than 8 bytes?

Hmmmm... I have no idea about the first but as for the second:

From:

<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGYLR200/8.1.4?DT=19980112234143#HDRCOPY>

--begin quoted text:

___ Format _____________________________________________________________ 
|                                                                        |
| >>__COPY__ _text-name_ __ __________________________________________>  |
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
|________________________________________________________________________|

text-name, library-name 
Text-name identifies the name of the copy text. Library-name identifies 
where the copy text exists. 




Must follow the normal rules of formation for a user-defined word: 



The word can be from 1-30 characters in length. 
The word can contain characters: A-Z, a-z, 0-9, hyphen. 
At least one characters must be alphabetic. 
The hyphen can't be the first or last character. 

--end quoted text

(should be interesting to see how the box-diagram comes out)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program name length limitations was Re: GOBACK

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T13:23:18-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8lacp$dif$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c8ksan$qut$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40a9021b.4370945@news.optonline.net> <c8krdc$ade$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <c8ksan$qut$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <c8krdc$ade$1@news.eusc.inter.net>,
> Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> --end quoted text
Reading further at the site referenced, it says that on OS390 and z/OS 
only the first 8 characters are used as the identifying name.  This is 
true for both the member name and library name.  Since the library name 
actually refers to a DDNAME (roughly equivalent to Unix Environment 
Variable for linking to the file name expressed in the script) it 
further has the limitations for DDNAMES in terms of legal characters.
> 
> (should be interesting to see how the box-diagram comes out)
> 
> DD
>
```

>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

[snip]

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

>> text-name, library-name

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>only the first 8 characters are used as the identifying name.

How interesting... so it would seem that COPY COPYMBR01 and COPY COPYMBR02 
would both bring in COPYMBR0.

'You can use up to 30 characters... but we'll only keep track of 8.'

>This is 
>true for both the member name and library name.  Since the library name 
>actually refers to a DDNAME (roughly equivalent to Unix Environment 
>Variable for linking to the file name expressed in the script) it 
>further has the limitations for DDNAMES in terms of legal characters.

The DDNAME is the province of JCL, about which I've previously admitted
ignorance... but one learns something new every day.  Just for laffs I
tried this, I created a copymember FRED0000 in MYUID.DELME.COBOL; in a
handy skeleton-program I coded COPY FRED0000 IN MYCOPY and in my compile
jcl I included

//MYCOPY  DD  DSN=MYUID.DELME.COBOL,DISP=SHR

... and it ran like a watch.

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8fu62089h@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405161205.41d6aab3@posting.google.com> <40a7e618.491317038@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405162345.2977d4fb@posting.google.com> <40a9021b.4370945@news.optonline.net>`

```

In article <40a9021b.4370945@news.optonline.net>, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) writes:
> 
> Python and Perl (now) are OO. Assuming I can call C libraries from Cobol, I
> can't think of any problem where C is more suitable.

Implement the Unix System V kernel in Python, Perl, or COBOL.  You
may use Maurice J Bach's _The Design of the UNIX Operating System_
as a reference.  And pretty much anything else you like.

Write the firmware for an embedded controller in Python, Perl, or
COBOL.  The controller is based on an 8-bit microcontroller CPU with
8 KB of ROM and 8 KB of RAM.  It must handle various input lines and
generate the correct output in real time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T19:50:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a91769.9825578@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <40a16cfb.67032548@news.optonline.net> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405161205.41d6aab3@posting.google.com> <40a7e618.491317038@news.optonline.net> <c8ao3q$4e4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On 16-May-2004, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
>> I want Cobol to be regarded by people in other cultures as a serious
>> general-purpose programming language, not a language good only for
"accounting
>> and stuff like that". To gain that respect, we must use Cobol for more than
>> accounting. I'm not saying what we should be working on, only that what most
>> of
>> us did in the past wasn't enough. And debating issues like paragraph
numbering
>> while the ship slowly sinks is futile.
>
>I've seen CoBOL used for mainframe applications of all kinds, but when shops
add
>web pages, they usually get Suns and use other languages.   It doesn't seem to
>be a function of "accounting" vs "database" vs "reporting".
>
>Specifically, what applications do you notice that are not now using CoBOL, but
>should be?

In the applications arena, I'd like to work on:

..PC applications with a GUI. They're often written in VB.
..Applications that are Web enabled, now written in Cognos, Java, and others.

I've seen many shops that, upon switching from mainframe to client-server,
thought it necessary to rewrite everything in a more 'modern' language. They
don't believe me when I tell them it's unnecessary, they can use what they have
with the addition of a GUI easily written in Cobol. They think it's technically
impossible for Cobol to do a GUI or Web-style interface.

In the systems arena:

..Databases. I doubt even one is written in Cobol.
..Business Intelligence/Data Warehousing.
..Middleware. Even something as simple as an XML parser.
```

Why is CoBOL a good fit?

> ..Business Intelligence/Data Warehousing.
> ..Middleware. Even something as simple as an XML parser.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T00:06:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a94bf9.23284372@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <40a16cfb.67032548@news.optonline.net> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405161205.41d6aab3@posting.google.com> <40a7e618.491317038@news.optonline.net> <c8ao3q$4e4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a91769.9825578@news.optonline.net> <c8b8t1$i2u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 17-May-2004, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any PC applications with a GUI?   Why is CoBOL a good fit?

Because Cobol is better for writing validation logic, in a client app, and
vastly better for writing whole systems, in a standalone PC app. VB is used
because anyone, even a non-programmer, can paint screens rapidly. It's not
really programming, it's drag-and-drop. But VB is weak at writing procedural
code. As a result, VB programs sometimes have poor validation.

With appropriate tools, for instance Flexus SP2, a Cobol programmer can create
GUI screens just as rapidly. They are indistinguishable from VB and C++ screens
because they use the same OLE and Active X controls.

>> ..Applications that are Web enabled, now written in Cognos, Java, and others.
>
>Why is CoBOL a good fit?

Similar reasons. Cognos is a large BI tool, but many shops use it only as a 'Web
Report Generator' with Cobol server-style code and/or a database behind it.
Since most Cobol programmers do not know Cognos Reporting, they hire Cognos
specialists. Cobol can produce XML just as easily as Cognos can.

Management doesn't think so because it wasn't done when they were Cobol
programmers, 5-20 years ago.

>> I've seen many shops that, upon switching from mainframe to client-server,
>> thought it necessary to rewrite everything in a more 'modern' language. They
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>There are lots of silly people out there.

They're not silly, they're using old paradigms to manage modern technology.

Of the stuff invented since they stopped coding, their knowledge comes from
airline magazines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T00:36:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405172336.7f1271d5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405161205.41d6aab3@posting.google.com> <40a7e618.491317038@news.optonline.net> <c8ao3q$4e4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a91769.9825578@news.optonline.net> <c8b8t1$i2u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> Any PC applications with a GUI?   Why is CoBOL a good fit?

The first GUI Cobol program I did started when MF first released 16bit
version 2.1 with early release that included support for Windows
programs.  This was when Win 3.0 was just out.  It was no worse
writing it in Cobol than in any other language, but there was great
deal of existing Cobol code that could be reused, plus the data files
in the existing system.  This was fully graphical, not just dialog
boxes, though there were a number of those.  It was a container ship
loading system that drew the bays, the tanks, bonjean curves,
stability, torsion and bend and shear curves.  Drag and drop
containers from terminal to bays.

These days most stuff of my Cobol is done with SP/2. It gives an
excellent UI, is easy to design screens with and isolates the UI from
the processing code.
 
> > ..Applications that are Web enabled, now written in Cognos, Java, and others.
> 
> Why is CoBOL a good fit?

CGI web server programs are easy to do, though you need to deal with
the modeless connections.  Its just as easy as other languages once
you have written some utility routines to parse the input and to
output using templates, or use propritry routines, but they don't do
anything that can't be done reasonably portably in Cobol.

The advantage of using Cobol (to me) is that it can reuse existing
application code (for example to pricing and discount module) and can
directly access existing data files for order systems and others.

> > ..Databases. I doubt even one is written in Cobol.
> 
> Why is CoBOL a good fit?

I doubt that it is.
```

> ..Business Intelligence/Data Warehousing.

I have a simple XML parser in Cobol, but why is it important to be
done in Cobol ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T19:50:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405101848.5132384c@posting.google.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <40a16cfb.67032548@news.optonline.net> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 15-May-2004, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why?   What is it about PERFORM that troubles you?

..Cannot pass parameters.
..THRU becomes a Shop Standard.
..Cannot do recursion.

Actually, you can pass parameters, but I don't advocate it.
set address of input-data to xxx
set address of output-data to yyy
perform convert-x-to-y

>> Nested Programs seemed ok in principle, but bulky in practice.
>
>I have never figured out what improvement they have in principle over performs
>and external calls.   And haven't seen them actually be used beyond playing
with
>the language.

I use them. Richard says the advantage is reduced namespace pollution. I see the
advantage as reduced administrative overhead. They need not be listed on program
inventories, do not require their own documentation, need not be promoted
through the CM system, etc. Locality is an advantage if you're forced to use a
lame text editor which can edit only one file at a time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T21:00:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8b94v$i7o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405101848.5132384c@posting.google.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <40a16cfb.67032548@news.optonline.net> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 17-May-2004, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Why?   What is it about PERFORM that troubles you?
>
> ..Cannot pass parameters.
> ..THRU becomes a Shop Standard.
> ..Cannot do recursion.

Stupid shop standards can happen just as easily without PERFORM.

When I want to do recursion, I use a tool set up for that.  When I don't I don't
see any need for carrying the baggage around to require it.
When I want to protect data (using parameters), I use a tool set up for that.  
When I don't, I don't see any need for carrying the baggage around to require
it.


> >I have never figured out what improvement they have in principle over
> >performs
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> lame text editor which can edit only one file at a time.

If you don't want them available to other programs, then they are just another
way of doing PERFORMs.   A more complicated way.
```

>

>> >performs
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>way of doing PERFORMs.   A more complicated way.

isolation of external calls.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T19:38:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >Why?   What is it about PERFORM that troubles you?
> 
> ..Cannot pass parameters.

In most cases parameters are not an issue, they are only of real
significance if the code is to be reused with different data items,
your very real case of reformatting different dates, for example. 
However, in general, if it is to be something that is done for several
different data items in one program then it is likely to be useful for
other programs too.  This then leads to it being an external program,
or a class method.

> ..THRU becomes a Shop Standard.

So argue against THRU.

> ..Cannot do recursion.

There are very few real cases for using recursion.  What recursive
ENTRY was designed to support is re-entrancy.  For example Windows
callbacks.

In most cases an iteritive solution is just as good as a recursive
one.

> Richard says the advantage is reduced namespace pollution. 

Did I actually say that ?  No.  I don't mind being criticised for what
I do say, but I deny you the right to make up things that I didn't
say.  In any case it tends to show that you just didn't understand the
issue.

_Excessive_misuse_ of ENTRY causes namespace polution. 

One major advantage of nested programs over internal use of ENTRYs is
_they_work_.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T05:20:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a997e8.42726445@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>callbacks.

You argue for one call mechanism for zero parameters, another for a call with
parameters, and a third for recursion. What other language is designed that way?
The only one I can think of is Basic. Even there, GOSUB, which is analogous to
PERFORM, has been abandoned. 

When a maintenance programmer sees the need for a parameter when calling a
paragraph that formerly had none, you would have him restructure the program to
make the paragraph an external program. Not gonna happen. He or she will take
the easy way out by moving it to a temp field in working-storage.

Re-entrancy is for multi-threading. Windows callbacks are not an example of
multi-threading.

To make a program thread-safe, multiple call mechanisms would require
restructuring EVERY paragraph into something else.

I agree there are few cases for recursion, but when you need it, it's nice to
have. Tree processing is one such. 

>> Richard says the advantage is reduced namespace pollution. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>_Excessive_misuse_ of ENTRY causes namespace polution. 

You beat me over the head with namespace pollution in re ENTRY, and said Nested
Programs were "carefully designed" to avoid the problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>

```
In article <40aa3abc.84415746@news.optonline.net>,
 robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> >On 17-May-2004, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes. VB is an OO language.

I'm assuming you are talking about the new VB.NET.

It isn't really BASIC.  The virtual machine it runs on is M$ hacked up 
JVM with the serial numbers filed off. (aka CLR)

VB.NET is Java conventions and object model with a BASIC-like syntax.  
The migration from VB 6 (non-OO BASIC) to VB.NET (OO "basic") is such a 
major headache with so many gotcha's that it ought to be a clue -- they 
are not really the same language anymore.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T13:24:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405181224.6051451e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com> <40a997e8.42726445@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> You argue for one call mechanism for zero parameters, another for a call with
> parameters, and a third for recursion. 

No I don't.  For a start I never argued for recursion. I have no
interest in recursion, iterative solutions are no more arduous, and
recursion, where it is supported, can be used with the Program-Id,
just as every other call is.

The PERFORM is not a 'call without parameters', it is a block
structuring mechanism and an iteration mechanism.  It happens that it
allows (or required in older versions) the block to be separated and
made out-of-line.

> What other language is designed that way?

All of them actually, except they mostly require the blocks to be
in-line.

> The only one I can think of is Basic. 

That may not represent a limitation of 'other languages'.

> Even there, GOSUB, which is analogous to PERFORM, has been abandoned. 

BASIC is not _a_ language, it is a collection of dozens of languages
which may, or may not, be similar.  In some BASIC languages the line
numbering is not used and GOSUB syntax requires a line number.

But then Cobol avoids such issues where 'the next release' requires
all the programs to be rewritten.

> When a maintenance programmer sees the need for a parameter when calling a
> paragraph that formerly had none, 

Since when did a 'maintenance programmer' ever see the need for a
parameter ?

> you would have him restructure the program to
> make the paragraph an external program. 

I never made that requirement, you are making it up.  You seem to be
inventing things for me to say just to argue against them.

What I did say is that if some code is so useful that it should be
reused with a parameter, _and_ you would change it to make that
happen, then it may be useful to other programs and an external
program would allow that.

This is not the job of a 'maintenance programmer' but is a design
decision.

OTOH hand you were recommending that for the sake of one routine to
take a parameter the _whole_program_ should be changed to using ENTRY
and have no section or paragraph labels at all.

> Not gonna happen. He or she will take
> the easy way out by moving it to a temp field in working-storage.

Yes, probably.  But then using ENTRY isn't going to ever happen
either, except where it is used for its intended purpore of external
re-entrancy.
 
> Re-entrancy is for multi-threading. Windows callbacks are not an example of
> multi-threading.

I don't know where you get your ideas from. MF introduced the systems
programming extensions, with LOCAL-STORAGE and recursion, around 1990.
This was used for Windows programs as callbacks require the run-unit
to be re-entrant.

Multi-threading came much later in the mid-90s.  Though it builds on
the same system programming features, it would be wrong, as you have
done, to reverse the timeline.
 
With the large graphics Windows programs that I wrote a 'yield' would
potentially result in the program being re-entered (depending on what
was in the message queue) and changing the working-storage and file
areas (but not the local-storage).  I used this regularly to keep the
screen updated, it didn't require me to abandon PERFORM of paragraphs
or any other mechanism.  It did require me to carefully check shared
data.

> To make a program thread-safe, 

I doubt there is much call to make existing programs thread-safe.

> multiple call mechanisms would require
> restructuring EVERY paragraph into something else.

No. You are wrong.  A multi-threaded program may need to be carefully
designed to cater for this, but PERFORM of paragraphs is not on the
'don't do' list.

> I agree there are few cases for recursion, but when you need it, it's nice to
> have. Tree processing is one such. 

You never need recursion, iteration can do exactly the same job. 
Recursion does store away your local data in the stack for you, with
iteration an array (or list if you wish) can do exactly the same, with
just a few lines of code.
 
> You beat me over the head with namespace pollution in re ENTRY, 
> and said Nested
> Programs were "carefully designed" to avoid the problem.

Yes. Using ENTRY for its intended task, an alternate external call
point is _not_ polution, it is an item _required_ to be added to the
run-unit namespace, just as program-ids are added to the namespace
because they need to be there so they can be called.

Nested programs are only locally visible and so do not add to the
run-unit namespace.

Polution is unrequired items in the namespace.  It is only your misuse
of ENTRY that does this.  Nested programs do not polute, by design. 
It was your claim that I said 'nested programs _reduce_ polution'. 
They do nothing to polution at all, they don't add or reduce it.

It is only your misuse of ENTRY that adds polution and eliminating
your misuse that reduces it.

But that was not the main objection to your 'better' use of ENTRY, the
main reason is that it just doesn't work in 'most compilers', even
where ENTRY exists.  Even where it may be made to work in a demo toy
program, it does not scale to applications and would cause huge name
management problems.

You claimed it was 'better' and I would regard everything that you put
in this category with deep suspicion.  Your ideas show a complete lack
of development, and have no understanding of what has been learnt from
other languages.

When you see 'fear' in your co-workers eyes, it isn't from your 'deep
intellectual insights'.

I think that you may be happier working in a different language, why
not try again to learn C.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T02:55:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40aab7c6.116430589@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com> <40a997e8.42726445@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405181224.6051451e@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>BASIC is not _a_ language, it is a collection of dozens of languages
>which may, or may not, be similar.  In some BASIC languages the line
>numbering is not used and GOSUB syntax requires a line number.

VB6 supports line numbers, GoTo, On number GoTo n,n,n .. and GOSUB. 
VB.NET dropped them as obsolete language elements.

Basic never had GOSUB n THRU n

>> When a maintenance programmer sees the need for a parameter when calling a
>> paragraph that formerly had none, 
>
>Since when did a 'maintenance programmer' ever see the need for a
>parameter ?

A paragraph processes foo. They 're-engineer' the database by adding new-foo.
Now the old part of the program wants to call the paragraph with foo and some
new code added by the maintenance programmer wants to call it with new-foo. 

Solution:
  move foo to temp-foo
  perform (paragraph)
--later--
  move new-foo to temp-foo
  perform (paragraph)

>What I did say is that if some code is so useful that it should be
>reused with a parameter, _and_ you would change it to make that
>happen, then it may be useful to other programs and an external
>program would allow that.

The paragraph is of no use to another program AND refers to 17 fields in this
program's working-storage. Would you have the maintenance programmer pass 17
parameters?

>This is not the job of a 'maintenance programmer' but is a design
>decision.

It happens all the time.

>OTOH hand you were recommending that for the sake of one routine to
>take a parameter the _whole_program_ should be changed to using ENTRY
>and have no section or paragraph labels at all.

Yes.

>> Re-entrancy is for multi-threading. Windows callbacks are not an example of
>> multi-threading.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>done, to reverse the timeline.

I gave no timeline. I said reentrant and multithreaded are not the same thing. 

A reentrant program is a single process, a single entry in the operating
system's task table. It executes serially. A multithreaded program is multiple
processes running in parallel and having multiple entries in the task table
(called in Unix LightWeight Process (LWP)). 

A multithreaded program must use synchronization to prevent two threads from
simultaneously accessing shared memory. That's not an issue in reentrant
programs.

A multithreaded program might have to wait for several parallel threads to
finish before it can continue. A reentrant program has only one thread. 
 
>With the large graphics Windows programs that I wrote a 'yield' would
>potentially result in the program being re-entered (depending on what
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>data.

Ok, that's multithreading, which was first supported by Microsoft in NT (and
OS/2). It was not supported by Win9x and certainly not by Win3. MF support for
reentrancy alone was not adequate. You needed a dispatcher and a way to lock
shared data. 

I'm guessing Microsoft forked a 'child process' for each message, and somehow
made your working-storage Shared Memory between the several processes. This is
called Multi-Tasking, which is NOT the same as multithreading. Most all
operating systems can do Multi-Tasking. The problems with Multi-Tasking are
inadequate (or no) synchronization services, poor clean-up of shared resources
(such as memory) after all processes terminate and poor management services
between parent and children. Multithreading was invented to solve these
problems. 

>> To make a program thread-safe, 
>
>I doubt there is much call to make existing programs thread-safe.

Every database Stored Procedure or External Procedure written in Cobol needs to
be thread-safe. If it isn't, you're making users wait in line for serial access,
one-at-a-time.

>> multiple call mechanisms would require
>> restructuring EVERY paragraph into something else.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>'don't do' list.

OK, if you do it the lazy way, threads are unaware of each other, each has its
own copy of working-storage, the only 'parent' task is systems software starting
Cobol programs as one-thread 'children'. That's how DB2 and CICS works. In that
case, traditional Cobol is adequate. 

If you want to do more advanced things, such as parallel processing to make your
application SCALABLE, you can't do it with PERFORMed paragraphs. 

>You claimed it was 'better' and I would regard everything that you put
>in this category with deep suspicion.  

If one dog has spots, all dogs have spots.

[ad homina deleted]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T00:16:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405182316.14690ca7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com> <40a997e8.42726445@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405181224.6051451e@posting.google.com> <40aab7c6.116430589@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> VB6 supports line numbers, GoTo, On number GoTo n,n,n .. and GOSUB. 
> VB.NET dropped them as obsolete language elements.

Exactly.  What use is VB to busines if the code has to be rewritten on
each release.  In any case VB isn't BASIC, it is just one of dozens,
or hundreds, of implementations.

Perhaps you would be happier using that language.
 
> Basic never had GOSUB n THRU n

Irrelevant.

> The paragraph is of no use to another program AND refers to 17 fields in this
> program's working-storage. Would you have the maintenance programmer pass 17
> parameters?

I wouldn't have the programmer do anything in particular, but then I'm
not a control freak trying to impose my 'better ideas'.

You had raised the issue of a date formatting routine where a passing
a parameter or two would be useful and the routine could be
encapsulated.

Now you have invented a completely different situation which may or
may not be better with a parameter, or may be perfectly usable with a
shared data item.

If you want to CALL using some parameters _and_ to use shared data in
WS, then nested programs do that, especially if the code is of no use
elsewhere.  Perhaps you should look up 'GLOBAL' as it applied to
nested programs.

Nested programs and classes not only allow access to data in parent
programs, where allowed only, but they also allow local data to be
private.

> >OTOH hand you were recommending that for the sake of one routine to
> >take a parameter the _whole_program_ should be changed to using ENTRY
> >and have no section or paragraph labels at all.
> 
> Yes.

Which is just silly.  There will be only one LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION and
one LINKAGE SECTION, data will have to be distributed amongst these
very carefully and care taken as to which items are valid at any
particular time.

If you had done anything more than a couple of toy programs and had
identified and solved all the inevitable problems then you may have
retained some credibility, but you thought that the _only_ danger was
forgetting a GOBACK.

> >> Re-entrancy is for multi-threading. Windows callbacks are not an example of
> >> multi-threading.
 
> I gave no timeline. I said reentrant and multithreaded are not the same thing. 

I know what you said. I just said you were wrong.  Re-entrancy is for
re-entrancy.  Windows programs are re-entrant regardless of whether
they are multithreaded or not.

[much irrelevant yammering about multi-threading deleted]
 
> A multithreaded program must use synchronization to prevent two threads from
> simultaneously accessing shared memory. That's not an issue in reentrant
> programs.

Yes it is.  It is an issue.  It may be an issue which you are unaware
of.

> Ok, that's multithreading, which was first supported by Microsoft in NT (and
> OS/2). It was not supported by Win9x and certainly not by Win3. MF support for
> reentrancy alone was not adequate. You needed a dispatcher and a way to lock
> shared data.

No. Completely wrong.  What I described was normal behaviour in
Windows 3.0 and everything after, and even in 2.0.  It is callback, it
is not multithreading.  MF support for re-entrancy was entirely
adequate. I know, I used it.

The mainline runs in a loop despatching messages, any 'yield'
(actually a messagepeek) is, in effect, a call to the windows message
despatcher which may throw out another message to the callback (an
ENTRY perhaps, or the same program-id) which activates the program.
Later when that has completed, the code that yielded resumes after the
'yield' back in the middle of the program.  Between the CALL and
continuing from it the WORKING-STORAGE may have been changed by a
re-entry.

This is re-entrancy. It is not multi-threading, it is co-operative
multi-tasking. Your claim about what re-entrancy is for is wrong.

I know what I am talking about, I have written huge Cobol Windows
3.0/3.11/95 etc programs down to the API starting in 1990 with early
release stuff when 3.0 was just out.
 
> I'm guessing ...

Don't just guess, go and learn something.

> >I doubt there is much call to make existing programs thread-safe.
 
> Every database Stored Procedure or External Procedure written in Cobol needs
> to be thread-safe. 

I doubt there is much call to load existing programs as database
procedures.  These are specifically written for the task.

> If it isn't, you're making users wait in line for serial access,
> one-at-a-time.

You should learn how machines with a single CPU do multi-tasking, it
is called timeslicing.  If a procedure is code only then the CPU is
only doing one-at-a-time.

> If you want to do more advanced things, such as parallel processing to make
> your application SCALABLE, you can't do it with PERFORMed paragraphs. 

What nonsense you do talk.  A program can PERFORM paragraphs
regardless of whether it is re-entrant, multi-threaded or
multi-tasking.  This mechanism is just a way of putting blocks of code
'out of line'.  It makes no difference whether the code is in-line or
out-of-line.

You are completely confused by your contrived argument that 'PERFORM
is a CALL without parameters'.  You then go off on a complete rant
based on this misconception as if something else will do that CALL in
a multi-thread application.  A PERFORM _isn't_ a CALL.

You are attempting to create an argument that is completely
irrelevant.

> If one dog has spots, all dogs have spots.

If a dog bites, then you don't put your hand out to it a second time.

Your analogy is inappropriate, I may trust another dog's better ideas.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T16:14:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8g14i0nsv@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com> <40a997e8.42726445@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405181224.6051451e@posting.google.com> <40aab7c6.116430589@news.optonline.net>`

```

In article <40aab7c6.116430589@news.optonline.net>, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) writes:
> 
> Ok, that's multithreading, which was first supported by Microsoft in NT (and
> OS/2). It was not supported by Win9x and certainly not by Win3.

It was most definitely supported in Windows 95.  See the docs for the
CreateThread API, for example: "Included in Windows 95 and later".

Preemptive multithreading was not supported in Windows 3, even under
Win32s, because Windows 3 had a non-preemptive scheduler.  However, it
was certainly possible to implement cooperative threading in Windows 3,
and that required reentrant modules.

> MF support for
> reentrancy alone was not adequate. You needed a dispatcher and a way to lock
> shared data. 

Only for preemptive threading.

> I'm guessing Microsoft forked a 'child process' for each message, and somehow
> made your working-storage Shared Memory between the several processes.

There was no need for multiple processes to have multiple messages being
serviced concurrently in a Windows 3 program.  Windows 3 "processes" ran
in a single address space, and were essentially all dynamically-loaded
extensions of the Windows kernel.  The kernel could call into a process
any time it (the kernel) had control.  Many programs were written such
that they never had to deal with concurrent message processing and
reentrancy, but it was possible and some did.

And all memory for all processes in Windows 3 was "shared", since they
all ran in a single, unprotected address space.  In practice, to make
use of this a program had to globally lock (or "wire", which was a lock
that required the memory be in the "low" region) a memory area, to
prevent Windows from moving it around while the process wasn't using
it.

> This is called Multi-Tasking, which is NOT the same as multithreading.

It was neither.  All Windows 3 "processes" ran as coroutines, and it
was a reentrant call into a coroutine.

Windows 3 did in fact multitask, albeit only cooperatively (no
preemption), but that's beside the point.  You could have Windows plus
a single application and have reentrant calls made into the message
loop.

> The problems with Multi-Tasking are
> inadequate (or no) synchronization services, poor clean-up of shared resources
> (such as memory) after all processes terminate and poor management services
> between parent and children. Multithreading was invented to solve these
> problems. 

Hardly.  Multitasking is orthogonal to synchronization, shared
resource availability or cleanup, and relationships between parent
and child processes (which is what I assuem you mean by the third
item above). Those all can be implemented poorly or well among
multiple processes in a multitasking OS, and multithreading does not
"solve" any of them.

Multithreading was invented for two reasons: to permit the
implementation of certain algorithms and coding techniques; and to
provide multiple flows of control with less overhead than required
for process startup and context switches.  (Whether it actually
offers any practical benefit, particularly in preemptive
implementations, is a hotly contested question.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote:

>
>In article <40aab7c6.116430589@news.optonline.net>,
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) writes:
>> 
>> Ok, that's multithreading, which was first supported by Microsoft in NT (and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>CreateThread API, for example: "Included in Windows 95 and later".

Whoops. Thanks for the correction.

[Win3 stuff clipped]

>> The problems with Multi-Tasking are
>> inadequate (or no) synchronization services, poor clean-up of shared
resources
>> (such as memory) after all processes terminate and poor management services
>> between parent and children. Multithreading was invented to solve these
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>"solve" any of them.

To see how bad multitasking can be, enter 'ipcs -mp' on must any Unix system
that's been running multitasking applications such as SAP. It is common to see
hundreds of megabytes of shared memory owned by processes that abended or were
killed weeks ago. 

When a process ends, multithreading makes it easier for the OS to dispose of
everything owned by it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T21:11:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8j6th02f87@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com> <40a997e8.42726445@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405181224.6051451e@posting.google.com> <40aab7c6.116430589@news.optonline.net> <c8g14i0nsv@news4.newsguy.com> <40ac36f9.214544737@news.optonline.net>`

```

In article <40ac36f9.214544737@news.optonline.net>, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) writes:
> mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote:
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> killed weeks ago. 

That's not the fault of multitasking; it's entirely due to the brain-
damaged semantics of the SysV "Columbus" IPC mechanisms (and they're
slow to boot).  Most modern Unix implementations also support the POSIX
IPC mechanisms, which don't have this problem.  This nicely demonstrates
my point: shared resources can be implemented poorly or well.

(Actually, Columbus semaphores are even worse than shared memory;
most implementations don't track as much housekeeping information for
them, so it's even harder to tell which ones might still be needed.
There's no concept of "opening" or "attaching to" a Columbus semaphore,
for example, so you can't tell whether any processes are currently
using it - unlike a shared memory segment.)

> When a process ends, multithreading makes it easier for the OS to dispose of
> everything owned by it.

All that's needed is a half-decent design for IPC resource ownership
and lifetime.  Windows had one prior to multithreading support, for
example.

Actually, so did Unix; it just wasn't Columbus shared memory,
semaphores, and queues.  (Pipes and FIFOs have well-designed
semantics.  Unfortunately, they're inherently slower than shared
memory, because they require a mode context switch.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T21:17:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8dugk0jmp@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405101848.5132384c@posting.google.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <40a16cfb.67032548@news.optonline.net> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net>`

```

In article <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net>, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) writes:
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> >robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
> >> AS/400 had its genesis in a 1970 revamp of S/360 to something called NS
> >> (New System). 

It was called FS, for Future Systems.  FS was cancelled circa 1976.
Legendary IBMer Lynn Wheeler was partially responsible; his research
into the (abysmal) performance of FS was ammunition for those arguing
against it.  His archives (at http://www.garlic.com/) have various
details.

FS was intended as the OS for either the 370 or a successor
tentatively named the S/380.  It was partially intended as a clone-
killer, and partially a grandiose attempt to incorporate every
advanced OS feature anyone at IBM could dream up in the hope of
creating an OS so "advanced" it would drown all of its competitors.

> >No.  AS/400 has nothing to do with 360.  It comes from the S36, S38
> >line.
>
> You're wrong. It came from a failed Big Iron concept that found a home in
> S/36-38 land. 

You're both right (and wrong).  The AS/400 ("Silverlake") project was
the migration strategy for S/36 and S/38, and OS/400 included an
S/38-compatibility mode for many years (it may still, for all I
know).  OS/400 incorporated many ideas from FS, for a variety of
reasons; one is that some of the (many) people who worked on FS went
on to work on the S/36 and S/38 (which were successors to the S/3;
the S/34 was a stripped-down S/38).  Another was that hardware had
improved to the point where some of the FS concepts, such as very
rigorous supervision of application behavior, were more feasible
(though the original AS/400s were still painfully slow, particularly
the little development boxes).

So the AS/400 belongs to the S/38 family, but OS/400 is most closely
related to FS, which was intended as an OS for the S/370 family
(though never completed, much less released).

> >Actually AS/400s are Power machines too now aren't they ?

Yes, for several years now.

> Probably. Under the covers, nearly all modern machines are RISC emulating
> CISC.

While this is arguable in a certain limited sense (most contemporary
CPUs are 8-bit microcontrollers, after all, and it's debatable
whether "emulating" is the proper term for a RISC core processing a
microdecoded CISC instruction stream), this has little to do with
what kind of processor AS/400s use.  The 400s are built on POWER
CPUs now because 1) IBM owns POWER, and 2) POWER has better
performance characteristics (particularly in terms of things like
power consumption, which are more important in data centers than
they are with home users) than the only other currently-available,
viable, comparable architecture, IA64.  (The 400 uses the 64-bit
POWER designs.)

> Natively RISC machines are VERY fast, especially Sun.

RISC architectures aren't inherently fast.  The best SPEC numbers
right now belong to x86-family CPUs, because Intel and AMD have
cranked their clocks up to ridiculous speeds.  Those are hugely
inefficient chips, but economies of scale have made them more
affordable than any other architecture for most purposes.

There are applications where a super-hot, power-devouring CPU is not
the best choice, and that's where something like POWER finds its
niche.  (Or where people want a decent 64-bit implementation. Or
where they want binary compatibility with another architecture
because they've invested in it.)

It's true that the modern RISC designs, from POWER on, are much
better (in terms of instructions per cycle and so forth) than the old
CISC designs.  But since the high-performance CISC CPUs are now RISC
cores with a layer of CISC-instruction decoding around them, it's a
moot point.

What it really comes down to is that there are a number of good ideas
for creating very fast CPUs; everyone is using the ones they can
afford; and Intel (and AMD) is throwing a clunky compatibility
wrapper around theirs, because they have to in order to keep their
customer base, but the core is so fast that they still get the
performance they need.

And "especially Sun"?  Sun's best SPARC performance numbers aren't
close to IBM's best POWER ones.  They're not close to Sun's best x86
numbers, in fact.  Take a look at http://www.spec.org/.  SPARC was a
nice design in its day, but Sun doesn't have the resources to keep up
with chip design and fabrication innovation.  They gave it a good try
and kept SPARC viable for an impressively long time, but they're not
Intel or IBM.  Inventing a usable silicon-on-insulator process and
retooling your fab to use it, for example, is a huge investment.  Sun
never had that kind of money.

> Both Sun and HP have
> announced they're abandoning their successful platforms in favor of Intel
> lookalikes. Visionaries know something we don't.

They know that it's cheaper for them to use Intel chips (and clones)
because of the aforementioned economies of scale.  Sun does, anyway.
What HP is doing is anyone's guess, particularly since the Compaq
merger.  And long before its decline HP got in bed with Intel for
IA64 - they decided VLIW was the way to go, and by partnering with
Intel they could keep the best of PA-RISC in a processor family that
would enjoy much larger volumes.  As it turned out, of course, IA64
is a dog and almost no one wants it (though probably Intel will
eventually throw enough resources at it for it to succeed).

ObCOBOL: GOBACK.
```

AS400 then followed.  IIRC the 36 was incompatible with the 34 and both 
were incompatible with the 38 although the 38 had capability to run 36 
work probably in some kind of hardware or software emulation.  The AS400 
was a true follow on to the System 38.

- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <40a16cfb.67032548@news.optonline.net> <c7rvum$970$1@panix5.panix.com> <40a1f7c7.102569347@news.optonline.net> <7Dsoc.17382$V97.9581@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40a2d9f2.160477830@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <c8dugk0jmp@news3.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0405181848.3b47faf0@posting.google.com> <c8fuu40kk4@news3.newsguy.com>`

Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message news:c8fuu40kk4@news3.newsguy.com...
>
> In article <217e491a.0405181848.3b47faf0@posting.google.com>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) writes:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> of our expected life spans?

First, more than half of the Longhorn developers had to be pulled from the project
to work on security issues.  Then more had to be pulled to work on XP SP2,
which forced a complete rethink and revamp of the development schedule.

Longhorn has now been stripped of almost everything of value, with a probable
delivery date of 2006, although 2007 is not impossible, given the climate
in which we live, with new attacks popping up incessantly.

A version of Windows with the originally planned content of Longhorn
will not be delivered before 2010.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-12T01:53:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405120053.11e8abf8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <4090222C.FDA56581@mb.sympatico.ca> <97mSTktPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6r8nu$80a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4091C078.4131B909@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6to00$paa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4098011B.BB962BF7@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0405041904.11ae2c47@posting.google.com> <409ffd4b.311475220@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405101848.5132384c@posting.google.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> Since you're not using PERFORM, there is no need for paragraph labels at all.

CALL does not replace PERFORM, PERFORM is the mechanism for
repitition, where is the CALL .. VARYING .. UNTIL ?   What would you
use ?  GO TO ?

> True. But if one avoids THRU and GO TO as you and I do, replacing *all*
> paragraph names with ENTRY is simple, mechanical, one-for-one. So is adding
> GOBACK at the bottom of every (former) paragraph.

It is also pointless, it introduces problems that you haven't thought
of (such as name space polution) and it fails to cater for repitition.

> Micro Focus *requires* ENTRY to do recursion. It doesn't allow Nested Programs
> to call themselves.

Your original claim was: "Yet another advantage is that ENTRY supports
recursion."

Microfocus Level II Cobol has ENTRY but no recursion. ENTRY does not
require recursion.  Your claim was wrong, your support for it is an
inversion of your claim.

In fact MicroFocus does _NOT_ require ENTRY to do recursion:

 """A program can call itself via either its program-id or an entry
point."""
 
It is true that nested programs cannot recursively call themselves,
but external called programs can call themselves.

> LINKAGE items are not a problem if each 'program' restricts itself to variables
> listed in its ENTRY statement, and does not call subordinate 'programs' which
> re-use them i.e. potentially change addressability.

It was your suggestion that all paragraphs be replaced by ENTRY, now
all the data items need to be reworked into LINKAGE, _and_ the logic
must be restricted to a single level.

> My proposal merely changes the grammar of
> PERFORM to be consistent with that of CALL by allowing one to pass parameters 
> and eliminating the troublesome THRU.

That is not what you proposed at all.

> My primary reference is the Language Standard, not compiler vendor manuals.
> When I cite the Standard, colleagues act defuddled, making it clear they've
> never read the Standard. 'Yeahbut, how does it work in XYZ Cobol.'

I recall that previously you were unconcerned about what the standard
said, it was quite clear that you had hardly read it even, your
criteria was 'whatever extensions the MF and Fujitsu compiler did'
_regardless_ even that the vendor's own manual did not support them.

For example:

""" -------------
>But it ain't Cobol.  POINTER is an extension in Cobol'85 and the use
of
>them may be different, or not implemented at all, on current
compilers.

This is FUD (fear, uncertainty and doubt). In practice, it is
implemented
exactly the same on all mainstream compilers: Micro Focus, IBM and
Fujitsu. It
is real-world COBOL, even though you don't like it.
  ---------------- 
That's what Fujitsu documentation says. I believed it for awhile,
until I tried
the industry-standard way and found it worked. I documented this in
the paper:
"Fujitsu: A good compiler, whose weaknesses are ... incorrect
documentation of
pointer operations, ...". Look at the Demo. It was compiled under
Fujitsu v3 and
it works. It doesn't have any BASED-STORAGE nor FUNCTION ADDR().
------------------- """

Your 'proposal' decries your assertion that you are a 'born again'
standards follower.  ENTRY will not be found in the standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-13T01:18:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a2bfa4.153742958@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <4090222C.FDA56581@mb.sympatico.ca> <97mSTktPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6r8nu$80a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4091C078.4131B909@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6to00$paa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4098011B.BB962BF7@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0405041904.11ae2c47@posting.google.com> <409ffd4b.311475220@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405101848.5132384c@posting.google.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405120053.11e8abf8@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>use ?  GO TO ?

The demo programs I posted use inline PERFORM for loop control and CALL in place
of PERFORM paragraph-name.

>It is also pointless, it introduces problems that you haven't thought
>of (such as name space polution) 

It's no worse than the possibility of using the same data name twice. Nobody
complains about that.

>> Micro Focus *requires* ENTRY to do recursion. It doesn't allow Nested
Programs
>> to call themselves.
>
>Your original claim was: "Yet another advantage is that ENTRY supports
>recursion."

>In fact MicroFocus does _NOT_ require ENTRY to do recursion:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>but external called programs can call themselves.

Correct. Requiring separate compilation for the recursive portion of a program
is an ugly solution, especially in a demo. The *practical* way to demonstrate
recursion is through ENTRY.

>> LINKAGE items are not a problem if each 'program' restricts itself to
variables
>> listed in its ENTRY statement, and does not call subordinate 'programs' which
>> re-use them i.e. potentially change addressability.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>must be restricted to a single level.

The trees demo I posted has four simple variables in LINKAGE and multi-level
CALLs.

If you want to construct straw men, you should insure they're not refuted by
recently posted code.

>> My proposal merely changes the grammar of
>> PERFORM to be consistent with that of CALL by allowing one to pass parameters

>> and eliminating the troublesome THRU.
>
>That is not what you proposed at all.

That's what my code does.

>I recall that previously you were unconcerned about what the standard
>said, it was quite clear that you had hardly read it even, your
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>is real-world COBOL, even though you don't like it.

My first preference is the J4 Standard. When it lets us down, as it did with
pointers, my second preference is de-facto standards. In the case of pointers,
the three compilers used by more than 90% of Cobol programmers support the EXACT
SAME syntax. That's a good example of a de-facto standard. 

>Your 'proposal' decries your assertion that you are a 'born again'
>standards follower.  ENTRY will not be found in the standard.

ENTRY is another de-facto standard, having  the EXACT SAME syntax on IBM, Micro
Focus and Fujitsu. It didn't go away when Nested Programs were introduced in
'85, so I doubt it will be dropped.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-13T01:31:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405130031.7cd14616@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6r8nu$80a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4091C078.4131B909@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6to00$paa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4098011B.BB962BF7@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0405041904.11ae2c47@posting.google.com> <409ffd4b.311475220@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405101848.5132384c@posting.google.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405120053.11e8abf8@posting.google.com> <40a2bfa4.153742958@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >It is also pointless, it introduces problems that you haven't thought
> >of (such as name space polution) 
> 
> It's no worse than the possibility of using the same data name twice. Nobody
> complains about that.

I thought that you didn't understand this.  All ENTRY names are
visible to the _whole_run_unit_.

Using a data name twice merely requires qualification and a compiler
error warns when this is needed.  Using a paragraph name twice in one
program may give a compile error and, if they are in different
sections, qualification can cater for this. All this is fixed before
the program is run.

Systems are usually built from several, or several dozen, programs
being called when required.  If ENTRYs are used then everytime a
program is loaded all its ENTRY names are added to the name space of
the run unit.  If two programs have the same name on an ENTRY, even
though each program individually has unique names, there is no
guarantee which will be used when a CALL is made.

Programs may work correctly when run separately in testing, or in
certain combinations, but at some point the two clashing programs may
be loaded together into the same run unit and the programs just don't
work correctly any more.  If one is lucky the run time will crash with
an error message indicating the fault. If not the program will just
give strange results for no apparent reason.




> Correct. Requiring separate compilation for the recursive portion of a program
> is an ugly solution, especially in a demo. The *practical* way to demonstrate
> recursion is through ENTRY.

> If you want to construct straw men, you should insure they're not refuted by
> recently posted code.

It is irrelevant whether it can be done, it _is_ relevant that it is
an issue that has to be programmed around.  Your claim was 'the only
danger is', when there are many that you seem unaware of.
 
> My first preference is the J4 Standard. When it lets us down, 

When it lets _US_ down ?  Who is the us ?

> as it did with
> pointers, my second preference is de-facto standards. In the case of pointers,
> the three compilers used by more than 90% of Cobol programmers 

Where do you get the 90% from ? you just made it up.

> support the EXACT SAME syntax. That's a good example of a de-facto standard. 

No Fujitsu does not _support_ the same mechanism.  It happens that the
compiler in some of its versions do accept the source and generate
code, but this is undocumented.  If it has a problem you will not get
support on this, if it disappears it a version then there is no
recourse.

> >Your 'proposal' decries your assertion that you are a 'born again'
> >standards follower.  ENTRY will not be found in the standard.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> '85, so I doubt it will be dropped.

It may have the same syntax, but that does not mean that it has the
same semantics.  ENTRY is specified as an additional entry point from
outside the program (which nested programs are not), for example as
Windows call-backs.  This does not guarantee its suitability for what
you intend.  There may be limits on run unit name space size, there
may be restrictions on name length, there certainly will be problems
with name space polution.

Just because it works in a toy demo program does not mean that it will
scale.  Cobol is used _because_ it scales well, you are just
re-inventing one of the problems with C (name space polution) that
reared its head 30 years ago, but at least they had a practical
solution: static procedures, and later they introduced C++ that dealt
with the issue entirely.

And you don't even know that it is an issue.

But please do a 'proof of concept', build a complete system using your
proposed mechanism and report back the point that it all turns to
custard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T00:01:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a40b32.238633634@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6r8nu$80a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4091C078.4131B909@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6to00$paa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4098011B.BB962BF7@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0405041904.11ae2c47@posting.google.com> <409ffd4b.311475220@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405101848.5132384c@posting.google.com> <40a14cb9.58773361@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405120053.11e8abf8@posting.google.com> <40a2bfa4.153742958@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405130031.7cd14616@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>

>> It's no worse than the possibility of using the same data name twice. Nobody
>> complains about that.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>give strange results for no apparent reason.

I wrote three programs and compiled them to .so to insure they would be loaded
by the operating system (dlopen calling ld) rather than the RM Cobol runtime.

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
 identification division.
 program-id. dupmain.

 procedure division.
     call 'dup-a'
     call 'dup-b'
     call 'duplicate'
     goback.

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
 identification division.
 program-id. dup-a.

 procedure division.
     display 'dup-a'
     goback.
 entry 'duplicate'.
     display 'a.duplicate'
     goback.

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
 identification division.
 program-id. dup-b.

 procedure division.
     display 'dup-b'
     goback.
 entry 'duplicate'.
     display 'b.duplicate'
     goback.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

When the dupmain called dup-b, ld issued an error message about duplicate names
and abended the run. There were no strange results.

The notification delay was caused by Micro Focus' penchant for lazy loading.
Normal behavior on Unix is to bind all symbols before execution begins. 

>> My first preference is the J4 Standard. When it lets us down, 
>
>When it lets _US_ down ?  Who is the us ?

This fraternity of Cobol programmers, united in pursuit of peace, harmony and
progress.

>It may have the same syntax, but that does not mean that it has the
>same semantics.  ENTRY is specified as an additional entry point from
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>with name space polution.

On Unix and NT, the only limit on symbol table size is max virtual memory per
process, which is typically set >1G or unlimited. It's not an issue.

>Just because it works in a toy demo program does not mean that it will
>scale.  Cobol is used _because_ it scales well, 

The most important scalability feature is a language's ability to exploit
multiple processors through multithreading. On that score, (non-OO) Cobol scores
near zero. Normal Cobol programs run as a single thread.

>you are just
>re-inventing one of the problems with C (name space polution) that
>reared its head 30 years ago, but at least they had a practical
>solution: static procedures, and later they introduced C++ that dealt
>with the issue entirely. 

Huh? All C procedures are static. Languages having dynamic procedures are
interpreted, OO or JIT. Did you mean statically linked? Only mainframers still
do that.

>And you don't even know that it is an issue.

Running out of memory is not an issue. The premise of your arguments became
obsolete >10 years ago.

>But please do a 'proof of concept', build a complete system using your
>proposed mechanism and report back the point that it all turns to
>custard.

I've been doing it at the program level for >20 years. Now that machines are
less confining, I propose applying the same principle at the paragraph level.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-11T03:48:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T1Ync.15984$Hs1.15278@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <c6lo8r$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4090222C.FDA56581@mb.sympatico.ca> <97mSTktPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6r8nu$80a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4091C078.4131B909@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6to00$paa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4098011B.BB962BF7@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0405041904.11ae2c47@posting.google.com> <409ffd4b.311475220@news.optonline.net>`

```
Just so it is clear to anyone reading this.  There is an ANSI/ISO ('85 and
below) Standard "ENTER" statement, but I think that Robert really meant "ENTRY"
statement when he said,

> I used to program the same way, but I recently discovered a better style --
> replacing paragraph names with ENTER statements and using CALL to invoke the
> procedures.


The ENTRY "header" is a relatively but certainly NOT universal extension.  The
biggest problem that I know about it is that when different ENTRY statements
have different parameters listed, some compilers keep addressability to
previously used Linkage Section items and others do not (and at least  one as a
compiler option to determine this).  Therefore, use of an ENTRY statement is not
(necessarily) very portable.   Use of nested programs can (in almost ALL cases)
provide the same functionality for any '85 Standard compiler or later.  Simply
use an EVALUATE as the first statement in your main program and call an
appropriate nested program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-11T14:14:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405111314.38e33531@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <c6lo8r$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4090222C.FDA56581@mb.sympatico.ca> <97mSTktPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6r8nu$80a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4091C078.4131B909@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6to00$paa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4098011B.BB962BF7@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0405041904.11ae2c47@posting.google.com> <409ffd4b.311475220@news.optonline.net> <T1Ync.15984$Hs1.15278@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote

> The ENTRY "header" is a relatively but certainly NOT universal extension.  The
> biggest problem that I know about it is that when different ENTRY statements
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> appropriate nested program.

Yet another issue with ENTRY statements is that of name space
polution.  The ENTRY names are external and thus global within the
whole run-unit.  The effect of having a name duplicated because it is
used in two programs in the run-unit seems to be undefined.

While using ENTRY for its intended purpose of alternate entries into
programs from external programs is 'safe' in that these names can be
managed as part of the program naming mechanisms, the wholesale
replacement of paragraphs by ENTRY would lead to significant name
management issues and/or system instabilty.

Nested programs keep their names local and thus these do not lead to
name space polution.
```
