```
I've often seen people land safely after they jump out of perfectly good 
airplanes with only a few square meters of fabric.

I've also often seen people land safely on the ground in a craft 
designed to safely transport people from place to place, via air, 
without harm, and piloted by an experienced handler.

Both are cases of air transportation.  And both, when done properly, 
provide a significent possibility of a good landing.

Our examples are similar -- except I used people instead of software. 

However, if you don't understand why 'hundreds of lines of code' in a 
single IF statement is a problem then you have no hope of understanding 
the goto problem.
```

```
> be accepted.  But in MY  experience, most code could stand a great deal of

Is this your concept of "properly written structured code"?

> B.     IF A NOT = B GOTO AFTER-B.
>         (same hundreds of lines of code)

Is this your concept of "properly written unstructured code"?


> Note that the "hundreds of lines of code" are identical in both cases.

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nobody has ever demonstrated points such as these to me.

Doing your same code comparisons.


	IF A=B
		PERFORM B-ROUTINE
	END-IF.

vs

	IF A NOT = B GO TO NOT-B.
	PERFORM B-ROUTINE
NOT-B.


This is more modular.   It is more clear than in your two examples the scope of
what B-ROUTINE does.   Now once I have removed the hundreds of lines of code we
have the above comparisons.   Not a lot of difference, but do you still prefer
your GO TO code?
```

```
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-05T12:13:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <c6lo8r$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4090222C.FDA56581@mb.sympatico.ca> <97mSTktPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6r8nu$80a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4091C078.4131B909@mb.sympatico.ca> <c6to00$paa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4098011B.BB962BF7@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote

> Why are programmers punishing themselves doing it as in B? 

That may be because they always write programs with what I call
'heavyweight' procedures.  When they code a part that is to be
PERFORMed they do it with a lot of red tape.  If they perform sections
then they must create a section header an initial paragraph label and
an end paragraph label, they alloacte a new prefix number to indicate
its place in the program hierarchy, almost guaranteeing that it won't
be reused from somewhere else, and they move the code to the physical
placement in the source given by its prefix.

This makes a lot of overhead for a simple separate block to perform,
so they take the 'easy' route and just bang in a GO TO and a label.

This make the performed procedures larger, less modular, and the
programmer only thinks of performs of large blocks of code.

Once the GO TOs are eliminated there is no need nor use for section,
or thru or exit labels.  With modern tools (anything in the last
quarter century) text serches will find any name so prefixes are not
required as they were with lineflow listings and card packs.  By
dumping the hierarchical numbering then performed paragraphs, without
all the red tape, can be adjacent to where they are performed.  The
style of procedures is now 'lightweight', it is easier to split a
paragraph in two in situ and perform then it is to bang in a go to.

In your simplified examples you cannot see the merit of removing the
GO TO, that may be because you would still use 'heavyweight'
procedures with number prefixes and exit pargarphs and all the other
red tape.  In fact all the things that were essential when source code
was in card trays.

But the real advantage of removing the GO TO, is that all the
'heavyweight' red tape can be removed, the sections, the thrus, the
exit pargraphs.  Then the artificial structure of the prefixes can be
dumped and every pargaraph label will do one thing, and only ever one
thing: be the target of a perform of just that paragraph.

All the errors related to missing THRU, missing SECTION, wrong exit
labels, inserted paragraphs, unexpected drop-thru, all disappear. 
And, more importantly, these things do not even need to be checked
for, they can't occur.  There is no fear to breaking a procedure down
into simpler modules for performing and leaving them in place, no need
to sort the sorce code by 'layer', it can be arranged by logic.  No
need to have 'hundreds of lines' they can be in many small paragraphs
grouped logically, and reusably.

The advantages of removing the GO TO is not about the GO TO at all.
```

```
      Am  11.05.04
 schrieb  lacey@mb.sympatico.ca (Peter Lacey)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca
   ueber  Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

PL> Richard wrote:

>> That may be because they always write programs with what I call
>> 'heavyweight' procedures.  When they code a part that is to be
>> PERFORMed they do it with a lot of red tape.  If they perform sections
>> then they must create a section header an initial paragraph label

   That is necessary because the syntax says so

>> and an end paragraph label,

   which is superflouus -- there is neither a practical need for it  
nor a syntactical rule calling for it

>> they alloacte a new prefix number to indicate its place in the
>> program hierarchy, almost guaranteeing that it won't be reused from
>> somewhere else, and they move the code to the physical placement in
>> the source given by its prefix.

  I have never used such prefix numbers, and I recommend not doing so.

  The name of the SECTION should be clear enough to tell the reader  
what purpose this section does have.

  COBOL ain't assembler.


Yours,

in schwarzer Haut gebrandmarkt wird."     - Karl Marx     12.11.1866
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-12T13:12:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

> >> That may be because they always write programs with what I call
> >> 'heavyweight' procedures.  When they code a part that is to be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    That is necessary because the syntax says so

Yes, but if they perform paragraph labels then they don't need to
create a section header.  Thus the section header is not 'necessary'.
 
> >> and an end paragraph label,
> 
>    which is superflouus -- there is neither a practical need for it  
> nor a syntactical rule calling for it

My original message was in response to a question:

>>> Why are programmers punishing themselves doing it as in B? 

The 'they' in my response are those that are 'punishing themselves'
writing in what I call 'heavyweight style'.  I completely agree that
there is 'no practical need', except of course if they were to want to
use a GO TO, which is the whole point of using 'heavyweight style' in
the first place.

If there is no need for the exit paragraph, then there is also no need
for the section header as the only point of having a section is to
encompass all the paragraphs within it (as long as _all_ PERFORMs are
of paragraphs). This is then a move from a heavyweight:

            43210-Process-Me SECTION.
            43211-Process-Me-Start.
                code.
            43219-Process-Me-Exit.
                EXIT.

to a lightweight:

            Process-Me.
                code
                .

> >> they alloacte a new prefix number to indicate its place in the
> >> program hierarchy, almost guaranteeing that it won't be reused from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   I have never used such prefix numbers, and I recommend not doing so.

Prefix numbers were extremely useful 30 years ago when my source code
was in a card box or two and I worked from lineflow listings.  Some
haven't moved on since then   ;-)
 
>   The name of the SECTION should be clear enough to tell the reader  
> what purpose this section does have.

I entirely agree with you, except the bit where you said 'SECTION'.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com>`

```

On 12-May-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> >   I have never used such prefix numbers, and I recommend not doing so.
>
> Prefix numbers were extremely useful 30 years ago when my source code
> was in a card box or two and I worked from lineflow listings.  Some
> haven't moved on since then   ;-)

And they survive because they don't cause harm.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-12T20:15:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> > Prefix numbers were extremely useful 30 years ago when my source code
> > was in a card box or two and I worked from lineflow listings.  Some
> > haven't moved on since then   ;-)
> 
> And they survive because they don't cause harm.

If someone were still rewriting the same programs that were being
written a quarter century ago (eg batch update, batch report), using
the same basic tools and reusing the same HIPO charts then I can see
how they may still find comfort in nicely numbered labels.    ;-)

In my view they do cause harm, or would to me if I used them.  The
implication of having numbered labels is there there is some strict
hierarchy in the code where 05... levels can only be performed from
04... levels, and also that the source code order is determined by
this numbering.  All the 05... must be after all the 04... labels.

Typically, my code cannot be organised that way.  Interactive systems
have their code executed in the order determined by the user input,
and does not follow some deterministic master plan (as batch programs
tend to).  The numbering is also a (possibly small) overhead on
creating new procedures.  If 'heavyweight' procedures are used then it
is another reason not to just create a new paragraph and perform it,
even if this could be performed from several places.  It may be easier
to just add the code into and existing section and copy the code lines
to other sections that would use it.  This is especially so if that
small new procedure just _had_ to be moved away where it would be
lost.

Using a 'lightweight' procedure style (ie dropping all the cruft,
especially numbering and shuffling code) means that the program is
many small, localised, reusable, paragraphs.

Label numbering was the first thing that I dropped a quarter century
ago when I got a decent screen based text editor that could just
search for what I wanted.

You may be lost without them, I find them extremely harmful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-13T14:24:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com>`

```

On 12-May-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> > > Prefix numbers were extremely useful 30 years ago when my source code
> > > was in a card box or two and I worked from lineflow listings.  Some
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> this numbering.  All the 05... must be after all the 04... labels.

They're just paragraph names.   Typically PERFORM 9000-WRITE-DETAIL would
indicate that you should look for that paragraph near the end of the program.  
But what if it's not there?   You find it exactly the same way that you find
WRITE-DETAIL.

Using TSO to edit, a standard that all paragraphs start with 4 numerics can be
nice.  I can have excluded code and then enter the command:
F p'#' 8 prev
to find the previous paragraph name.   No big deal.   Other editing tools can
handle this better.

I have had programs where the numbering wasn't in order (sometimes in a copy
member).   I haven't seen that it caused any problem.   It didn't create any
bugs.   Everything worked fine.   Programmers weren't confused.   People just
cut and pasted paragraphs from one program to another.   They're just labels.

If you like thinking of it in a structural way - well that's what standards
people often want anyway.   If they want I/O and error paragraphs at the end of
the program, they will be at the end of the program regardless of the naming
convention of paragraph names.

Typing a few extra characters is no onerous chore.

I agree that
PERFORM 1000-MAIN-ROUTINE THRU 1000-EXIT
is dangerous.   Besides my dislike of EXIT paragraphs, I believe that exit
paragraph needs to have a more meaningful name.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-13T13:05:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405131205.305b17d8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com> <c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> They're just paragraph names.   Typically PERFORM 9000-WRITE-DETAIL would
> indicate that you should look for that paragraph near the end of the program.  
> But what if it's not there?   You find it exactly the same way that you find
> WRITE-DETAIL.

If the numbers are non-functional, then they are just a waste of time
and space.  If they are all '9000-something' then they don't add
anything to the name.  If they are '9351-Write-Detail' and
'9417-something-else' then they waste time finding out what the actual
number is.

> Using TSO to edit, a standard that all paragraphs start with 4 numerics can be
> nice.  I can have excluded code and then enter the command:
> F p'#' 8 prev
> to find the previous paragraph name.   No big deal.   Other editing tools can
> handle this better.

Because I usually wind up with relatively small paragraphs then 'Page
Up' finds the previous paragraph for me, or maybe two.

Do you have to use a command string because it is not a screen editor,
or because your paragraphs are so large that it is fewer keystrokes
than a few 'page ups' ?

> I have had programs where the numbering wasn't in order (sometimes in a copy
> member).   I haven't seen that it caused any problem.   It didn't create any
> bugs.   Everything worked fine.   Programmers weren't confused.   People just
> cut and pasted paragraphs from one program to another.   They're just labels.

Then what is the point of the numbers ? are they just random digits
that are entered to satisfy the standards police ?

If I want to PERFORM Read-Next-Transaction then I can just write that,
I don't have to find out whether it is 4321-Read-Next.. or
9312-Read-Next..  Using meaningful names is useful because they are
meaningful.  Putting meaningless digits on the front of the name
simply stops its meaningfulness being useful.

> If you like thinking of it in a structural way - well that's what standards
> people often want anyway.   If they want I/O and error paragraphs at the end of
> the program, they will be at the end of the program regardless of the naming
> convention of paragraph names.

Exactly, if that is where they should be.  However, if I am in a
'level 3' paragraph and I want to do a PERFORM of some code and want
this in another paragraph (because I may perform it from another IF or
to restrict indent levels or because it will be >20 lines or whatever)
then the last thing that I want to do if to bury it a few hundred
lines away 'because it is a 4xxx-'.  I want it _here_, right next to
me.  I can do that.

If paragraphs are organised by level and by position then the
programmer may decline to make those lines remote, and may build
larger paragraphs, more indented, more convoluted IFs to avoid
duplicating action code.  Then they may think 'I can simplify this,
make it less indented, just by using a GO TO to an -EXIT'.  Then they
need the THRU.
 
Of course if the numbers don't indicate the level and position, then
please explain exactly what they do achieve ?  meaningless cruft to
get past the style police perhaps ?

> Typing a few extra characters is no onerous chore.

No, but finding out what they are _is_.

If they are by level and position and a procedure is split because it
becomes too complicated then are all the lower levels renumbered and
moved ?  The answer in many sites is probably No because procedures
are never split when they become too complicated, it would be too much
effort to renumber, so they 'simplify' with a few GO TOs.

While the charcters themselves may not be onerous, the effect on the
programming style may be large.  35 years ago I was writing with
numbers and making decisons about how code was laid out based on the
effort required to deal with the numbers, that was how it was done
then (and it was boxes of cards).  When I dumped the numbering my
productivity went up and I found I no longer needed -Exit paragraphs
to cater for procedures that grew overly complex.

> I agree that
> PERFORM 1000-MAIN-ROUTINE THRU 1000-EXIT
> is dangerous.   Besides my dislike of EXIT paragraphs, I believe that exit
> paragraph needs to have a more meaningful name.

I believe that exit paragraphs should be scrapped entirely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-13T20:37:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c80mac$ctv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com> <c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405131205.305b17d8@posting.google.com>`

```

On 13-May-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> > They're just paragraph names.   Typically PERFORM 9000-WRITE-DETAIL would
> > indicate that you should look for that paragraph near the end of the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> number is.

Wastes of time and space don't cost anything.   It's been a long time since I
programmed CoBOL via teletype.


> If paragraphs are organised by level and by position then the
> programmer may decline to make those lines remote, and may build
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> need the THRU.

I haven't seen that happen.   And I have seen this standard since 1969 in
various places.


> Of course if the numbers don't indicate the level and position, then
> please explain exactly what they do achieve ?  meaningless cruft to
> get past the style police perhaps ?

Not much.   My statement is that they don't cost anything.    Evolution says
that traits that don't cost stick around.


> > Typing a few extra characters is no onerous chore.
>
> No, but finding out what they are _is_.

How is it more difficult to find out how to spell the name of
9000-FIND-NEXT-IDENT, compared to FIND-NEXT-IDENT?    Either way, we've got to
check.


> If they are by level and position and a procedure is split because it
> becomes too complicated then are all the lower levels renumbered and
> moved ?  The answer in many sites is probably No because procedures
> are never split when they become too complicated, it would be too much
> effort to renumber, so they 'simplify' with a few GO TOs.

Not in my experience.   They are just names.   I thought I said that.


> > I agree that
> > PERFORM 1000-MAIN-ROUTINE THRU 1000-EXIT
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I believe that exit paragraphs should be scrapped entirely.

You will note my agreement here.    While I don't use them, my argument against
them won't be complete until EXIT PARAGRAPH is available though.

exit paragraph logic has costs in increased mistakes.   That is a real issue. 
Typing 4-5 extra characters with paragraph names is not.

So when I get a chance to input into standards, I concentrate my efforts on the
former.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-13T21:06:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405132006.1e2868f6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com> <c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405131205.305b17d8@posting.google.com> <c80mac$ctv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> Wastes of time and space don't cost anything.

Well, they cost me.  I don't have the luxury of twiddling around until
5 pm turns up.
 
> Not much.   My statement is that they don't cost anything.

Interesting economic theory you live in.   ;-)

> Evolution says that traits that don't cost stick around.

Well _that_ explains why I dumped this years ago.

> > No, but finding out what they are _is_.
> 
> How is it more difficult to find out how to spell the name of
> 9000-FIND-NEXT-IDENT, compared to FIND-NEXT-IDENT?    Either way, we've got to
> check.

If every utility paragraph is 9000- then it is completely pointless. 
If it is Find-Next-Ident then I don't need to look it up, it is
'Find-Next-Ident', I can just write that in the PERFORM.  It is only
when it has 9nnn-Find-Next-Ident that I need to find out what random
digits were used as nnn.

> > If they are by level and position ...
> > too much effort to renumber, ...

> Not in my experience.   They are just names.   I thought I said that.

Well no, they are not _names_, that is the part the follows the
number.  If the numbers don't indicate position or hierarchy, then
they simply have no purpose at all.

Of course when they were first used (in the 1960s) it was common to
have numeric only labels in other languages, every number had to be
unique and a hierarchic structure indicated how code and labels were
grouped.  This was when spagetti code used gotos between dozens of
labels within a 'procedure'.

When programmers moved to 'modern' languages that allowed alphanumeric
labels they still clung to the old ways of organizing code, still used
goto and even alter, still used numbers.

> So when I get a chance to input into standards, I concentrate my efforts on
> the former.

Good luck with that then.  Drag your site into the 1980s by any means
you can.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

```
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T14:23:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c82koh$hgq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com> <c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405131205.305b17d8@posting.google.com> <c80mac$ctv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405132006.1e2868f6@posting.google.com>`

```

On 13-May-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> If every utility paragraph is 9000- then it is completely pointless.
> If it is Find-Next-Ident then I don't need to look it up, it is
> 'Find-Next-Ident', I can just write that in the PERFORM.  It is only
> when it has 9nnn-Find-Next-Ident that I need to find out what random
> digits were used as nnn.

It might be FIND-NEXT-ID.

You know and I know, that we can't guess about the spelling of the paragraph
that does a function.  We have to look it up.


> > Not in my experience.   They are just names.   I thought I said that.
>
> Well no, they are not _names_, that is the part the follows the
> number.  If the numbers don't indicate position or hierarchy, then
> they simply have no purpose at all.

originally meant - but what it really means is you.
```

```
>
>On 13-May-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>It might be FIND-NEXT-ID.

Not if the standard prohibits abbreviations. Run my demos through a spellchecker
and you'll find they contain nothing but English words.  By doing that, you
don't waste time looking to see how 'identifier' was abbreviated _this_ time. Or
worse, inadvertently create two versions, then have someone guess at the
spelling and get the wrong one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T10:32:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40A4E6AA.66E5DE5A@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405051113.22569ee3@posting.google.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com> <c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405131205.305b17d8@posting.google.com> <c80mac$ctv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405132006.1e2868f6@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>
> When programmers moved to 'modern' languages that allowed alphanumeric
> labels they still clung to the old ways of organizing code, still used
> goto and even alter, still used numbers.

"ALTER' did not survive very long.  As one who was there then, I can tell you that
our instructors urged us not to use it as it added too much uncertainty to program
flow; was, in fact, an unnecessary carry-over from assemblers, which do use this
technique.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> you can.

There's far too much venom in what you post.  Is it a personal insult that other
people don't use your style?  Are you going to assert that yours is the only valid
style?  Or that other styles won't work?  Life is too short for getting all upset
because other people don't agree with you.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T17:06:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405141606.58fc17b3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com> <c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405131205.305b17d8@posting.google.com> <c80mac$ctv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405132006.1e2868f6@posting.google.com> <40A4E6AA.66E5DE5A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote 

> "ALTER' did not survive very long.  As one who was there then, I can tell you that
> our instructors urged us not to use it as it added too much uncertainty to program
> flow; was, in fact, an unnecessary carry-over from assemblers, which do use this
> technique.

Exactly, and it was my point that numeric sequencing labels is also an
unnecessary carry over from ancient assemblers.

>> Good luck with that then.  Drag your site into the 1980s by any
means
>> you can.

> There's far too much venom in what you post.  Is it a personal insult that
> other people don't use your style?  

Not at all, it isn't personal at all.  They are using it because that
is what the site standard tells them to.  I am insulting the archaic
standard that needs to be dumped, just as Howard said he was working
against them.

> Are you going to assert that yours is the only valid
> style?  Or that other styles won't work?  Life is too short for getting all upset
> because other people don't agree with you.

I hope that I am pointing out that if labels are always prefixed by a
fixed 9000- just to satify the letter of the standard that wants a
number on the front then this is completely pointless (though it may
be predicatable).

If there are different numbers on each label but they do not follow
any hierarchy or sequencing (ie just random), then these just get in
the way and waste time with no apparent benefit (except that the
standards police won't get you).

If the numbers are carefully structured to follow the levels in the
HIPO chart and these are used to sequence the order of the labels then
this _is_ useful, or at least was when the mechanism was mandated for
assembler programs on boxes of cards.

I am not making these arguments "because this is my style", these are
axiomatic.

It seems that in some sites the 'standards' are as if they were set
back in the 60s by the senior programmer who was previously brought up
on assembler.  When he bacame the DP Manager no one dared suggest that
his 'baby' is a load of archaic crap, now completely detached from
advances in technology in senior management he would be upset if his
legacy to programming style were dumped.
```

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T16:15:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8aof8$4m3$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <40A10991.1E5C49DE@mb.sympatico.ca> <98fyo$EeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0405121212.4a5c721f@posting.google.com> <c7u13s$c15$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405121915.6edbb641@posting.google.com> <c800e7$rfn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405131205.305b17d8@posting.google.com> <c80mac$ctv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0405132006.1e2868f6@posting.google.com> <40A4E6AA.66E5DE5A@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0405141606.58fc17b3@posting.google.com>`

```

On 14-May-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> If there are different numbers on each label but they do not follow
> any hierarchy or sequencing (ie just random), then these just get in
> the way and waste time with no apparent benefit (except that the
> standards police won't get you).

And I didn't argue that it had significant benefit.   I argued that it didn't
have significant cost.   So it survives.

Typing extra characters isn't a significant cost.   You seem to think that there
is a significant advantage in being able to guess the name and spelling of a
paragraph that you want to perform as long as it doesn't contain meaningless
numbers.    But I remain unconvinced.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T20:55:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<993fS2v9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405141606.58fc17b3@posting.google.com> <c8aof8$4m3$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
      Am  17.05.04
 schrieb  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  c8aof8$4m3$1@peabody.colorado.edu
   ueber  Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

>> If there are different numbers on each label but they do not follow
>> any hierarchy or sequencing (ie just random), then these just get in
>> the way and waste time with no apparent benefit (except that the
>> standards police won't get you).

HB> And I didn't argue that it had significant benefit.   I argued that
HB> it didn't have significant cost.   So it survives.

  I once worked in a project where I had established a standard scheme  
for transaction programs, whose skeleton was generated automatically  
(SSG for those familiar with OS/1100), with one section called  
something like the "Input-Processing SECTION".

  There was one colleague in the team insisted that he should prefix  
the section names with numbers, and in one program he had a

  010-Input-Processing SECTION

and a

  011-Input-Processing SECTION


  I think it did a lot of harm that the names of the sections did not  
tell anything about different tasks performed in these sections.

  The section names should differ in their descriptive name, not in a  
number.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Die Interessen der Nation lassen sich nicht anders formulieren als unter
dem Gesichtspunkt der herrschenden Klasse oder der Klasse, die die
Herrschaft anstrebt."            - Leo Trotzki         (27. Januar 1932)
```

```
Russell Styles wrote:

>     There used to be rumors that some people put code into some of the
> programs that dealt with reassigning solders that checked to insure
> that certain people where never assigned to some of the most remote
> posts.

Heh - I wouldn't put it past 'em.  There was actually an official deal 
at one time for someone who could write a .dll to take heart rate 
information from a telemetry (sp?) device.  If they wrote it, their SSN 
would be hard-code into the fitness software so they would pass for the 
next 5 years.

(No, I didn't do it...  :>  )
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-05-01T13:21:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6dd8322.0405011221.10f11698@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <c6lo8r$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4090222C.FDA56581@mb.sympatico.ca> <97mSTktPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Bottom posting

Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<4091195D.2F95E852@mb.sympatico.ca>...
> Lueko Willms wrote:
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

I largely agree with Peter, though I would say that there are probably
several "right" ways to write programs and also several "wrong" ways
too.  Also probably many ways that incorporate both "right" and
"wrong" aspects.

I think the GO TO statement is a very useful facility that has been
grossly abused by many programmers and pundits.  Just because
something has been badly used does not necessarily mean that it should
never be used.

I currently try to use as few GO TO statements as I can get away with
without making the resultant code excessively convoluted, often I
don't need any at all.

A conditional construct is often much more legible/intelligible for
having a series of fairly simple IF statements that each terminate in
a GO TO section exit than a large nested IF or EVALUATE that rambles
over several screens or pages.  Nevertheless, I still use nested IFs
and the EVALUATE statement as they also are very useful.  It all
depends upon the circumstances.

Robert
```

```
Peter Lacey wrote:

> I have written programs - and maintained programs written by others - in both
> styles.  Therefore I feel qualified to make the above statement.  The key
> postulate is "properly written".

I'll not even comment about the particular item in question - just a 
side comment.  What appears "properly written" to the original 
programmer can seem decidedly "improperly written" to the maintenance 
programmer - that's what makes our job more of an art than a science.  :)
```

<a id="t-97aad85032"></a>
## Thread: Sorts — 9 msgs

### Sorts

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-01T23:00:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<409420e7.162904098@news.optonline.net>`

```
Conversation between me (rw) and a co-worker (xx)

xx: What do you have there?
rw: Two Cobol programs that demonstrate under-used features. 
     My favorite is replacing paragraph names with Entry statements.
xx: What are you going to do with them?
rw: I was thinking of posting them to a newsgroup.
xx: You should publish them in a magazine.
rw: I used to write for Computer Language. It didn't pay much money 
     but name recognition got an invitation to speak at a big 
     convention. That, in turn, led to a consulting contract.
xx: Did you get the same reception in the newsgroup?
rw: Nah, lots of bickering and name-calling.
xx: There you go.


*             I'll take Sorts And Lists for a thousand, Alex
*
* These two programs -- Sorts and Lists -- demonstrate Cobol Features
* overlooked by most programmers, while running timing tests on
* sort algorithms.
*
* Glossary.
* We will see three data structures:
*
* Table   An array defined with Occurs. Same as a sequential file.
*
* List    Each entry has a pointer or index containing the address
*         of the next entry, and optionally the previous. Same as
*         a 'random' file.
*
* Tree    Like a family tree, where each node contains one data
*         value and pointers to up to two children, named Left,
*         containing entries less than the node's data, and Right.
*         Nodes with fewer than two children are called Leaves.
*         Same as an indexed file.
*
* Sorts program
*
* Feature: Nested Programs
*
* Cobol is often criticized for having 'an ocean of data' in
* working-storage that is globally accessible to all parts of the
* program. It is also criticized for having overly large monolithic
* programs. Nested programs, seen below, are one answer to that
* objection; we will see another in Trees. They are written like
* external called programs, but they are in the same source file
* with the caller. If they were separately compiled, there would
* be additional administrative work to list them on program
* inventories, promote them through Change Management, perhaps
* list them as dependents in make files.
*
* Nested programs have their own private working-storage and lack
* access to their parent(s)' working-storage unless passed as
* a parameter or declared Global. They have been in the ANSI
* Cobol Standard since 1985.
*
* Test 1. Cobol file sort
*
* This is a familiar Cobol sort with input and output procedures,
* release and return records. No Features here, it is shown to provide a
* timing baseline.
*
* Test 2. Micro Focus table sort
*
* Feature: Table sort
*
* Micro Focus provides an extension allowing you sort a table in memory.
* When we time it, we find its speed is almost identical to the file
* sort in test 1, and half the speed it 'should' be as shown in test 3.
* Apparently, under the covers it is releasing records to and returning
* records from the file sort. This was an extension to the 1985 Standard,
* is included in the 2002 Standard.
*
* Test 3. Quick sort
*
* Feature: Calling C language functions from Cobol
*
* This test calls qsort() in the Standard C Library, and directs it to
* call memcmp().
*
* How do you tell Cobol to use the C calling convention? Not necessary,
* Micro Focus uses the C convention by default. How do you tell the
* linker (loader) to look in C libraries? Not necessary. We stopped
* doing static binding (link edit) many years ago. Binding is now done
* at execution time by a program named "ld", which follows
* LD_LIBRARY_PATH. The C Standard Library is in that path by default. In
* short, you don't have to do anything special. You can see libraries
* that will be loaded at execution time by typing "ldd sorts".
*
* C libraries contain a plethora of useful functions. See
* http://www.tcnj.edu/~cs/doc/CStdLib.html
* also
* http://www.bebits.com/app/2917
* also
* a hundred others in /usr/lib, many defined in Standards
*
* The speed of quick sort is said to be proportional to N log2(N), where
* N is the number of entries and log2 is the base 2 logarithm. Most
* high-speed algorithms run at this speed, for example Shell, Heap and
* Comb sorts. Low-speed sorts such as Bubble run at N*N, are suitable
* only for small values of N, say less than 100.
*
* Tests 4 & 5. Radix sort followed by Insertion sort
*
* Features: The radix algorithm
*           Linked lists using indexes
*
* When we had to sort a large number of punched cards, we put them into
* approximate sequence by sorting on the first few columns with a card
* sorter. Next we put them through a machine called a collator, which
* pulled cards out of order, typically 5-10%. We sorted those cards on
* the full key, then used a collator to merge them into the big deck.
*
* Would you believe the card sorting algorithm, written in Cobol, is
* five times faster than the best high-speed algorithms written in C,
* and about ten times faster than the sorts delivered by Micro Focus?
*
* The algorithm inspects one 'column' at a time, right to left, tossing
* 'cards' into 'pockets' by linking the 'card' to what had previously
* been the last in the 'pocket'. At the end of each pass, it 'gathers'
* the pocket strings into a single string ('deck') by linking the last
* 'card' in a 'pocket' to the first 'card' in the next 'pocket'.
*
* A 'column' can be any convenient size -- one bit, one byte or one
* word. The demo program defines a column as two bytes, 16 bits. This
* requires 65,536 'pockets', of which only a few thousand are actually
* used.
*
* When cards are approximately in order, the program 'merges' the ones
* out of order into place with an Insertion Sort. As a primary
* algorithm, Insertion is slow, close to N*N. But when the entries are
* almost in sequence, it is quite fast. It almost always puts them in
* order with one pass, then makes a second pass to verify that the job
* is complete. It takes a third pass only when there are two consecutive
* steps down. In the 50,000 item test, that doesn't happen, it runs in
* two passes. If radix had sorted on seven bytes rather than six, they
* would have been in perfect order. Insertion would have taken only one
* pass.
*
* The Radix-Insertion algorithm speed is proportional to kN + 2N+, where
* k is the number of columns sorted by Radix (in this case 3) and the +
* following 2N is close-range rearrangements by Insertion. In THEORY,
* the relative speeds of test 3 vs. test 5 should be 50,000 *
* log2(50,000) = 785,000 vs. 3*50,000 + 2*50,000+ =~ 300,000. The ratio
* is 2.6. In PRACTICE, it is 5.0 times as fast. As usual, the devil is
* in the details. If the test program had used subscripts rather than
* indexes, its speed would have been cut in half. Not because indexes
* are that much faster, because every subscript operation generates a
* call to the runtime whereas index operations produce inline code (seen
* via the now commented ASM options).
*
* [If your calculator doesn't have log2, take ln (base e) and divide by .69,
* which is ln(2).]
*
* To compile this "cob -x sorts.cbl". -x produces a Unix executable.
* To execute "sorts".
*
* Continued in trees.cbl
*
* ---------------------------------------------------------------------

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
      $SET NOBOUND
      $SET OPT"2"
      $SET NOTRUNC
      $SET IBMCOMP
      $SET NOCHECK
*     $SET ASM
*     $SET ASMLIST
*     $SET SOURCEASM
 identification division.
 program-id. Sorts.
 author. Robert Wagner.
* Compare speed of three sorts.

* results: Sun SPARC, 1.8 GHz, each test was run ten times.
*                                  --- ratio ---
*                                   2.   3.   4.   5.
* 1. Cobol file sort         3.7   1.0  1.5  7.4  9.3
* 2. Micro Focus table sort  3.8        1.5  7.6  9.5
* 3. qsort()                 2.5             5.0  6.3
* 4. radix-insertion          .5                  1.3
* 5. radix to linked list     .4
*
* Trees.cbl contains an expanded version of these test results.

 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  test-data GLOBAL.
     05  radix-columns               comp  pic s9(02).
     05  test-number                 comp  pic s9(02).
     05  card      occurs 50001 indexed                *> the data
            x-n first-n last-n previous-n-0 previous-n-1 previous-n-2 x-last.
         10  sort-key.
             15  key-column occurs 15 indexed x-c comp-x pic xx.
         10  next-n                             index.
         10  next-n-1                           index.

 01  init-data.
     05  init-columns value zero     comp  pic s9(02).
     05  init-number  value zero     comp  pic s9(02).
     05  init-card occurs 50001 indexed i-n i-next i-last.
         10  init-key-1                    pic v9(18).
         10  init-key-2                    pic v9(12).
         10  init-next                     index.
         10  init-next-1                   index.

 01  timer-variables.
     05  repeat-factor value 10      comp  pic s9(09).
     05  previous-key                      pic  x(30).
     05  card-count                  comp  pic s9(09).
     05  start-time.
         10  start-hours                   pic  9(02).
         10  start-minutes                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-seconds                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-hundredths              pic  9(02).
     05  end-time.
         10  end-hours                     pic  9(02).
         10  end-minutes                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-seconds                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-hundredths                pic  9(02).
     05  elapsed-time                      pic  z(04).9.

 procedure division.
   perform construct-data

   display '1. Cobol file sort'
   move 1 to init-number
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       perform initialize-test-data
       call 'cobol-sort'
   end-perform
   perform timer-off
   perform verify-sort

   display '2. Micro Focus sort'
   move 2 to init-number
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       perform initialize-test-data
       call 'mf-sort'
   end-perform
   perform timer-off
   perform verify-sort

   display '3. quick sort N log2(N)'
   move 3 to init-number
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       perform initialize-test-data
       call 'quick-sort'
   end-perform
   perform timer-off
   perform verify-sort

   display '4. radix-insertion kN + 2N+'
   move 4 to init-number
   move 3 to init-columns
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       perform initialize-test-data
       call 'radix-sort'
   end-perform
   perform timer-off
   move zero to init-columns
   perform verify-sort

   display '5. radix-insertion to linked list'
   move 5 to init-number
   move 3 to init-columns
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       perform initialize-test-data
       call 'radix-sort'
   end-perform
   perform timer-off

   goback

 . construct-data.
   set x-last, i-last to 50001
   perform varying i-n from 1 by 1 until i-n = i-last
       compute init-key-1 (i-n) = function random
       compute init-key-2 (i-n) = function random
       set i-next to i-n
       set i-next up by 1
       set init-next (i-n) to i-next
       set init-next-1 (i-n) to i-last
   end-perform
   move high-values to init-card (i-last)

 . initialize-test-data.
   move init-data to test-data
   set first-n to 1

 . verify-sort.
   move zero to previous-key, card-count
   set x-n to first-n
   if  x-n equal to x-last
       display 'test failed, no first'
   end-if
   perform until x-n equal to x-last
       if  sort-key (x-n) less than previous-key
           display 'test failed '
               previous-key space sort-key (x-n)
           set x-n to x-last
       else
           move sort-key (x-n) to previous-key
           if  init-columns not equal to zero
               set x-n to next-n (x-n)
           else
               set x-n up by 1
           end-if
           add 1 to card-count
       end-if
   end-perform
   if  card-count not equal to 50000
       display 'test failed, count is ' card-count
   end-if

 . timer-on.
     accept start-time from time
 . timer-off.
     accept end-time from time
     compute elapsed-time rounded =
        (((((((end-hours * 60) +
           end-minutes) * 60) +
           end-seconds) * 100) +
           end-hundredths) -
         ((((((start-hours * 60) +
           start-minutes) * 60) +
           start-seconds) * 100) +
           start-hundredths)) / 100
     display  elapsed-time ' seconds'
 .

 identification division.
 program-id. cobol-sort.
* 1. Performance of Micro Focus file sort.
 environment division.
 input-output section.
 file-control.
     select sort-file assign  to 'sortwork'.
 data division.
 file section.
 sd  sort-file.
 01  file-record.
     05  file-key         pic x(30).
 procedure division.
   sort sort-file on ascending file-key
       input procedure input-procedure
       output procedure output-procedure
   goback
 . input-procedure.
   perform varying x-n from 1 by 1 until x-n = x-last
       release file-record from sort-key (x-n)
   end-perform
 . output-procedure.
   perform varying x-n from 1 by 1 until x-n = x-last
       return sort-file into sort-key (x-n) at end continue
   end-perform
 . end program cobol-sort.

 identification division.
 program-id. mf-sort.
* 2. Performance of Micro Focus table sort.
* Appears to be using above file sort, because speed is nearly
* identical, and half as fast as it 'should' be.
 procedure division.
   sort card on ascending sort-key
   goback
 . end program mf-sort.

 identification division.
 program-id. quick-sort.
* 3. Performance of quick sort in the C library
 working-storage section.
 01  memcmp-pointer procedure-pointer.
 procedure division.
   set memcmp-pointer to entry 'memcmp'

   call 'qsort' using
       card (1)
       by value 50001
       by value length of card
       by value memcmp-pointer

   goback
 . end program quick-sort.

 identification division.
 program-id. radix-sort.
* 4 & 5. Radix sort followed by insertion sort.
* This treats cards as a linked list.
* It does not move cards; it changes forward 'pointers' next-n.
* Test 4 converts the list to a table (for searching); test 5 does not.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  radix-variables.
     05  swap-count                comp   pic s9(09).
     05                  value '00'.
         10  first-pocket          comp-x pic  xx.
     05                  value '99'.
         10  last-pocket           comp-x pic  xx.
     05  radix-pockets.
         10  radix-pocket occurs 65536 indexed pocket, p-last.
             15  first-in-pocket   index.
             15  last-in-pocket    index.
 01  sorted-data.
     05  sorted-columns value zero    comp  pic s9(02).
     05  sorted-number  value zero    comp  pic s9(02).
     05  sorted-card    occurs 50001 indexed s-n.
         10  sorted-key                     pic  x(30).
         10                                     index.
         10                                     index.

 procedure division.
   set p-last to last-pocket
   set p-last up by 1
*  One iteration per column. Speed is kN, where k is the key length
*  in columns. In this case a column is two bytes.
   perform varying x-c from radix-columns by -1 until x-c < 1
*  RUN through the input string, tossing entries into pockets
*  (uppercase words refer to card sorter operations)
       perform varying pocket from first-pocket by 1 until pocket > p-last
           set first-in-pocket (pocket), last-in-pocket (pocket) to x-last
       end-perform
       add 1 to last-pocket
       set x-n to first-n
       perform until x-n equal to x-last
*   determine pocket
           set pocket to key-column (x-n, x-c)
           set pocket up by 1
           if  first-in-pocket (pocket) equal to x-last
               set first-in-pocket (pocket) to x-n
           end-if
           set last-n to last-in-pocket (pocket)
*   link to previous last in pocket
           if  last-in-pocket (pocket) not equal to x-last
               set next-n-1 (last-n) to x-n
           end-if
*   make this last in pocket
           set last-in-pocket (pocket) to x-n
           set x-n to next-n (x-n)
       end-perform
*  GATHER - link tail of each pocket to head of next
       set first-n, last-n to x-last
       perform varying pocket from first-pocket by 1 until pocket > p-last
           if  first-in-pocket (pocket) not = x-last
           set x-n to first-in-pocket (pocket)
               if  first-n equal to x-last
                   set first-n to x-n    *> first card
               end-if
               if  last-n not equal to x-last
                   set next-n-1 (last-n) to x-n
               end-if
               set last-n to last-in-pocket (pocket)
           end-if
       end-perform
       set next-n-1 (last-n) to x-last  *> last card
*  MOUNT - input is now in the new order
       perform varying x-n from 1 by 1 until x-n = x-last
           set next-n (x-n) to next-n-1 (x-n)
       end-perform
   end-perform

*  If radix-columns = 15, the above is all you need.
*  By itself, it runs faster than the MF sort when radix-columns < 9.
*  The optimized solution sets radix-columns so the radix sort puts
*  data almost in order. It then merges out of order data into place
*  using an insertion sort, which usually works in two iterations --
*  one to sort and one to verify.
*  The total time is 3N + 2N+ = 5N+

*  MERGE out of order cards
*  This is a modified insertion sort which does not
*  require backward links. When finding a step down, it searches
*  forward looking for a step up, then exchanges the big record before
*  the step down with the little record before the step up, then takes
*  a step backward. It cannot work in one pass because the step back
*  cannot find (n - 2) when there are two consecutive steps back.
*  I don't know whether this is a documented algorithm; I just made it up.

   move 1 to swap-count
   perform until swap-count = zero
       move zero to swap-count
       move card (first-n) to sorted-card (1)
       set s-n to 2
       set previous-n-0 to x-last
       set previous-n-1  to first-n
       set x-n to next-n (first-n)
       perform until x-n equal to x-last
           if  sort-key (x-n) less than sort-key (previous-n-1)
               perform until x-n equal to x-last or
                   sort-key (x-n) not less than sort-key (previous-n-1)
                   set previous-n-2 to x-n
                   set x-n to next-n (x-n)
               end-perform
*  exchange two cards (could use three moves)
   call 'CBL_XOR' using sort-key (previous-n-1) sort-key (previous-n-2)
       by value length of sort-key
   call 'CBL_XOR' using sort-key (previous-n-2) sort-key (previous-n-1)
       by value length of sort-key
   call 'CBL_XOR' using sort-key (previous-n-1) sort-key (previous-n-2)
       by value length of sort-key
*              move sort-key (previous-n-1) to sort-key (x-last)
*              move sort-key (previous-n-2) to sort-key (previous-n-1)
*              move sort-key (x-last)       to sort-key (previous-n-2)
*              move high-values to sort-key (x-last)
               set x-n to previous-n-0
               if  x-n equal to x-last
                   set x-n, first-n to previous-n-1
               end-if
               add 1 to swap-count
           end-if
*  Straighten out linked list so program can Search All. Adds 25% to time.
           if  test-number equal to 4 and
               swap-count equal to zero
               move sort-key (x-n) to sorted-key (s-n)
               set s-n up by 1
           end-if
           set previous-n-0 to previous-n-1
           set previous-n-1 to x-n
           set x-n to next-n (x-n)
       end-perform
   end-perform
   if  test-number equal to 4
       move high-values to sorted-key (x-last)
       move sorted-data to test-data
   end-if
   set first-n to 1

   goback
 . end program radix-sort
 . end program Sorts.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorts

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-08T08:38:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<409c8f0c.86610493@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<409420e7.162904098@news.optonline.net> <109okljkg6u6pe9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
>You know, just the other day I was wondering where you had gotten off to 
>- good to see you peeking your head in here!  :)

Thanx for the greeting. I moved across the Hudson from the land of Financial
Services in NY to the land of Controlled Substances in NJ. Gotta love the
euphemisms. The former means 'no tangible product' whereas the latter means
'lots of room for slippage with a 90% gross profit percent and support from the
FDA.' Life is good in both places but Jerseyites are more mannerly and
laid-back. Plus, the countryside around Morris County is beautiful. 

I miss the good people of Montgomery. It's a world apart from the NorthEast,
with a grace that Yankees will never understand. Black people who aren't angry,
who drive middle-class cars, wear middle-class clothes, listen to African music
rather than rap or hip-hop, do not have street gangs. In short, they're not
angry about their station because they're not oppressed.

I'm working on a demo of multi-threading in Cobol.. Look for it here, soon.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorts

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-08T16:19:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<-qadneuSu6JJ0wDdRVn-sA@giganews.com>`

```
Robert Jones wrote:
> Message snipped
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> SHARE, the IBM user group.

[...]

Yes, I, too, wonder if the U.S. will ever completely adopt the Metric
system. But what really keeps me awake nights is why have we never heard of
cases of "Spontaneous Animal Combusion?"

Migrating ducks falling from the sky like Bomber Command casualties over
Berlin. Earthworms going "poof" underfoot. Nothing but ashes in little
Fluffy's bed! Explosions in the midsts of a buffalo stampede. And if this
happened to, say, a termite, might not the house burn down?

Thank God there's probably no chance of this happening to DEAD animals(?!),
else there would be a new meaning to "heartburn." Nevertheless, it's
worrisome.
```
