###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T07:06:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tjjpc.6742$hH.165434@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c82lli$i1f$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <c82o3p$pdk$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aasbrd3ii655a@corp.supernews.com> <c83s6k$gd1$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com>`

```


LX-i wrote:
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why is that?  They didn't like efficiency?

Back in the days of IBM OS/VS COBOL and CICS 1.6.1 there were several 
COBOL verbs that did not work correctly or efficiently in a CICS 
environment.  SEARCH may well have been one of them.  EXAMINE/INSPECT 
may have been another.

The story I was told at the time was that certain verbs generated 
calls to the OS/VS COBOL runtime library routines, and those routines 
were not built to run in the CICS quasi-reentrant environment.

In 1992 we switched to COBOL II, and I forgot all the old restrictions.

Some shops may have simply forbidden SEARCH because they believed it 
was too complicated for their programmers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T08:30:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c852gp$l0r$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c83s6k$gd1$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com> <tjjpc.6742$hH.165434@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <tjjpc.6742$hH.165434@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

[snip]

>> Why is that?  They didn't like efficiency?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>may have been another.

INSPECT was one, STRING/UNSTRING were others... I don't recall ever coding 
a SEARCH for a CICS program, things were either in very small tables or 
one would hit a file/table to get them.

[snip]

>In 1992 we switched to COBOL II, and I forgot all the old restrictions.

I'll never forget the 'holy excrement!' sensation when I first saw a 
multi-item INSPECT's PMAP... errrr, LISTing under IGYCRCTL... I'll try to 
remember that for Monday, as well; to code

INSPECT INPUT-REC REPLACING ALL 'a' BY 'A'
                            ALL 'b' BY 'B'
...
                            ALL 'z' BY 'Z'.

(or whatever the syntax is that'll compile... can't remember if each one 
needs an ALL or not)

... and compile it under IKFCBL00 and IGYCRCTL.  The former will produce 
what is technically called a 'God-Awful Ugly Mess' while the latter will 
produces something like two or four statements... one of which is a TRT, I 
know, but I forget the rest.

>
>Some shops may have simply forbidden SEARCH because they believed it 
>was too complicated for their programmers.

... or themselves, see my response earlier.

DD
```

```
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> know, but I forget the rest.
The instruction is TR for Translate which converts as stated above as 
opposed to TRT - Translate and Test which is used to scan for selected 
characters and set condition codes and Register 1 with the found character.
> 
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T16:08:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<egrpc.8028$eH1.4190205@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c83s6k$gd1$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com> <tjjpc.6742$hH.165434@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <c852gp$l0r$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:c852gp$l0r$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <tjjpc.6742$hH.165434@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
> Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ... or themselves, see my response earlier.

Don't laugh.

True story:

First time I did some work for a particular client (IBM mainframe COBOL), it
was because their department's lead COBOL programmer, who had been doing
their COBOL work for nine (9) years, was not familiar with the application.

So I did my thing, and when completed the lead programmer wanted to inspect
the source code to ensure it was maintainable - an eminently sensible thing
to do, right?

Imagine my surprise when this lead programmer with nine years of experience
told me that in addition to my code being very maintainable, she was really
glad to have seen it, because it was the first time she had ever seen the
SEARCH verb used.

The lesson I learned was, never assume ANYTHING about a customer's skill or
experience level.....

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T16:40:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ad3dpt27nst06@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<tjjpc.6742$hH.165434@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c82lli$i1f$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <c82o3p$pdk$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aasbrd3ii655a@corp.supernews.com> <c83s6k$gd1$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com> <tjjpc.6742$hH.165434@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
> 
> LX-i wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> built to run in the CICS quasi-reentrant environment.

I'd buy that.  A lot of our old code has exit paragraphs on -every- 
paragraph - I was told that this was to compensate for a long-ago-fixed 
bug.  :)

> In 1992 we switched to COBOL II, and I forgot all the old restrictions.
> 
> Some shops may have simply forbidden SEARCH because they believed it was 
> too complicated for their programmers.

Too complicated?  To me, it's simpler than an inline perform (and 
cleaner - you don't have to "subtract 1" as you sometimes may with an 
inline perform), plus the fact (well, it' s my experience, anyway) that 
it's faster.  There was a program that would take about 3 minutes to run 
if it didn't find anything.  I modified it to read the data into memory 
in big chunks, and used SEARCH to find matching values - it takes about 
5 seconds now.  (Granted, some of that is in I/O improvement, but some 
of it is due to SEARCH.)

It's just a training issue - I've never had it properly explained to me 
outside this newsgroup.  I've passed on the knowledge, though, and not 
one person I've explained it to has had any trouble grasping it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T08:22:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8521u$kpd$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <10aasbrd3ii655a@corp.supernews.com> <c83s6k$gd1$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why is that?  They didn't like efficiency?

I'm not sure about the 'efficiency' aspect... come Monday, if I remember 
and have the time, I'll fire up ol' IKFCBL00 (the IBM mainframe ANSI '74 
compiler that installations still have but try to keep very well hidden) 
and code a few tests to see what kind of PMAPs they make.  I'll do a 
PERFORM of an -EXIT paragraph to compare fields of different lengths 
(one-char vs. multi-char) and a couple of SEARCHes to do the same; I'll 
report here about the code generated by both.

Now you might ask... why do I use OldBOL for this?  I do so because the 
guys in charge of the shops where SEARCHes were forbidden were trained in 
OldBOL... and not very well, it seems, because while they would tell me 
Very Very Seriously that SEARCHes were prone to efficiency issues...

... the guys in the trenches would ask 'So, did Jonesie tell you how bad 
SEARCHes were?  Don't believe it... he just doesn't understand how they 
work and they confuse him.'

DD
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```
I don't use SEARCH for small tables.   I don't really have good guidelines for
how big a table should be to switch to SEARCH, SEARCH ALL, or keyed file, but
size does make a difference in my choice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-24T07:56:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8t2eo$fcc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <10aasbrd3ii655a@corp.supernews.com> <c83s6k$gd1$1@panix5.panix.com> <10aatmaobtt01fc@corp.supernews.com> <c8521u$kpd$1@panix5.panix.com> <c8alvb$1s6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:c8alvb$1s6$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> I don't really have good guidelines for
> how big a table should be to switch to SEARCH, SEARCH ALL, ...

Little-known point about SEARCH vs. SEARCH ALL:  The standard does require
that a SEARCH without ALL be done serially through the table.  What it does
*not* require is that the implementor use a "non-serial" (typically binary)
search algorithm for SEARCH ALL.

The rules are set up to disallow duplicates and misordering within the table
for SEARCH ALL, which make it possible for a non-serial search to work
predictably, and suggests explicitly that a non-serial search *may* be used
in this case.  I know of one implementation that chose to make the
duplicates case predictable (at the request of users), and identical to that
of SEARCH without ALL, by making the SEARCH ALL algorithm serial.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T06:53:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <c82eb3$ali$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>
>> As you may know, NEXT SENTENCE is fraught and does not always behave
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is something else the case?

Well, yes. First, there's your "unless" modifier.

Second, NEXT SENTENCE gets in the way for those who speak the COBOL dialect
of using one period per paragraph.

Third, NEXT SENTENCE is an artifact from a simpler time before scope
delimiters muddied its logic water.

My view is that the logic constructs of scope delimiters and NEXT SENTENCE
are fundamentally incompatible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T08:40:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8534n$lah$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <c82eb3$ali$1@panix5.panix.com> <4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com>`

```
In article <4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com>,
JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Well, yes. First, there's your "unless" modifier.

All right, consider it first... what about it?  It states that the
boundaries of a NEXT SENTENCE are not to exceed the scope of the PERFORM 
which invoked it.

>
>Second, NEXT SENTENCE gets in the way for those who speak the COBOL dialect
>of using one period per paragraph.

Not at all... in such a case that 'unless' you noticed above kicks in and 
you have a de facto EXIT PARAGRAPH, how's *that* for convenience?

(It took me a while to come to that conclusion... my first response was 
'Those who speak the dialect of one period per paragraph get in the way of 
using NEXT SENTENCE; if the dialect one speaks restricts one's use of 
Standard constructions then one might want to consider a speech-coach.')

>
>Third, NEXT SENTENCE is an artifact from a simpler time before scope
>delimiters muddied its logic water.

As Wittgenstein said, 'Logic is a game played by a particular series of 
rules'... if those rules have changed it is up to the players to keep up 
on them.

>
>My view is that the logic constructs of scope delimiters and NEXT SENTENCE
>are fundamentally incompatible.

A lovely view, some might think... but can it be supported by reasoned 
argument?  If not then it might well be relegated to the Realm of 
Opinion... and long ago my Drill Sergeant taught me something about 
opinions.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-16T13:40:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com> <c8534n$lah$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> >> Not at all... in such a case that 'unless' you noticed above kicks in and 
> >> you have a de facto EXIT PARAGRAPH, how's *that* for convenience?

> Mr Plinston, when the Standards Committee post here then they can post 
> their own reasoned arguments, when you post here you are welcome to do the 
> same.

If you wish.

The NEXT SENTENCE clause is not an EXIT PARAGRAPH, it is a transfer to
after the next full stop.  Using END-IF allows for the reduction of
condition level without using a full stop.  This combination allows
the execution of code at condition level zero (eg outside any IF) to
be controlled by the result of the IF.

In fact it can be considered to be a GO TO to an anonymous label at
the position of the next full stop.  It is this complete anonymity
that is (one of) the problem with this contrived abuse of the
standard.  Any full stop can be the target as long as it is the first
following the NEXT SENTENCE.  This means that changing code outside
the scope of the IF statement(s) will affect the result of code inside
the IF.

Someone later maintaining the code can add a full stop knowing that
this is at lavel zero without noticing that they have fallen into the
bug trap.

This is especially a problem if the NEXT SENTENCE is contrived to
reach a point that is between the end of the IF and the end of the
paragraph, to an intermediate full stop.  There is no indication, at
the full stop, that this is the case and so moving or removing, or
adding another, has no implicit restriction.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-16T21:44:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>If you wish.

I stated what the Standards Committee can do when they post here, Mr 
Plinston, and what you are welcome to do.

>
>The NEXT SENTENCE clause is not an EXIT PARAGRAPH, it is a transfer to
>after the next full stop.

When a paragraph is controlled via a PERFORM, Mr Plinston - as it might 
well be when fall-through logic is avoided - and when there is only one 
period per paragraph - as some folks code, for whatever reason they see 
fit - then a NEXT SENTENCE executed control is passed back to the invoking 
PERFORM, bypassing all further code in the paragraph.

When an EXIT PARAGRAPH is executed control is passed back to the invoking 
PERFORM.

The fact of the NEXT SENTENCE's function under these conditions is that of 
bypassing execution of subsequent code in the paragraph.

The fact of EXIT PARAGRAPH's function is to bypass execution of subsequent 
code in the paragraph.

If the fact of NEXT SENTENCE's function under these conditions is
identical to an EXIT PARAGRAPH's function then the NEXT SENTENCE is 'being
such in effect' an EXIT PARAGRAPH.

'Being such in effect' is a definition of 'de facto'.

Under these conditions, then, a NEXT SENTENCE is a de facto EXIT 
PARAGRAPH.

If there is an error in assumption, definition or logic in the above then 
please, by all means, indicate it and demonstrate otherwise.

[snip]

>Someone later maintaining the code can add a full stop knowing that
>this is at lavel zero without noticing that they have fallen into the
>bug trap.

This seems, then, to be an argument against the 'one period/full stop per 
pargraph' style.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-16T21:45:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> When an EXIT PARAGRAPH is executed control is passed back to the invoking 
> PERFORM.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> pargraph' style.

It depends on how you look at it.  To me, this is an argument for "EXIT 
PARAGRAPH" as a valuable addition to the COBOL language.  It would -not- 
be broken by programmers of differing styles maintaining the same code, 
whereas counting on NEXT SENTENCE to do the job would.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T08:21:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i wrote:
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The exact same problem remains.  All you have done is changed the code 
so that instead of making the addition of sentences a problem, you have 
made the addition of paragraphs a problem. Now, you can no longer spilt 
a paragraph that is too long into two paragraphs without the possibility 
of introducing logic errors.

At some point in the game, code has to be delimited down to a single 
"token? unit?" of code that stands alone.  In Cobol, the units have 
traditionaly been "program", "section" "paragraph" and "sentence". We 
have eliminated sections, as their original use was for load units or 
overlays. Now you want to eliminate a second structuring unit.

Any top-down structured methodology *requires* that code be easily be 
subdivided, just as any bottom-up methodology *requires* that code units 
can be combined.  Eliminate periods to one per paragraph, and all you 
have really done is shuffled the problem elsewhere, and removed one of 
your main methodologies for both.

Donald


Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T19:51:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com> <892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> of introducing logic errors.

Of course you can't go in willy nilly, without analyzing the program, 
and add ANYTHING without the possibility of breaking something, be it a 
section (I know of at least one former regular poster who claims to 
perform nothing less), paragraph name, sentence, or clause.

However, if you have a program, particularly one you've written from 
scratch, that only ever performs a paragraph, and only ever goes to the 
"getOuttaDodge" paragraph, EXIT PARAGRAPH provides an excellent tool to 
eliminate the error-prone THRU.

(Please, no comments on coding style on this next paragraph - it's an 
example of what we have, not necessarily what we do now.)  For example, 
we have many online transaction programs.  Generally, you'll see 
something like

PERFORM 125-SCREEN-EDITS THRU 125-SCREEN-EDITS-EXIT
[code omitted]
125-SCREEN-EDITS.
     IF SCREEN-TYPE-TRANS = 'L' OR 'C' OR 'D' OR 'I'
         NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
         MOVE 1234 TO REJCDE
         GO TO 125-SCREEN-EDITS-EXIT.
     MOVE SCREEN-EQUIP-ID TO EQPP-EQUIP-ID
     FETCH EQPP-158 RECORD
     IF ERROR-NUM = ZEROES
         NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
         MOVE 5678 TO REJCDE
         GO TO 125-SCREEN-EDITS-EXIT.
     IF SCREEN-STATUS = 'FMC' OR 'NMC' OR 'PMC'
         NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
	MOVE 0987 TO REJCDE
         GO TO 125-SCREEN-EDITS-EXIT.

Now, this is pretty straightforward, and has a low risk of being botched 
during maintenance.  But, you've still got that THRU sitting up there, 
making it easy for someone to come in after the last edit and add a 
paragraph, causing it to be executed unintentionally.  What if we could 
say...

PERFORM 125-SCREEN-EDITS
[code omitted]
125-SCREEN-EDITS.
     IF SCREEN-TYPE-TRANS = 'L' OR 'C' OR 'D' OR 'I'
         NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
         MOVE 1234 TO REJCDE
         EXIT PARAGRAPH.
     MOVE SCREEN-EQUIP-ID TO EQPP-EQUIP-ID
     FETCH EQPP-158 RECORD
     IF ERROR-NUM = ZEROES
         NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
         MOVE 5678 TO REJCDE
         EXIT PARAGRAPH.
     IF SCREEN-STATUS = 'FMC' OR 'NMC' OR 'PMC'
         NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
	MOVE 0987 TO REJCDE
         EXIT PARAGRAPH.

This seems to me to be a worthy upgrade of the language, and parallels 
the behavior of EXIT PROGRAM provided for subprograms (both nested and 
external) in the COBOL 85 standard.  In fact, I have one program that 
does edits for all the background reports in our system.  I've actually 
converted that program to use nested subprograms precisely for that 
reason - EXIT PROGRAM gets me out early, without the risky THRU clause.

> At some point in the game, code has to be delimited down to a single 
> "token? unit?" of code that stands alone.  In Cobol, the units have 
> traditionaly been "program", "section" "paragraph" and "sentence". We 
> have eliminated sections, as their original use was for load units or 
> overlays. Now you want to eliminate a second structuring unit.

I can see how procedure division sections were valuable (and some would 
argue still are).  I'm not advocating elimination of a second 
structuring unit.  What happened with the explicit scope delimiters in 
COBOL 85 is that the sentence can actually be a very large, complex 
combination of statements.  I would say that "sentence" would (in 
practice, not by definition) be replaced by "statement".

> Any top-down structured methodology *requires* that code be easily be 
> subdivided, just as any bottom-up methodology *requires* that code units 
> can be combined.  Eliminate periods to one per paragraph, and all you 
> have really done is shuffled the problem elsewhere, and removed one of 
> your main methodologies for both.

Actually, I've found that viewing periods as part of the paragraph label 
(one before, one after) actually helps in maintenance.  Code can easily 
be cut-and-pasted from one portion of the code to another, and 
conditions can be added and removed, without worrying about whether it 
was at 0-level when we cut it, and now it's at 2-level.  All that really 
matters is the indentation, and since our compiler doesn't support 
free-format source, column 72 limitations.

I'm also old enough to know that what I call limitations, others will 
call "valuable structuring methods".  My beliefs on this come from my 
experience with maintenance programming, and things I've seen others 
done based on similar training.  That's why I'm not an advocate for 
removing THRU from the language, for example.  I just like the potential 
the (new) clauses provide.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T06:05:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LIhqc.18246$hH.453847@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com> <892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com> <10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com>`

```

LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message news:10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com...
> Donald Tees wrote:
> > LX-i wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> perform nothing less),

I do that.

> paragraph name, sentence, or clause.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> eliminate the error-prone THRU.

There is a better way, IMO.
>
> (Please, no comments on coding style on this next paragraph - it's an
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraph, causing it to be executed unintentionally.

Why would anyone intentionally add a paragraph between the start
and end points of another paragraph?  One of the main purposes
of numbered paragraphs is to avoid such incompetence.

> What if we could
> say...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> reason - EXIT PROGRAM gets me out early, without the risky THRU clause.

There is no need for a change to the language.
Nor is there anything risky about the THRU clause.
There is also no need for GO TO as an exit strategy.
>
> > At some point in the game, code has to be delimited down to a single
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> the (new) clauses provide.

I would structure the code to minimize the amount of time required
for someone new to pick up the program and decipher the logic.

        PERFORM 1250-SCREEN-EDIT  THRU 1250-SCREEN-EXIT.

[code omitted]

1250-SCREEN-EDIT                                      SECTION.

        IF  SCREEN-TYPE-TRANS = 'L' OR 'C' OR 'D' OR 'I'
              PERFORM 1300-RECORD-READ  THRU  1300-RECORD-EXIT
        ELSE
              MOVE   1250  TO  REJCDE.
1250-SCREEN-EXIT.
        EXIT.

1300-RECORD-READ                                    SECTION.

        MOVE  SCREEN-EQUIP-ID  TO  EQPP-EQUIP-ID.

        FETCH EQPP-158 RECORD.

        IF  ERROR-NUM  =  ZEROES
             PERFORM 1350-STATUS-TEST  THRU  1350-STATUS-EXIT
        ELSE
             MOVE 1300 TO REJCDE.
1300-RECORD-EXIT.
        EXIT.

1350-STATUS-TEST                                    SECTION.

        IF  SCREEN-STATUS = 'FMC' OR 'NMC' OR 'PMC'
              NEXT SENTENCE
        ELSE
               MOVE  1350  TO  REJCDE.
1350-STATUS-EXIT.
        EXIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T14:35:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8d6ut$koi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com> <892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com> <10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com> <LIhqc.18246$hH.453847@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On 18-May-2004, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

> Why would anyone intentionally add a paragraph between the start
> and end points of another paragraph?  One of the main purposes
> of numbered paragraphs is to avoid such incompetence.

Some people don't like numbering paragraphs.

Some people use PERFORM THRU without obvious exit paragraphs.

What is easy is to not notice that the paragraph you're adding is after a
SECTION, and you forget to make your new performed routine a SECTION.

And some people just aren't used to working with spaghetti code and PERFORM
THRU's.


> There is no need for a change to the language.
> Nor is there anything risky about the THRU clause.
> There is also no need for GO TO as an exit strategy.

I have had to fix code that was incorrect because of miss-use of the THRU
clause.   Following structured methods would have avoided those errors.


> I would structure the code to minimize the amount of time required
> for someone new to pick up the program and decipher the logic.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>         EXIT.

OK.   So what makes this easier to decipher than having END-IF, no PERFORM THRU
(or PERFORM section) and EXIT PARAGRAPH?

I have always been bothered by PERFORM SECTION-NAME thru PARAGRAPH-EXIT.   It
seems confusing to me as what you're really saying is:

PERFORM 1250-SCREEN-EDIT and 1250-SCREEN-EXIT thru 1250-SCREEN-EXIT.

Compilers don't choke on this, but it is quite illogical.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T13:35:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RRrqc.19642$qJ5.501996@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<LIhqc.18246$hH.453847@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com> <892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com> <10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com> <LIhqc.18246$hH.453847@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:
> 
> Why would anyone intentionally add a paragraph between the start
> and end points of another paragraph?  One of the main purposes
> of numbered paragraphs is to avoid such incompetence.
> 

I have often broken an excessively large routine down into two or more 
separate routines.  While that is not quite the same thing as adding "a 
paragraph between the start and end points of another paragraph", the 
same principal applies.

In fact, I would consider it an intregal part of "top-down" methodology.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T14:01:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8fpbf$if4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com> <892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com> <10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com> <LIhqc.18246$hH.453847@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <RRrqc.19642$qJ5.501996@news20.bellglobal.com> <Flwqc.1986$fF3.39371@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On 18-May-2004, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

> The question should have been
> "Why would anyone intentionally add a paragraph between
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to execute the added paragraph, which was not the OP's intent.

Carelessness.

Someone is used to single paragraph performs, and in the middle of the night has
to debug a complex IF paragraph.   The simplest way to do this is to pull out
the conditional code and put it in a paragraph of its own to be performed.

So now the code is:
    PERFORM PARAGRAPH-1 thru OTHER-PARAGRAPH-4.
    ...
PARAGRAPH-1.
PARAGRAPH-2.	
...

   IF COMPLEX-CONDITON
        PERFORM MIDNIGHT-FIX
   END-IF.
    more code

MIDNIGHT-FIX.
**   didn't notice that this code is part of a long PERFORM-THRU or a SECTION.
    conditional code.

OTHER-PARAGRAPH-2.
OTHER-PARAGRAPH-3.
OTHER-PARAGRAPH-4.
    EXIT.
```

- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com> <892qc.3197$325.407652@news20.bellglobal.com> <10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com> <LIhqc.18246$hH.453847@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <RRrqc.19642$qJ5.501996@news20.bellglobal.com> <9lAqc.528889$oR5.416783@pd7tw3no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Donald Tees wrote:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy


Yes.  Although the entire concept of structuring code as an OOP module 
rather than a simple executable can also be applied to the call 
argument.  Rather than performing paragraphs or sections, or doing 
in-line nested programs, it seems to me that "invoke "self" has the 
advantages of isolation, the ability to pass arguements, the ability to 
have local storage at the module level, *AND* the ability to use recursion.

Donald
```

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:

> LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message news:10aindg1ggu4v2c@corp.supernews.com...
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I do that.

Okay - that makes 1 regular and 1 former regular...  :)

>>Now, this is pretty straightforward, and has a low risk of being botched
>>during maintenance.  But, you've still got that THRU sitting up there,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> of numbered paragraphs is to avoid such incompetence.

Maybe due to lack of proper analysis - maybe a lack of training (we do 
get complete programming neophytes) - maybe carelessness - maybe an 
honest mistake.  If we were all trained on sections, THRU, and their 
implications, it wouldn't be as big a deal.  However, that's not how 
COBOL is being taught these days.

>>This seems to me to be a worthy upgrade of the language, and parallels
>>the behavior of EXIT PROGRAM provided for subprograms (both nested and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> There is no need for a change to the language.

It's not a change - it's in the 2002 standard.  :)  (Just need to 
convince the vendor to adopt it...)

> Nor is there anything risky about the THRU clause.

That's up for debate.  :)  I understand that you probably have never (in 
recent memory, at least) had a problem with a THRU biting you.  However, 
that hasn't been my experience.

> There is also no need for GO TO as an exit strategy.

I agree - I'd like more of a "get out early" strategy.

> I would structure the code to minimize the amount of time required
> for someone new to pick up the program and decipher the logic.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>         EXIT.

What is the purpose of the "THRU EXIT"?  I thought performing a section 
was sufficient.  (Like I said, I've never been formally trained on 
sections, I just know from what I've read and examples I've seen.)

What I've taken to doing is similar to the above - structuring one 
paragraph for each field edit.  Then, if a screen has a (L)oad, 
(C)hange, and (D)elete options, I perform each paragraph in the 
[option]-driver.  I don't know that I would change that even if we had 
EXIT PARAGRAPH available.  I do know that I have encountered a couple of 
occasions here recently when I would have liked to use it.

Thanks for your response...  :)
```

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:10ag9mg1r7bk83e@corp.supernews.com...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> It depends on how you look at it.  To me, this is an argument for "EXIT
> PARAGRAPH" as a valuable addition to the COBOL language.

Ummm ...  you might want to double-check Format 6 of the EXIT statement in
ISO/IEC 1989:2002 (Page 446).   Compilers conformant to the '85 and prior
standards might not understand the construct, but EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT
SECTION are both in the *language* as currently defined.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T01:20:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405170020.56807d98@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> When an EXIT PARAGRAPH is executed control is passed back to the invoking 
> PERFORM.

Actually no.  An EXIT PARAGRAPH transfers control to a position at the
end of the current paragraph.  In the case of a PERFORM THRU or
PERFORM section this may, or may not, result in the next transfer of
control being back to the invoking PERFORM.

That is: the EXIT PARAGRAPH has a wider repertoire than your rather
restricted view of it.

Claiming that 'EXIT PARAGRAPH passes control back to the PERFORM' is
the same error that has 'NEXT SENTENCE transferring control to after
the END-IF'.  It does do this only in very restricted cases.
 
> The fact of the NEXT SENTENCE's function under these conditions is that of 
> bypassing execution of subsequent code in the paragraph.

The problem being that of maintaining those specific and critical
conditions in every future scenario, such as the program being given
to a maintainer at 2am who is unaware of this requirement.

> Under these conditions, then, a NEXT SENTENCE is a de facto EXIT 
> PARAGRAPH.

Are you actually advocating its use as such ?
 
> This seems, then, to be an argument against the 'one period/full stop per 
> pargraph' style.

No, it is an argument against doing anything that _relies_ on there
being only one full stop per paragraph, unless of course you have some
mechanism that can ensure this.

The number of full stops in a paragraph at zero scope level, beyond
those neccessary for syntax, is a style issue.  Abusing NEXT SENTENCE
in a completely contrived way makes it a critical logic issue.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T07:44:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8a8ij$rpd$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405170020.56807d98@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0405170020.56807d98@posting.google.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Actually no.

In the example given - perhaps I might have been clearer in repeating the
qualification 'When a paragraph is controlled via a PERFORM, Mr Plinston -
as it might well be when fall-through logic is avoided - ' which seems to 
have gotten lost in your uncommented editing - actually yes.

[snip]

>
>Claiming that 'EXIT PARAGRAPH passes control back to the PERFORM' is
>the same error that has 'NEXT SENTENCE transferring control to after
>the END-IF'.  It does do this only in very restricted cases.

Which were qualified in my example, Mr Plinston... again, my apologies for 
neglecting the explicit repetition which some might require for clarity.

> 
>> The fact of the NEXT SENTENCE's function under these conditions is that of 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a maintainer at 2am who is unaware of this requirement.

Mr Plinston, to the best of my knowledge there are no constructs which 
cover 'every future scenario'; to expect such may lead to disappointment.

>
>> Under these conditions, then, a NEXT SENTENCE is a de facto EXIT 
>> PARAGRAPH.
>
>Are you actually advocating its use as such ?

Mr Plinston, to the best of my knowledge observing 'de facto' equivalence 
is neither adocating nor disparaging, it is an acknowledgement of 'made' 
(factum, from faciere) thing.

> 
>> This seems, then, to be an argument against the 'one period/full stop per 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>mechanism that can ensure this.

Enforcement of site standards is up to the site administrator, Mr 
Plinston; a logical extension of your condition 'unless of course you have 
some mechanism that can ensure this' would be to forbid a programmer use 
of SOURCE-COMPUTER WITH DEBUGGING MODE' unless you have some mechanism 
which can ensure this does not wind up in Production.

(side note - I have said to folks whose excessive eagerness to implement a 
fix has resulted in going into my personal libraries, grabbing my version 
of the code before I have said it is ready, slamming it into Prod and 
trying to call me on the carpet for the error 'The code clearly states 
that it is to be used with DEBUGGING MODE; I cannot be held reasonably 
responsible for someone else's decision to slam un-DEBUG'd code into 
Prod.')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T01:21:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405170021.448db85f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <217e491a.0405151136.61d97cb1@posting.google.com> <c86h2a$308$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0405161240.631d41d6@posting.google.com> <c895dg$eo6$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> When an EXIT PARAGRAPH is executed control is passed back to the invoking 
> PERFORM.

Actually no.  An EXIT PARAGRAPH transfers control to a position at the
end of the current paragraph.  In the case of a PERFORM THRU or
PERFORM section this may, or may not, result in the next transfer of
control being back to the invoking PERFORM.

That is: the EXIT PARAGRAPH has a wider repertoire than your rather
restricted view of it.

Claiming that 'EXIT PARAGRAPH passes control back to the PERFORM' is
the same error that has 'NEXT SENTENCE transferring control to after
the END-IF'.  It does do this only in very restricted cases.
 
> The fact of the NEXT SENTENCE's function under these conditions is that of 
> bypassing execution of subsequent code in the paragraph.

The problem being that of maintaining those specific and critical
conditions in every future scenario, such as the program being given
to a maintainer at 2am who is unaware of this requirement.

> Under these conditions, then, a NEXT SENTENCE is a de facto EXIT 
> PARAGRAPH.

Are you actually advocating its use as such ?
 
> This seems, then, to be an argument against the 'one period/full stop per 
> pargraph' style.

No, it is an argument against doing anything that _relies_ on there
being only one full stop per paragraph, unless of course you have some
mechanism that can ensure this.

The number of full stops in a paragraph at zero scope level, beyond
those neccessary for syntax, is a style issue.  Abusing NEXT SENTENCE
in a completely contrived way makes it a critical logic issue.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-15T16:59:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ad4hhq68jdv01@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c8534n$lah$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <c82eb3$ali$1@panix5.panix.com> <4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com> <c8534n$lah$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com>,
> JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> opinions.

It can be supported by my compiler...

if mySwitchIsSet
     perform additionalSwitchTests
     if mySwitchIsSet
         next sentence
     else
         display "Switch cleared by additional tests"
     end-if
end-if

My compiler will not accept this code snippet (in proper context, of 
course).  And paragraph 3.6.16 of the compiler's manual, syntax rule 3, 
states "If the END-IF phrase is specified, the NEXT SENTENCE phrase must 
not be specified."

I agree with JerryMouse, if for no other reason than personal 
experience.  CONTINUE always goes to the scope delimiter (I know it 
doesn't really "Go" anywhere, but that's the effect), and I've found it 
an acceptable replacement for NEXT SENTENCE.

I really like the concept of NEXT SENTENCE working as a de facto EXIT 
PARAGRAPH statement - it's a shame our compiler doesn't interpret it 
that way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nUwpc.1758$SZ4.856@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
So, I'll bet your compiler WILL accept the following (whether it is "good or
bad" is another question)

 if mySwitchIsSet
      perform additionalSwitchTests
      if mySwitchIsSet
          If mySwtichIsSet
              next sentence
          else
               continue
      else
          display "Switch cleared by additional tests"
      end-if
 end-if
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com>`

```
On Fri, 14 May 2004 06:53:04 -0500, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
enlightened us:

>Hugh Candlin wrote:
>> The coding style that I use was adopted to help avoid such logic
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>

PLEASE!!  What Cobol compiler does such a thing?  For sure every Cobol
compiler I've ever worked on in the IBM mainframe world treats NEXT
SENTENCE the same way, i.e., it transfers control to the first command
after a period (full stop) or delimiter (END-IF).  CONTINUE, on the
other hand, transfers control to the next STATEMENT which may or may
not be preceded by a period (full stop).

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


I saved $300 in taxes this year but am paying
$300 more for gas. Bush giveth and his oil buddies
taketh it away.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T14:01:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c83c30$1gi2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote in message
news:2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com...

> >In other words, NEXT SENTENCE does NOT always act as an appendage for
ELSE
> >and may transfer control to nether regions of your code. Better is
CONTINUE.
> >
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> after a period (full stop) or delimiter (END-IF).

Then every compiler you worked on in the IBM mainframe world was
non-compliant with the standards.   There are three sorts of sentences
defined in the '85 standard, and each of them is always terminated by a
separator period.  NEXT SENTENCE is defined in the syntax for IF and SEARCH
only, and in both cases, its execution causes control to pass to the next
executable *sentence*, not the *statement* following the scope delimiter for
the current IF or SEARCH (if they're not the same).  The '85 standard is
clear on that point, but apparently some users (and perhaps some
implementations) weren't.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T16:23:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405141523.41127327@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote 

> PLEASE!!  What Cobol compiler does such a thing?  For sure every Cobol
> compiler I've ever worked on in the IBM mainframe world treats NEXT
> SENTENCE the same way, i.e., it transfers control to the first command
> after a period (full stop) or delimiter (END-IF).  

NO. WRONG.  It obviously does not work as _you_ expect.

It should work, and almost certainly does work, by transferring
control to the statement following the next full stop.

However, having said that, there are some compilers that have an
obscure option that "Changes the behaviour of the NEXT SENTENCE
statement".  You may also note that in ANS'85 NEXT SENTENCE is not a
statement at all, but is a clause of some formats of the IF and SEARCH
statements.

Combinations of NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF are not allowed by ANS'85
(argue about contrived ways to avoid the intent of the standard if you
dare) but if you do use this as a compiler extension, then it
_does_not_ transfer control to after this, but to some later arbitrary
point where there is a full stop.

> CONTINUE, on the
> other hand, transfers control to the next STATEMENT which may or may
> not be preceded by a period (full stop).

CONTINUE does _NOT_ 'transfer control to' anywhere at all.  CONTINUE
does absolutely nothing, it is a null op and just a place holder where
the syntax requires a statement.  This usually results execution
continuing at the next place that it correctly should do.

For example:

      PERFORM VARYING .. UNTIL ...
          CONTINUE
      END-PERFORM
      .

Does _NOT_ 'transfer control to the next statement ...', it continues
the PERFORM .. UNTIL.
```

```
in them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T23:33:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZGcpc.782$SZ4.689@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote in message
news:2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 14 May 2004 06:53:04 -0500, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
> enlightened us:
<snip>
> PLEASE!!  What Cobol compiler does such a thing?  For sure every Cobol
> compiler I've ever worked on in the IBM mainframe world treats NEXT
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"you is wrong"

There is *NO* IBM compiler (that I know of - and I know lots of them) that
transfers control to a statement following an END-IF *unless* there is a period
after that END-IF.

FYI - IBM *does* have a documented extension allowing NEXT SENTENCE followed by
an END-IF - but id most certainly does NOT violate the ANSI/ISO rules and
transfer control to the END-IF - but rather to the statement (logically)
following the next period.
```

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>

- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com> <c8412o$eg9$1@news.eusc.inter.net>`

```
On Sat, 15 May 2004 00:00:16 -0300, "Clark F. Morris, Jr."
<cfmtech@istar.ca> enlightened us:

>SkippyPB wrote:
>
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>> 


Based on the responses here, maybe I wasn't understood or maybe I
don't understand.  Either way, my point is (using an example this
time), if I have the following IBM mainframe Cobol code:

If fldA = fldB
    next sentence
else
    move fldC to fldB.

Compute fldE = fldB + fldG.

As I've always understood Cobol, when the IF statement is true, next
sentece will send execution directly to the Compute statment.

If I had the following erroneous code:

If fldA = fldB
    next sentence
else
    move fldC to fldB

Compute fldE = fldB + fldG.

Move fldE to display-fldE.

When the IF statement is true, next sentence will send execution
directly to the Move statement.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


I saved $300 in taxes this year but am paying
$300 more for gas. Bush giveth and his oil buddies
taketh it away.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-16T14:08:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405161308.2477c4e0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com> <c8412o$eg9$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <n51fa0hbnetge23tgt7n7lei060heo2gnf@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote 

> Based on the responses here, maybe I wasn't understood or maybe I
> don't understand.  Either way, my point is (using an example this
> time), if I have the following IBM mainframe Cobol code:

When code is written using ANS'74 standards (as your examples are)
then NEXT SENTENCE works unsurprisingly.  The IF scope terminates at
the full stop which means that the next sentence starts at that same
point.

The issue is with the introduction of ANS'85 scope terminators the IF
can be terminated without using a full stop:

     IF ( something )
         PERFORM A
     ELSE
         PERFORM B
     END-IF
     PERFORM C
     .

It is quite clear that it will do A or B and then always do C.  If you
changed 'PERFORM A' to 'CONTINUE' then it will still always do C. Now
change 'PERFORM A' to 'NEXT SENTENCE' and work out the result.
```

```
before the COMPUTE and you will have different results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T16:27:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oPudnVT77fCqszTdRVn-hg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com> <c8412o$eg9$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <n51fa0hbnetge23tgt7n7lei060heo2gnf@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
>
> I saved $300 in taxes this year but am paying
> $300 more for gas. Bush giveth and his oil buddies
> taketh it away.

You're half correct; you got the tax cut thanks to Bush.

Oil prices are going up, in large measure, to the tree-huggers. Here's a
couple of reasons:

1. Although Saudi Arabia has opened the tap, we can't use much more of SA
crude because of its high sulpher content.

2. No new refinery has been built in the US in 17 years.

3. World-wide demand has increased dramatically, especially in China and
India, plus increases in Japan and the U.S.

4. Several years ago, the EPA mandated oxygenates to be added to gasoline.
MBTE was the preferred oxygen-enriching substance, but that is now outlawed.
The only remaining oxygen-enricher is ethanol which is WAY more expensive
than MBTE. The interesting thing is that, as more and more cars use fuel
injection, the need for oxygen-supplementing compounds is lessened.

Increased gasoline prices, however, are but one consequence of an improving
global economy. On balance, an improved economy trumphs the price of
gasoline.

As an aside, if we really went to war over oil (not that there's anything
wrong with that), why didn't we invade Venezula? It's closer and the food's
better.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: price of gasoline (was Avoiding Logic Error)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T01:44:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a9539c.25238829@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com> <c8412o$eg9$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <n51fa0hbnetge23tgt7n7lei060heo2gnf@4ax.com> <oPudnVT77fCqszTdRVn-hg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>Oil prices are going up, in large measure, to the tree-huggers. Here's a
>couple of reasons:
>
>1. Although Saudi Arabia has opened the tap, we can't use much more of SA
>crude because of its high sulpher content.

Sulphur can be removed in the refinery, for a cost. It's an economic issue.

When diesel went low-sulphur, eight years ago, it caused widespread O-ring
failure in fuel injectors, causing expensive rebuilds. Fuel injectors in upscale
cars such as BMW cost up to $300 per, and there are six of them. You don't hear
much about low-sulphur gasoline because yuppies have more political klout than
truck drivers. O-rings have since been changed but we must wait for older cars
to leave the system.

>2. No new refinery has been built in the US in 17 years.

It's worse than that. Between 1977 and 2002, the number of refineries operating
in the US fell from 282 to 153. There are 129 refineries sitting idle. They can
be purchased for 20% of the cost of building new, so why would anyone want to
build a new one? 

To understand why that's so, you have to understand the Oil Depletion Allowance
and how it influenced oil company accounting. Big oil companies have four major
business units -- Exploration and Production, called Upstream, and Refining,
Transportation and Marketing, called collectively Downstream. (I'm ignoring
Chemicals here.) The Oil Depletion Allowance used to allow them to deduct 15% of
upstream *gross profit* from corporate income tax, which is based on *net
profit*. The four divisions 'sell' product to each other for intracompany prices
that are easily manipulated to move profit from one division to another. The Oil
Depletion Allowance gave them a strong incentive to move as much as possible
upstream. To see this, look at any oil company's P&L. For instance, Exxon Mobil
in the first quarter of 2004 reported (in millions)::

US upstream *net* income     $ 1,154  75%    
US downsteam *net* income        392  25%      (up from 174 same quarter 2003)
Total US income                      1,546

Prices are artifically set so that refineries and transportation lose money,
retail operations make a little, most profit is in production and exploration.
On $1,154 *net* profit, the *gross* profit is at least double. Thus corporate
income tax would be (1546 * .35) - (2500 * .15) = 166 or 11%. 
If refineries lose money, why build new ones? Better to shut them down (129
above) or sell them to Valero for 20% of new (they're fully depreciated).

In 1975, the Depletion Allowance was limited to small producers who do not
operate a refinery (I used to be one). That was intended to stop the 'tax break
for the rich.' In its place, Big Oil got the 'intangible drilling cost
deduction', which produced an even bigger deduction by letting them deduct 70%
of the cost of drilling a well in the first year, and the rest over five years.
They also got the 'enhanced oil recovery credit', which pays them for operating
stripper wells (marginal, almost played out) and for pumping out-of-spec oil
(high sulphur, high viscosity). Something for everyone -- new wells and old.
Profits were still moved upstream; refineries still lose money.

>3. World-wide demand has increased dramatically, especially in China and
>India, plus increases in Japan and the U.S.

True.

>4. Several years ago, the EPA mandated oxygenates to be added to gasoline.
>MBTE was the preferred oxygen-enriching substance, but that is now outlawed.
>The only remaining oxygen-enricher is ethanol which is WAY more expensive
>than MBTE. The interesting thing is that, as more and more cars use fuel
>injection, the need for oxygen-supplementing compounds is lessened.

Only three States have banned MBTE -- California, New York and Connecticut.

Another reason for high gasoline prices, that you did not mention, is recent
consolidation of Big Oil -- Conoco Phillips, Exxon Mobil, Chevron Texaco, BP
ARCO, etc. -- and increasing monopolization by pushing independent retailers
out. Between 1993 and 2003, retail share of the ten biggest oil companies
increased from 56 to 79 percent. On the East and West Coasts, the four largest
control 76 and 66 percent, respectively. It would be higher on the East coast if
Texaco Chevron or Shell owned stations flying their flag. They are owned by a
Saudi company.

The first quarter Exxon Mobil cited above is their highest quarterly profit in
13 years. Chevron Texaco is up 33 percent over the first quarter last year.

They got independents out by pushing through a law requiring underground steel
tanks be replaced by plastic in 1998. Replacement had to be done by a small
number of 'certified' contractors, who were fully booked by the majors a year
before the deadline. In smaller stations, those pumping less than 10K gal/mo,
the cost could not be economically justified. Steel tanks were in older
stations, which were largely independents.

What all these have in common is MONEY, not tree-huggers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T10:05:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6r4ka0do7qgm5o8himlvp57fdtqju60m0l@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <2lr9a092neoeo5tostdfl1o1edq6gvmhp3@4ax.com> <c8412o$eg9$1@news.eusc.inter.net> <n51fa0hbnetge23tgt7n7lei060heo2gnf@4ax.com> <oPudnVT77fCqszTdRVn-hg@giganews.com>`

```
On Mon, 17 May 2004 16:27:25 -0500, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
enlightened us:

>SkippyPB wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The sig line was a joke.  Actually I had to pay taxes this year.
Bush's tax cut did not help this household at all.

>Oil prices are going up, in large measure, to the tree-huggers. Here's a
>couple of reasons:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OPEC has cut back production.  The price per barrel of crude oil makes
up around 50% of the cost of gasoline.  The US has never imported oil
from Saudi to refine for gas due to its high sulphur content, so it
matters not what Saudi does.

>2. No new refinery has been built in the US in 17 years.
>

That is a function of the oil companies, not the government.  When oil
was in short supply in the 80's, new refineries were built.  Once the
oil prices came back down, the oil companies could not make enough
money to offset their investment in the new assets.  Hence the move to
not build any more refineries was a corporate profits one rather than
a government or supply issue.

>3. World-wide demand has increased dramatically, especially in China and
>India, plus increases in Japan and the U.S.
>

Yes thanks to the car manufacturers wanting more profits and
exploiting a loop hole in EPA standards that have a lower emissions
and mileage standard for vehicles classed as light trucks.  The
manufacturers built SUVs and mini-Vans which are classed as light
trucks and so don't have to meet the same clean air standards as
passenger cars nor do they have to meet the same MPG standards and
they have a higher profit margin than anything else they make.  The
manufacturers created the market through aggressive advertising and
the gullible public bought it.  Now, with gas prices soaring, sales of
SUV's for the 1st quarter have dropped 15%.  People don't like paying
over $30 to fill up their tanks and then getting less than 20 miles
per gallon.  Those wonderful Hummers are likely to get 9 miles per
gallon.

>4. Several years ago, the EPA mandated oxygenates to be added to gasoline.
>MBTE was the preferred oxygen-enriching substance, but that is now outlawed.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That would be MTBE -- Methyl Tertiary-Butyl Ether.  The only reason it
and RFG were required by the 1990 Clean Air Act to be added to
gasoline was to reduce air pollution.  From the EPA's web site is the
following:  " MTBE is used in gasoline throughout the United States to
reduce carbon monoxide and ozone levels caused by auto emissions. MTBE
replaces the use of lead as an octane enhancer since 1979."

MTBE is being phased out because it has been discovered to be a water
contaminant.

>Increased gasoline prices, however, are but one consequence of an improving
>global economy. On balance, an improved economy trumphs the price of
>gasoline.
>

Only if you have a job that pays enough so that you can afford to pay
the higher price.  What you ignore is the fact that higher gasoline
prices trickle into the cost of nearly everything else due to
transportation costs.  No improved worldwide anything will turmp that.

>As an aside, if we really went to war over oil (not that there's anything
>wrong with that), why didn't we invade Venezula? It's closer and the food's
>better.
>

Too much cocaine...too much temptation for the weekend warriors.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"A mother takes twenty years to make a man of her boy and
another woman makes a fool of him in twenty minutes."
 -- Robert Frost
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> On Mon, 17 May 2004 16:27:25 -0500, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
> enlightened us:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bush's tax cut did not help this household at all.

Okay, I'll put you down as indifferent to the tax cuts.

>
>> Oil prices are going up, in large measure, to the tree-huggers.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> matters not what Saudi does.

It does matter. Low sulpher oil that heretofore went into heating oil,
plastics, lubricants, etc., can be diverted to gasoline if Saudi oil takes
up the slack for those products.

>
>> 2. No new refinery has been built in the US in 17 years.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> a government or supply issue.

Who said anything about the government? I said tree-huggers.

>
>> 3. World-wide demand has increased dramatically, especially in China
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> gallon.

It wasn't a loop-hole. It was a deliberate decision to not drive up the cost
of business. This happens EVERY time the government imbeds social or
economic goals in the tax code.

>
>> 4. Several years ago, the EPA mandated oxygenates to be added to
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> contaminant.

That's only if it GETS in the water. Wouldn't it be better to concentrate on
leakage containment? How about just not mandating MTBE or ethanol or
anything and simply say "Your limits for NO2 NO CO, etc. are such-and-such
and we don't care how you get there?"

>
>> Increased gasoline prices, however, are but one consequence of an
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> transportation costs.  No improved worldwide anything will turmp that.

Let's say you drive 15,000 miles/year at 20mpg that's 750 gallons of gas.
Suppose gas goes up by $1.00/gallon, or $750/year. That's $14.42 a week
increase. One movie and popcorn. Cut your miles by 1/3 and the amount you
spend in a year for gasoline actually goes down.

Who doesn't have a job? No one I know.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T21:15:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Tue, 18 May 2004 17:20:15 -0500, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
enlightened us:

>SkippyPB wrote:
>> On Mon, 17 May 2004 16:27:25 -0500, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am not indifferent to tax cuts.  The cuts were helpful to some but
not the majority and, in fact, they caused the federal deficit to go
higher which will eventually offset any benefit those ill advised tax
cuts provided.

>>
>>> Oil prices are going up, in large measure, to the tree-huggers.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Matters little.  We buy our oil primarily from OPEC and when they cut
production, every country in OPEC cuts including Saudi Arabia,
Venezuela et. al.


>>
>>> 2. No new refinery has been built in the US in 17 years.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And it wasn't tree-huggers that had anything to do with no new
refineries being built either.  It was purely a corporate decision to
maximize profits.  Check out the major oil companies and the
percentage of profits they are reporting despite the higher priced
crude.

>>
>>> 3. World-wide demand has increased dramatically, especially in China
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No it was a loop hole.  It has never been closed because the auto
industry lobby is one of the strongest there is.  The auto industry
CEOs lobby the republicans and the labor unions lobby the democrats
and neither group wants to see light trucks come under the same EPA
standards as cars.  There is little evidence available that says
making cars and trucks fuel efficient less polluting drives up the
cost of business.  And even if it did, it gets passed on to the
consumer anyway.

>>
>>> 4. Several years ago, the EPA mandated oxygenates to be added to
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is way too much about how MTBE gets into the water supply than I
want to go into here.  Suffice it say that there are a myriad of ways
it gets there.

>>
>>> Increased gasoline prices, however, are but one consequence of an
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you are promoting conservation, BRAVO!  That is the single most
effective way to cut the costs of driving and get OPEC to lower the
cost of crude.  Supply and Demand.  Lessen demand, increase supply,
costs go down.  Econ 101.

>Who doesn't have a job? No one I know.
>

Seeing as how the unemployment rate is approximately 5.6% in the U.S.,
there are millions of people who either don't have work or are
underpaid doing to now having to take on lesser paying part time jobs.
That you don't know anyone without a job is of little consequence to
those who don't and to those who have given up looking and aren't
counted in the unemployment figures.

>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"A mother takes twenty years to make a man of her boy and
another woman makes a fool of him in twenty minutes."
 -- Robert Frost
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
In article <oPudnVT77fCqszTdRVn-hg@giganews.com>,
 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> SkippyPB wrote:
> >
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I said I was going to stay out of this one...both perform-thru AND oil.  
But I just have to:

   YOU FORGOT TAXES

In GA we have the lowest (or close to the lowest) gas tax in the country 
-- $0.12/gallon.  And gas is not all that different in actual price from 
what I remember in the early/mid 1980's.  The real pre-state tax cost of 
gas here is around $1.50 - $1.70, depending on your prefered flavor.

When you also consider the federal $0.18 (or is it $0.23...brain cramp) 
tax you get a real gas price that is somewhere between $1.25 and $1.45 
per gallon -- a completely reasonable rate.

The single largest factor in the variance from the cheap georgia gas 
cited in my example is the state and local tax on the product.

Yet we hever hear anyone complain about their local politicos when 
talking about the pump.

Oh, well, as someone once said -- "Democracy is a system where people 
get what they ask for, usually good and hard..."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T14:12:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c82k3q$gts$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <iMZoc.55273$Ut1.1487683@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On 14-May-2004, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

> Using that style, your example would be coded as
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>               NEXT SENTENCE.

You can use this same concept with CONTINUE and END-IF.   And the code will be
more efficient as CONTINUE is a NOP, while NEXT SENTENCE is a GO TO.

And periods will be optional.
```

#### ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com>,
William Bub <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote:
>I just made the same coding error I made about two years age. I coded
>something like the following:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>    .

First, it might be helpful to pay *scrupulous* attention to lining up 
conditionals and their scope-delimiters; indenting that second END-IF by 
its appropriate two spaces might make the error more obvious.

Second, you might try alternating IFs with EVALUATEs... in the example 
above it seems trivial but when more conditions are involved it may be 
helpful.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

On 13-May-2004, "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote:

> Every time REC-TYPE is not 1, MORESTUFF is not executed, even if EDIT-ERR is
> not Y. Of course, I now see that I need a second END-IF after PERFORM
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> error more difficult to make? Note: this is IBM mainframe.

One controversial coding style is to include full stop periods.

Another coding style is to use more obvious indentations.

Another coding style is to put if logic within PERFORMs.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T10:35:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>

It would seem to me that making periods illegal as part of a standard is 
far more controversial than including them,  I'll bet you cannot find a 
single cobol program in use that does not have at least one full stop 
period.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T15:51:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c82ptn$lfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c82jvi$gl2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <nR4pc.78679$FH5.1768576@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On 14-May-2004, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> > One controversial coding style is to include full stop periods.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> period.

There has been at least one long discussion in this group in which I was in
favor of using full stop periods, and someone else wanted to get rid of them,
except where mandated (at the end of paragraphs).    That led me to include
"controversial" in my recommendation - so we can avoid another style war right
now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T22:36:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40a54911.320020328@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c82jvi$gl2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <nR4pc.78679$FH5.1768576@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>It would seem to me that making periods illegal as part of a standard is 
>far more controversial than including them,  I'll bet you cannot find a 
>single cobol program in use that does not have at least one full stop 
>period.

That's easy. 

--- begin complete Cobol program ---
procedure division.
    display 'Hello world'
--- end complete Cobol program ---

Granted, it has a period after the division header. When you said "making
periods illegal", I assume you meant 'in the procedure division'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T23:29:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lDcpc.776$SZ4.359@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c82jvi$gl2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <nR4pc.78679$FH5.1768576@news20.bellglobal.com> <40a54911.320020328@news.optonline.net>`

```
Although some compilers accept that program, it is NOT standard and most that do
accept it issue warning messages.

In "Standard" (current, past, and next) a period is REQUIRED at the end of each
procedure division sentence.  And there is no "legal" way to code a procedure
division without sentences.

FYI - the following *is* a legal '85 Standard program (that actually
accomplishes something - sort-of)

 Identification Division.
    Program-ID.   Allocate-EXTERNAL.
Data Division.
 Working-Storage Section.
01   Ext-Data External  Pic X(100).

The  Procedure Division is *optional* in the '85 Standard - and calling a
program like that above will "allocate" the external data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-14T20:30:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405141930.441aa43e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <c82jvi$gl2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <nR4pc.78679$FH5.1768576@news20.bellglobal.com> <40a54911.320020328@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >It would seem to me that making periods illegal as part of a standard is 
> >far more controversial than including them,  I'll bet you cannot find a 
> >single cobol program in use that does not have at least one full stop 
                        ^^^^^^
> >period.
 
> --- begin complete Cobol program ---
> procedure division.
>     display 'Hello world'
> --- end complete Cobol program ---

This fails the 'in use' clause.  It also fails to be a 'Cobol program'
for a number of compilers, relying as it does on a several vendor
specific extensions.
 
> Granted, it has a period after the division header.

I am glad that you noticed this failure.

> When you said "making
> periods illegal", I assume you meant 'in the procedure division'.

Not only do you change what you meant, you also attempt to change what
other people meant too.

And my references have 'PROCEDURE DIVISION.' as being part of 'the
procedure division'.
```
