- **In-Reply-To:** `<c8ts5a$hqa$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<RhMpc.41916$TT.33207@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <RoPpc.2485$H_3.1791@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <RL_pc.34556$2f6.1267628@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <HH0qc.4344$SZ4.3045@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <hd2qc.34771$2f6.1279414@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bc2d123b6ab508e7062e367c8269dc5b@news.teranews.com> <AA4qc.625907$Po1.557782@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <cfaeadf3.0405172152.5f351c34@posting.google.com> <Lqiqc.629845$Po1.137460@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <c8meo0$h2a$1@morgoth.sfu.ca> <c8ts5a$hqa$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```
Mike Cowlishaw wrote:
>>If you take the trouble to check, you will see that until now I have
>>not posted any messages to this thread.  When it first appeared, I
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I didn't ever call it a challenge.  That was David Frank.

> 
>>Here are some of my reasons for this statement:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I wasn't criticizing the use of elapsed real time, but only making sure that 
readers who had not read the specification understood the type of measurement 
involved.

> 
>>Now, if you take a program that implements this
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
The results over the net will also be random variables.

> 
>>Therefore, simple
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I do not see any mention of best of three in the specification.  I finally 
found it on the page where results are posted which I had not looked at the 
time I wrote my previous message.  As to repeatability, see the table below.

> 
>>Now the benchmark specification calls for a comparison of the time
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
What I meant by that was that the specification itself only says the following:

"Comparison of the relative timing of Input/Output and calculations can be 
made by having the inner steps 1-8 above be controlled by a compile-time or 
run-time switch which either carries out the eight steps of the calculation as 
described or prepares a four-character string to be written to the output file 
as step 9. (A runtime switch is more convenient, and its runtime cost will be 
neglible (sic).)"

Now this statement says nothing about how many runs of each type should be 
done or how the results should be combined to arrive at a comparison.

> 
>>and the postings I have
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
But the specification clearly says:

"This is expected to allow

    1. The comparison of Input/Output time with calculation time
    2. the investigation of different implementation approaches."

Now the real time in which the i/o gets done is indeed measured, but the 
calculation time is not.  Moreover there is no clear statement of how the 
results of the benchmark will be used, so it is impossible to say whether the 
measurement is accurate enough to be useful, or if the model is appropriate 
for the purpose.


> 
>>Any attempt to measure total computation time using similar
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
To start with, let's try to get a handle on how volatile the benchmark is as 
specified.  Since I am in the process of migrating my laptop from a Thinkpad 
600 to a Thinkpad T22, I currently have three systems available for testing:

TP 600:  Pentium II, 300MHz, 192MB RAM, 5GB IBM DADA 25120 UltraDMA2/PIO4

TP T22:  Pentium III, 900MHz, 384MB RAM, 20GB HITACHI_DK23CA-20B UltraDMA2/PIO4

Desktop: ASUS CUSL2-C Pentium III, 1GHz, 512MB RAM,
          40GB IC35L040AVER07-0 UltraDMA5/PIO4  (OS, DLL's, program)
          80GB SAMSUNG SP8004H UltraDMA5/PIO4 (Data)

All tests were run with the systems booted normally and connected to the lan, 
but with no other applications running.

I tested three versions of the benchmark written in Personal PL/I for OS/2:
   telcodb:  decimal big endian input, calculations in decimal
   telcobb:  binary big endian input, calculations in binary
   telcobl:  binary little endian input, calculations in binary.

All three versions produced the exact results published in the specification 
for both the ten record test case and the million record input.  The output 
text file has 10 character records of the form xxxxxxx.xx terminated by cr-lf. 
  The size of the output file is therefore 12000000 bytes.  The 12000000 byte 
output files from all three programs are byte for byte identical.  The 
i/o-only runs wrote the same number of bytes to the output file as the 
computation runs, deviating slightly from the specification as I can see no 
reason to write only 4 bytes when the specification calls for the real output 
to be a text file (i.e., cr-lf terminated lines) containing the total cost of 
the call represented using either ascii, ebcdic, or utf-8 characters for which 
four bytes is clearly inadequate.  Intermediate variables and the value t 
written to the output file have sufficient precision to deal with call 
durations up to 9999999 seconds (over 115 days).  The total variables have 
sufficient precision to accumulate the calls for 10**9 customers each making 
3000 long distance calls of twice the average 180 second duration in the 
billing period.

The table below shows the results of running each program 50 times doing io 
only and 50 times doing full computation on each machine.  The first line in 
each section, labeled M gives the maximum observed value for the total time, 
the i/o time and the difference between these two values.  This is to 
illustrate the behavior of extreme values.  The next three lines, T, I, and C 
are the total time, i/o time and  "computation" time, T-I, respectively; and 
the three numbers on each line are the mean value based on 50 observations, 
the standard deviation, and the standard error. The next two lines, F and L, 
give the difference between the total time and i/o time for the first and last 
runs.  Finally the line labeled B gives best of three differences for runs 2, 
3, and 4; 24, 25, and 26; and 47, 48,and 49 respectively.

telcodb:          600                    T22               Desktop

M 32.16   12.03 20.13  M 11.74   7.51  4.23   M 8.33   2.51  5.82
T 31.6154 .2407 .0340  T 11.0270 .3054 .0432  T 8.2564 .0270 .0038
I 11.2332 .4241 .0600  I 6.4052  .7365 .1041  I 2.3192 .0740 .0105
C 20.3822 .4876 .0690  C 4.6218  .7973 .1128  C 5.9372 .0788 .0111
F 20.74                F 3.5                  F 6.07
L 20.91                L 4.67                 L 5.89
B 20.63   21.28 20     B 3.91    5.35  5.16   B 6.07   5.98  5.98

telcobb:

M 15.22   11.97 3.25   M 11.71   7.59  4.12   M 3.75   2.72  1.13
T 14.3670 .2696 .0381  T 7.7394  .7405 .1047  T 3.5912 .0646 .0091
I 11.0618 .4245 .0600  I 6.9928  .5009 .0708  I 2.3476 .0749 .0105
C 3.3052  .5029 .0711  C .7466   .8440 .1264  C 1.2436 .0989 .0140
F 3.43                 F 1.36                 F 1.25
L 4.52                 L .29                  L 1.11
B 3.29    3.68  3.37   B 1.02    1.55  -.19   B 1.21   1.37  1.08

telcobl:

M 15.66   12.58 3.08   M 8.5     7.29  1.21   M 3.68   2.48  1.20
T 14.8584 .3664 .0518  T 7.5090  .3019 .0427  T 3.5708 .0596 .0084
I 11.9672 .2265 .0391  I 6.3526  .6099 .0863  I 2.3314 .0728 .0103
C 2.8912  .4590 .0649  C 1.1564  .6806 .0962  C 1.2394 .0941 .0133
F 2.11                 F 1.42                 F 1.09
L 2.7                  L 0.77                 L 1.33
B 3.12    3.04  2.58   B .88     1.35  1.81   B 1.25   1.26  1.23


Discussion:

The i/o done within the timed portion of each of the three programs is 
identical: eight bytes are read in and twelve bytes are written out in each 
iteration.  Accordingly, comparison of the three I lines in each column should 
give some insight into the stability of measurement of the i/o time based on 
50 replications.  There is excellent agreement in the Desktop column, but the 
other two are not so reassuring: the 99% confidence interval for the telcobb 
result does not overlap the 99% confidence interval of either of the other two 
estimates in the T22 column; similarly, in the 600 column, the 99% confidence 
interval for the telcobl result is disjoint from the other two.  Either more 
replications are needed or there is some systematic uncontrolled factor 
affecting these results.

Next let us consider the differences in performance of telcobb and telcobl. 
There is precisely one substantive difference between the .exe modules for 
these two programs: this is the insertion of the two byte instruction, bswap 
eax (0fc8), at relative address 5d0 to convert from big endian to little 
endian format.  This has the effect of shifting the next 59 bytes of the 
instruction stream forward by two bytes.  At that point there is an 
unconditional jump followed by alignment to a paragraph boundary, from which 
point the modules are identical.  The only other differences in the exe 
modules are trivial ones related to the difference in the names of the modules 
which could have no bearing on execution performance.  The important point is 
that the portions of the programs that are executed when the computations are 
being skipped are identical and fetched from the same locations.  Given this, 
it seems apparent that the expected execution time of telcobb and telcobl 
should be identical when the computations are skipped and the execution time 
of telcobb should slightly exceed that of telcobl when the computations are 
being performed.  Nevertheless, we see that for the T22, the io-only time for 
telcobb  seems to be significantly greater than that of telcobl at the 1% 
level, whereas for the 600 the reverse appears to be the case.  I can only 
conclude that 50 replications is an insufficient number to average out the 
effects of extraneous events within the system (even when it is essentially 
quiesced except for the program under test).  When we turn our attention to 
the T line, it is particularly disconcerting that for the 600, the total time 
for telcobb appears to be significantly less that that of telcobl at the 1% 
level.  (I can think of two possible scenarios that could conceivably but not 
very likely explain this anomaly, but I won't have time to check them out for 
a couple of days -- barring that, this result would indicate that more 
replications are needed to obtain stable results.)

As for the F and L lines, they look pretty erratic to me, but then samples of 
size one are known to be meaningless.  Similarly, the values on the B lines 
don't inspire me to put a lot a faith in the best of three.  The -.19 for 
telcobb on the T22 tells it all.  Sampling variability comes to the fore.

Now for the M lines.  As both you and Bill Turner pointed out, the critical 
aspect of a billing application is getting the bills out on time.  The time 
between data availability and mailing time (minus invoice printing and 
envelope stuffing time) is the window of opportunity for the program that 
computes the billing data.  Thus the maximum time the run is likely to take 
has considerable relevance.  It seems to me that from this perspective, the 
thing to concentrate on is a measure of the worst case total run time.  A real 
billing application would have to read in considerably more information (e.g., 
telephone number of caller, number called, date and time of call) and compute 
and output considerably more results.  Whether the benchmark results can be 
used to extrapolate to a realistic situation is hard to say.

I am not posting the source programs, since that would only be an occasion for 
David Frank to start a flame war.  However I will be pleased to send them to 
you by private email upon request.
```

```
In my algebra class, they told me that something like

   z = iz+1

has TWO variables in it ("z" and "iz") therefore, it would be necessary to have
another equation to solve for the 2 variables.  Either that or "iz" would
actually stand for "i" times "z" - in which case you would be solving an
equation for when the variable "z" was equal to "i times z plus 1". (Lack of
spacing around the "+" wouldn't have been accepted by any teacher that I had).
```

```
Most people don't "post" replies - they send them to the person who is
coordinating the challenge.

Yes, I have run my test agasint BOTH of the files (10 and million) that are
"easily" downloaded from the challenge site.  (Actually, I have used 3 of the 4
posted files.  I used the 10 record version for both packed-decimal and binary -
then stuck to the packed-decimal input).

I suppose that it a reasonable request to see all the results files.  I have now
placed 4 files for "viewing"

  http://home.netcom.com/~wmklein/dox/TELCO1.TXT
     uses the 10 record input file and does the calculations

http://home.netcom.com/~wmklein/dox/TELCO2.TXT
     uses the 10 record input file and does I/O (both files) but no calculations

http://home.netcom.com/~wmklein/dox/TELCO3.TXT
     uses the million record input file and does calculations  (most records
omitted in the file placed online.  I can provide the full file to anyone who is
interested)

http://home.netcom.com/~wmklein/dox/TELCO4.TXT
   same as above (but with no calculations)

Obviously, the benchmark requirements ask for two versions;
    One that does both input and output I/O *and* does calculations
    Another that does both input and output I/O but does not do the
calculations.

Mr Frank's note says that he has done:

 My do-looped results are:
  seconds = 2.4     (do loop #1 write suppressed)
  seconds = 17.8    (do loop #2 official benchmark time)

so it looks like he still hasn't actually produced results that match the
specifications (i.e. one with calculations and I/O and one without calculations
but with full I/O).  It is interesting (to me) that his "full" program (on the
large file) takes 17.8 seconds while the COBOL version takes 13.5 seconds.

Makes me wonder why he asked why people still use COBOL <G>.

P.S.  I did state in my original posting of the code that I (slightly) expanded
on the original specification by using comma separators for the thousands
indicator in the totals.  This could (easily) be removed, but I don't think that
COBOL programmers would usually create "hard-to-read" large numbers without
this - UNLESS specifically asked to.  I certainly don't "insist" that others
provide this same feature, but I do think any "real world" report would do so.

Similarly, I wrote out the "start and end times" with colon and period
separators.  My code would actually be "easier" without this, but again, I doubt
that any real world program would print times "unformatted".

In answer to the question,
    "what is the size of the file your program generates?"

The run with the large input (and doing the calculations) is:
   70,313 kb
```

```
As far as your reported,

> seconds = 2.14     <-  input file only, no calculations, no write output
> seconds = 2.41     <-  input file, calculations,. no write output
> seconds = 13.73   <-  benchmark time

timings.  There are two timings that I would like to see (for the "million
record" input).  I would be happy to report mine for the 10 record input as
well, but I find that so "small" that I don't see much point in it.

To meet the CLEAR specifications of the benchmark - you need to produce to
timings:

1) a version that does
     I/O for input file, I/O for output file, and calculations

2) a version that (by selection of a run-time OR compile-time switch - for the
same source code) does
    I/O for the input file, also I/O (for all million input records) but NO
calculations

It might be "nice" or interesting (to some) if you ALSO provided other options.
(For example, the COBOL Report Writer version that started this long thread does
NOT meet the specifications as it is impossible to run a version that does both
types of I/O but no calculations - because of the way that COBOL RW works.  I
"posted" a reference to this source code, but clearly indicated that it doesn't
meet the specifications).  I certainly don't mind your showing how Fortran can
handle a smilar programming specification that does NOT use I/O for input but
uses and internal table.  This is possible to do in COBOL - but as I can't see
any particular use of such a program, I haven't made such a program.

Finally, you state,

 time for just the calculations  is  2.41 -2.14 = .27  which I have reported
before

However, until (and unless) your "demonstrate" this for the million record input
file by actually DOING what the specifications indicate (i.e. provide two runs
of the million record input - both with full I/O but only one with
calculations - as selected by a run-time or compile-time switch).  I just don't
understand WHY you think that you "saying this" means that anyone will believe
you.

I have posted (on the net) copies of my output from the two runs that I did with
the million record input (with MOST of the lines cut-out so it isn't MB's
large).  Please let me know when (and where) you will be posting your output
files that match a single source program that actually MEETS the posted
applications specifications.
```

```
Sooo, why are you being mysterious about your computer hardware/cost?

Investigating why reading input file has went up in last timings reported,
I found I had asked compiler to not optimize.
Strangely, it speeds up first 2 timings, but overall time wasnt improved:
seconds = 1.62
seconds = 1.92
seconds = 13.77
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-22T13:15:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<xnIrc.10621$eH1.5793112@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<RhMpc.41916$TT.33207@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <RoPpc.2485$H_3.1791@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:RoPpc.2485$H_3.1791@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net...
> So far, I haven't seen a "43 line" version of the code that actually DOES
the
> required (by the specifications) I/O - much less formats the output
correctly
> for both the 10 record and million record inputs.

FWIW, I did do a BASIC language version for this program. Way more than 43
lines!

The source code is public domain and may be viewed/copied (*) at
http://www.powerbasic.com/support/forums/Forum7/HTML/002329.html


There is also a related posting at
http://www.powerbasic.com/support/forums/Forum6/HTML/004265.html , in which
I cite Mike Cowlishaw of IBM / UK commenting that the point was NOT to
compare languages or operating systems. (Also a comment from another
participant that he created a version which ran faster -  his only problem
being he got the wrong answer).

Comparison of "CPU time" versus "I-O time"  *could* be done but it would
require the code run on Windows NT, 2000 or XP (I run on Windows/98). (A
minor code addition would be required: a call to the GetProcessTimes API and
suitable output from what it returns.)

* To copy:
Since some browsers - specifically MS Internet Explorer - do not capture
line breaks if using Select/Copy, the best way to copy the code is to click
the "edit" button near the top of the post (middle icon with the pencil).
Then you can do a right-click, select all, right-click, copy and the code
will be placed on the  Windows clipboard with all formatting preserved.

You do NOT need to 'register' for this BBS to do this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

- **From:** "David Frank" <dave_frank@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-23T09:28:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<i9_rc.10983$0X2.322913@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<RhMpc.41916$TT.33207@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <RoPpc.2485$H_3.1791@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <xnIrc.10621$eH1.5793112@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message 
news:xnIrc.10621$eH1.5793112@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
> news:RoPpc.2485$H_3.1791@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net...
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Your coded banker's rounding is not very good as it could fail if
price * rate   product produces  an extended fraction beyond .0000

Note I use Fortran dp comparison to detect a 1/2 cent fraction  0.5 which is
exactly represented in any binary floating format, but a .005 fraction that 
you
are trying to detect is not.
Plus you *100  followed by  /100  more than once,
which also leads to problematical results in a exact decimal required 
solution.
(I never resort to /100)

Your 48 sec runtime is about what I would expect.

I claim BOTH the speed and conciseness of code title for Fortran.
(the Cobol solution altho about same speed with its output file writing
  used a cpu twice as fast to achieve its timings)..

Sooo,  bottom line is that unlike PL/I,  even PowerBasic has the syntax to
tackle this problem.
Thanks for sharing your efforts with us..
```

```
>
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > FWIW, I did do a BASIC language version for this program. Way more than
43
> > lines!
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> price * rate   product produces  an extended fraction beyond .0000

Cannot occur under specifications as given; and note my own code is only
invoked when the "5" occurs as third decimal digit.

I wouldn't have had to do it at all if the compiler had worked 'as
advertised.'  But of course we all know that in the Real World, fecal matter
occurs.

> Plus you *100  followed by  /100  more than once,
> which also leads to problematical results in a exact decimal required
> solution.

You must be an academic. I got the right answer; ergo I must have done the
math correctly.

>Sooo,  bottom line is that unlike PL/I,  even PowerBasic has the syntax to
tackle this problem.

I don't know PL/1, but I refuse to believe that any modern or even
quasi-modern language does not offer the tools to solve this. Sure, using
some language products it could get ugly, but you will recall 1983, when the
Chicago White Sox added "winning ugly" to our lexicon.

Correspondent BIlly S from Stratford-on-Avon UK offers some words of wisdom
here:  "The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, it is in our selves."

Or as I am fond of saying, "It's not the paintbrush, it's the artist."

MCM
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<gO%sc.3461$9p.1558@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<RhMpc.41916$TT.33207@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <RoPpc.2485$H_3.1791@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <xnIrc.10621$eH1.5793112@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <i9_rc.10983$0X2.322913@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <LZ0sc.3112$r22.1842@newssvr32.news.prodigy.com> <mHHsc.21739$0X2.553701@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <iUMsc.341$n65.105@newssvr33.news.prodigy.com> <2tXsc.27782$Ol3.22280@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"David Frank" <dave_frank@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:2tXsc.27782$Ol3.22280@twister.tampabay.rr.com...
>
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I actually took the time to read this, and I have an announcement to
make;
> > an announcement containing some good news and some bad news.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> challenges right?

> If thats not a true statement, show us the PowerBasic translation for just
> CANT #1  or #2  or #3  (your choice)..
> Robin sure CANT using PL/I ..

Some of these "can'ts" are so incredibly banal...e.g., # 18 "CANT find last
word index in trailing blank text using a loop   <= 3 statements."

Conditioning this on "using a loop of three or fewer statements" is
intellectual bankruptcy. Nobody cares about how many lines of some 3GL
High-level language source code it takes.

I can accomplish all* the "Can'ts" using PowerBASIC or COBOL, although in
some cases I need a few more, and in some cases a few less lines of source
code. If I wrote in any other 3Gl High-Level languages, I could do it in
those languages, too. And if worst came to worst, I could do it in the 2GL
x86 assembly laguage, not that that would be a whole lot of fun.

Which is my point.

For example, you might think it impossible using any language product to
calculate and display the factorial of one hundred and display it, accurate
to to all 158 digits. But it's not. Download my solution here (package
includes a lengthy text explanation)
http://www.talsystems.com/tsihome_html/downloads/Factor.zip

One can solve the IBM / Telco problem using any language which can open and
read fixed-record length files; do integer (and/or floating point) addition
and multiplication; has some way to get the bits of a single character (or
supports direct reading of either BCD or COMP-5 data), and can open and
write a character string to a file. As I said, I'm not a PL/1 programmer,
but I believe PL/1 has these tools available.

I hereby move my "whining drivel" comment from "postscript" to "front and
center" status.

OOPS... I forgot one other thing you need to solve the IBM/Telco problem:
imagination. Maybe that's the real deficiency here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Killfile (was Re: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-26T19:30:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Of6tc.72210$hH.1311492@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7H5tc.14998$eH1.6590081@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<RhMpc.41916$TT.33207@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <iUMsc.341$n65.105@newssvr33.news.prodigy.com> <2tXsc.27782$Ol3.22280@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <Uz3tc.12096$be.5141@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <c92n95$r27$1@panix5.panix.com> <7H5tc.14998$eH1.6590081@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Michael Mattias wrote:

> (snip) 
> 2. I don't have a clue about whatever this "killfile" thing is or does, so I
> couldn't do that if I wanted to.

I have never done this, but I believe a "killfile" is a filter you can 
set in your newsreader to automatically filter out messages by a 
specific poster, or on specific subject line.  The postings become 
invisible to you, so you avoid the irritation.  This is sometimes 
called a "bozo bin".  This doesn't work perfectly, you might still see 
replies that quote a killfiled poster.

One of the other entertainments of usenet is publicly killfiling a 
poster, usually by responding with an insult, followed by "PLONK!" on 
a line by itself.

Rather than leave misinformation lying around, it would probably be 
better to disagree politely, with reasoned arguments, and ignore 
continuing tirades.

Just my opinion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<10bam7aoh0tc778@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Uz3tc.12096$be.5141@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<RhMpc.41916$TT.33207@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <RoPpc.2485$H_3.1791@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <xnIrc.10621$eH1.5793112@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <i9_rc.10983$0X2.322913@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <LZ0sc.3112$r22.1842@newssvr32.news.prodigy.com> <mHHsc.21739$0X2.553701@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <iUMsc.341$n65.105@newssvr33.news.prodigy.com> <2tXsc.27782$Ol3.22280@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <Uz3tc.12096$be.5141@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> Michael,
>   Sorry that I got Mr. Frank posting to comp.lang.cobol (as well as
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> lack of logical conclusions, and basically irrelevant posts.

He lost me at "Can't" #4 - of what value is that?  I might just as well 
say "ForTran sucks because it can't define a variable using a Picture 
clause!"  You can turn a bolt with several different tools - vise grips, 
pliers, channel locks, set wrenches...  And, all of these can also be 
used, in absence of a hammer, to pound a nail into the wall.  :)

The best bet is to use the tool a) that's right for the job, and b) that 
you're comfortable with.  Mr. Frank is obviously comfortable with 
ForTran, and that's fine...  But, his assertion that all other tools are 
inadequate is misguided at best.
```

```
Hi,

assume that there's someone familiar with it.

I've got a problem with the Fujitsu COBOL version 3 for students that I
downloaded. I'm in the process of exploring the product {Programming
Staff � COBOL85} when I encountered a problem in the linking process,
WINLINK. The �exe� file can�t be created. It is indicated in the error
message that the �entry point was not defined�. Do you have any idea
why this occurred? I�m thinking that this is possibly due to the system
requirement. I installed the product under Windows 2000 whereas Window
95/98 NT 4.0 is the one required. In this case, an option is to
download Microsoft Compatibility software. I did but still got the same
problem. I�m aware that there�s no assurance that the product will work
even with the help of that said software. Do you think this has any
relevance with the problem I encountered?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL ver 3 for students - help!!!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T08:11:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL ver 3 for students - help!!!

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T19:00:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40A90BD4.2030905@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Ps_pc.3410$H_3.338@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<crine.16e8rz@mail.codecomments.com> <Ps_pc.3410$H_3.338@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> Check the COBOL FAQ, or check archives for discussions of marking a "Fujitsu"
> COBOL program as
> 
>    MAIN
> 
I'd like to add that this was the first problem I had
when working with Thane on his book.

My experience did not include the creation of Main and
other type programs. As I understand it now, without
the declaration to the ocmpiler that the program is to
be produced as a MAIN program (which causes it to be
taged with an extension of exe, the program is
produced as a callable subroutine and becomes what
MS calls a DLL. (Data link library). I gather that
DLL's take on a possible more global use becaue
other MAIN programs might find a use for them as well.

OOP. I hope I have it nearly right.

Warren Simmons
```

<a id="t-1206173116"></a>
## Thread: INSPECT: OS/VS vs. IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and OS/390 3.2.0 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### INSPECT: OS/VS vs. IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and OS/390 3.2.0

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T10:42:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8aj06$g3j$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

All righty... as mentioned the other day I coded:

01  CHAR-1      PIC X.    
01  CHAR-80 PIC X(80).    

...
PROCEDURE DIVISION.               
                                              
    PERFORM INSPECT-1.                        
    PERFORM INSPECT-80.                       
    GOBACK.                                   
INSPECT-1.                                    
    INSPECT CHAR-1 REPLACING ALL 'a' BY 'A'.  
INSPECT-80.                                   
    INSPECT CHAR-80 REPLACING ALL 'a' BY 'A' 
                             ALL 'b' BY 'B'  
                             ALL 'c' BY 'C'  
                             ALL 'd' BY 'D'  
                             ALL 'e' BY 'E'  
                             ALL 'f' BY 'F'  
                             ALL 'g' BY 'G'  
                             ALL 'h' BY 'H'  
                             ALL 'i' BY 'I'  
                             ALL 'j' BY 'J'  
                             ALL 'k' BY 'K'  
                             ALL 'l' BY 'L'  
                             ALL 'm' BY 'M'  
                             ALL 'n' BY 'N'. 

... and compiled.  For the first INSPECT OS/VS COBOL gave:

LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,230(0,13)           PRM=1                  
LA    0,008(0,6)            DNM=1-139              
ST    0,234(0,13)           PRM=2                  
MVI   230(13),X'00'         PRM=1                  
XC    24C(8,13),24C(13)     PRM=8           PRM=8  
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,244(0,13)           PRM=6                  
LA    0,028(0,12)           LIT+0                  
ST    0,248(0,13)           PRM=7                  
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,23C(0,13)           PRM=4                  
LA    0,029(0,12)           LIT+1                  
ST    0,240(0,13)           PRM=5                  
MVC   238(4,13),02A(12)     PRM=3           LIT+2  
MVI   250(13),X'80'         PRM=9                  
LA    1,230(0,13)           PRM=1                  
L     15,010(0,12)          V(ILBOINS0)            
BALR  14,15                         
L     1,220(0,13)           VN=01   
BCR   15,1                          

... and for the second...

LA    0,001(0,0)                                  
ST    0,230(0,13)           PRM=1                 
LA    0,008(0,6)            DNM=1-139             
ST    0,234(0,13)           PRM=2                 
MVI   230(13),X'00'         PRM=1                 
XC    24C(8,13),24C(13)     PRM=8           PRM=8 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                  
ST    0,244(0,13)           PRM=6                 
LA    0,028(0,12)           LIT+0                 
ST    0,248(0,13)           PRM=7                 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                  
ST    0,23C(0,13)           PRM=4                 
LA    0,029(0,12)           LIT+1                 
ST    0,240(0,13)           PRM=5                 
MVC   238(4,13),02A(12)     PRM=3           LIT+2 
XC    268(8,13),268(13)     PRM=15          PRM=15 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,260(0,13)           PRM=13                 
LA    0,02E(0,12)           LIT+6                  
ST    0,264(0,13)           PRM=14                 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,258(0,13)           PRM=11                 
LA    0,02F(0,12)           LIT+7                  
ST    0,25C(0,13)           PRM=12                 
MVC   254(4,13),02A(12)     PRM=10          LIT+2  
XC    284(8,13),284(13)     PRM=22          PRM=22 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,27C(0,13)           PRM=20                 
LA    0,030(0,12)           LIT+8                  
ST    0,280(0,13)           PRM=21                 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,274(0,13)           PRM=18                 
LA    0,031(0,12)           LIT+9                  
ST    0,278(0,13)           PRM=19                 
MVC   270(4,13),02A(12)     PRM=17          LIT+2  
XC    2A0(8,13),2A0(13)     PRM=29          PRM=29 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,298(0,13)           PRM=27                 
LA    0,032(0,12)           LIT+10                 
ST    0,29C(0,13)           PRM=28                 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,290(0,13)           PRM=25                 
LA    0,033(0,12)           LIT+11                 
ST    0,294(0,13)           PRM=26                 
MVC   28C(4,13),02A(12)     PRM=24          LIT+2  
XC    2BC(8,13),2BC(13)     PRM=36          PRM=36 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   
ST    0,2B4(0,13)           PRM=34                 
LA    0,034(0,12)           LIT+12                 
ST    0,2B8(0,13)           PRM=35                 
LA    0,001(0,0)                                   

... and this kept on going for another five screensful; if someone wants 
the whole thing then ask me by email.  IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and 
OS/390 3.2.0 yielded, for the first and second, respectively:

TR    8(1,2),292(10)          CHAR-1 

... and ...

TR    16(80,2),36(10)         CHAR-80 

DD
```

```
01  A-TABLE                  PIC X(16000) VALUE SPACES.

    03  A-TBL         OCCURS 16000  INDEXED BY A-INDX.   
        05  A-CODE            PIC X(1).                  
01  CHAR-1      PIC X.

SEARCH A-TBL                    
  AT END                        
      DISPLAY ' AT END'         
  WHEN A-CODE (A-INDX) = CHAR-1

BC    15,332(0,11)            GN=14(0003D4) 
EQU   *                                     
L     2,364(0,9)              A-INDX        
AH    2,30(0,10)              PGMLIT AT +18 
ST    2,364(0,9)              A-INDX        
EQU   *

C     2,364(0,9)              A-INDX        
BC    11,362(0,11)            GN=8(0003F2)

L     3,300(0,9)              BLW=0
```

```
One moment, Mr Simmons... ahhhhhh, here it is, right where it is always 
handy... according to the System/370 Reference Summary, GX20-1850-6, File 
No. S370/4300-1, Seventh Edition (July 1986), Page 8...

subscripted), the name Translate, format SS and Op Code DC and nothing 
classy or noteworthy.
```

<a id="t-7d52017068"></a>
## Thread: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements — 25 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T23:33:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Just thought I would share my personal opinions on these structures - starting a
new thread although much has already been stated that I agree with and others
that I do not.

FYI - for purposes of this note, I am *not* distinguishing between paragraphs
and sections.  It seems to me that the "style" discussions on that distinction
is quite distinct from what is being discussed recently.

"Traditional" (pre-85 Standard) COBOL used paragraphs as the one any only
*STANDARD* way to include "inline distinct procedures" (I'll ignore the ENTER -
not ENTRY - statement that probably was intended to do this - but I never used a
compiler that did much with it - so I can't speak from my experience to that
structure.)

During the development of the '85 Standard (and its implementation by a variety
of vendors), it was thought that "localization" of procedure *and* code was a
"good" thing for ease of understanding and (for some implementations)
performance.  Hence the creation and inclusion of NESTED PROGRAMS.  As such
programs could be "brought" in by a COPY statement and such procedures reached
by CALL IDENTIFIER (as well as CALL "literal").  This feature (at least in
theory) provided the following (theoretical?) advantages over PERFORM paragraph
logic:

 - data defined "near" where it was used (not a single "GLOBAL" cover-all
Working-Storage section)
 - localization of programming logic
 - ability to "pass data" to a routine within the same source "deck".

Personally, I haven't seen any "shops" or development projects that have WIDELY
implemented nested programs to "replace" PERFORMed paragraphs. My impression of
WHY they haven't "caught on" (widely) include (but are not limited to):

 - lack of (or at best delay in)  "good" debugging tools for them
 - hesitation to use COPY statements to bring in "large chunks" of procedural
code
 - fact that you still need to recompile the ENTIRE source program when a nested
program changes (unlike what you need to do for a (dynamically) called
separately compiled program)
 - Fact that most COBOL programmers don't see any harm in LARGE monolithic
programs (with HUGE generic Working-Storage sections)
 - General feeling that there is "little gain" for the required re-training and
change in paradigm for "large shops"

As opposed to both PERFORM statements and nested programs, ENTRY statements are
neither portable to all compilers nor to they actually work the same for all
compilers that accept them.

Furthermore,  I don't (personally) know of any compiler that documents support
for a CALL statement from within one program to an ENTRY statement in that SAME
program.  (I have only seen it as documented where one could use it to replace a
CALL statement to a SEPARATELY compiled program with an ENTRY statement).
Therefore, it seems (to me) not to do the same thing as either a PERFORM or a
NESTED PROGRAM, but rather to provide an "alias with a different entry point"
for a separately compiled program.  To me, this seems to provide NO functional
enhancement over simply having an EVALUATE statement at the beginning of a
program and from within it either doing a PERFORM or a CALL of a nested program.
In this way, one could be "guaranteed" of portable source code with identical
semantics on all conforming implementations.

NOTE:
  For those using CALL to ENTRY points in an IBM mainframe environment, there
are a variety of "gotchas" that some programmers are "hit by" because they are
relatively poorly documented.  A couple of my favorites are:

from:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg20/4.1.6.3

"An intervening CANCEL for dynamic calls to the same module at differing entry
points. CANCEL causes the program to be invoked in initial state when it is
called at a new entry point. "

and of course there is always the IBM recommendation NOT to use "entry"
statements in a "structured application" as at:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igya1101/3.9.1

where they say,

  "It is possible to specify another entry point where execution  begins, using
the ENTRY label in the called program.  However, this is not  a recommended
practice in a structured program.)"
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-17T20:52:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net...
[snip]
> Furthermore,  I don't (personally) know of any compiler that documents
support
> for a CALL statement from within one program to an ENTRY statement in that
SAME
> program.

Micro Focus COBOL/2 V2.5, which, if I recall correctly, you worked
on in a previous life with Micro Focus. That support is provided
is given in the PC Programmer's Guide, page 7-2 - 7-3, which
discusses recursion. An example for computing a factorial is
given. The description of the statements includes the following:
"Line 13 - This is the entry point of the recusive routine 'factor1'.
A program can call itself via either its 'program id' or an 'entry point'".

I don't claim that you ever knew this; only that, if you forgot, this
might jog your memory.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T20:43:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
From two of my "usually reliable sources" (at Micro Focus),

    ***

"Bill,
I can't point you to documentation, but I can tell you what works. With the
RECURSIVE attribute specified, I was able to call an entry point within the same
program. Without the RECURSIVE attribute, I got a fatal runtime error saying
that the recursive COBOL call was illegal."

  *** and

"Hi Bill. I believe you will need to make the program recursive in order to be
able to do this (call an entry point from within the same program). Not sure of
where the definitive statement is though..."

  ***

NOTE WELL:
   Once you get into making the calls RECURSIVE - remember that you will
(always/autmatically) get WS in "initial state" each time you do the call - and
when you GoBack or EXIT program you will "pop" the state that you "pushed" when
you did the call.

This certainly MAY have been documented as a "way" to do recurssion in MF COBOL
2.5 - but it was certainly NOT something that I personally ever remember doing
or seeing.  I think it is even LESS "visible" in the current MF product.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T01:04:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>From two of my "usually reliable sources" (at Micro Focus),
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>RECURSIVE attribute specified, I was able to call an entry point within the
same
>program. Without the RECURSIVE attribute, I got a fatal runtime error saying
>that the recursive COBOL call was illegal."
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>  ***

There is no RECURSIVE compiler option or "attribute". The compiler determines
you are doing recursion by seeing the program call itself.

-- quote --
Each call of a recursive routine is called an instance. Each instance needs a
different set of the data items used by the routine; so this COBOL system
enables you to have a Local-Storage Section. Every time a new instance of the
routine starts, a new, initialized copy of this section is created in memory;
this is deleted when the instance finishes. You get a run-time error message if
you try to use recursion in a program that does not contain a Local-Storage
Section." 
Program Development, chapter 3

>NOTE WELL:
>   Once you get into making the calls RECURSIVE - remember that you will
>(always/autmatically) get WS in "initial state" each time you do the call - and
>when you GoBack or EXIT program you will "pop" the state that you "pushed" when
>you did the call.

The above quotation says you get a Local-Storage Section for each instance, NOT
a new Working-Storage Section.

Someone is confusing RECURSIVE, there is no such option, with REENTRANT, which
comes in two flavors. 

REENTRANT(2) does exactly what you described. It is intended to painlessly make
any program thread-safe i.e. having two simultaneous callers. The manual says
you should change Working-Storage to Local-Storage or Thread-Local-Storage.
Experimentation shows that's not necessary; it works fine when left as
Working-Storage.

Local-Storage does not initialize with VALUEs. It doesn't issue any diagnostic;
it just doesn't do it. Thread-Local-Storage does initialize with VALUES, same as
Working-Storage. 

REENTRANT(1) makes Working-Storage 'shared' (one copy) and gives a distinct
Local-Storage for each instance of the program. This is the preferred mode for
reentrant programs, but the programmer must use synchronization measures to
simultaneous updates to Working-Storage.

-- quote --
If you specify REENTRANT"1", all compiler-generated temporary work areas are
allocated on a per-thread basis. All user data and FD file areas allocated in
the environment and data divisions are shared by all threads. It is the
responsibility of the programmer to ensure serialization to the program's data
by use of the CBL_ synchronization calls.

If you specify REENTRANT"2", all working-storage and file section data, as well
as system work areas, are allocated dynamically on the stack. This eliminates
thread contention on these areas, so that the program behaves safely if called
from multiple-threads concurrently. No program locking or unlocking is needed.
The disadvantage with this compiler directive setting is that there is no data
shared (other than any data defined as EXTERNAL) between the threads.

REENTRANT"2" is a quick and simple way of getting a program running in a
multi-threaded application, but you should aim to compile with REENTRANT"1".

A reentrant program must itself resolve all possible contention on data items in
its Working-Storage Section and File Section. One or more of the techniques for
resolving data contention must be used to accomplish this; see the next section
for details. 

Multi-threading, chapter 2

>This certainly MAY have been documented as a "way" to do recurssion in MF COBOL
>2.5 - but it was certainly NOT something that I personally ever remember doing
>or seeing.  I think it is even LESS "visible" in the current MF product.

I'm working on a demo showing how to do multithreading, including how to
synchronize memory access MUCH faster than using the library provided.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T01:55:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8zqc.810$Tn6.529@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert,
  Check your CURRENT Micro Focus documentation before making such claims.

Check out either:
   NetExpress
http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/lrpubb.htm

or
  ServerExpress
http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/sx40/lrpubb.htm

Look at the SYNTAX and RULES for the
   PROGRAM-ID
paragraph.

In both case, you will see that Micro Focus matches the 2002 ISO Standard by
implementing the RECURSIVE phrase.

The statement
  "You get a run-time error message if  you try to use recursion in a program
that does not contain a Local-Storage Section."

was true with some of the EARLY MF implementations.  If you tell me where you
are quoting from, I can find out how it is worded in CURRENT Micro Focus
documenation.  I found (in the current documentation) the topic

Common, Initial and Recursive Attributes

which says (in part),

"A recursive program may call itself directly or indirectly. The program's
internal data is initialized as described in the section State of a Function,
Method, Object or Program. The recursive attribute is attained as specified in
the section The Program-ID Paragraph in the chapter Identification Division."

It is true that there is a separate copy of local storage for every instance of
a recursive program.  However, notice that the current documentation also says,

"A function, method or program may be activated recursively. Therefore, several
instances of a function, method or program may be active at once."

Also,

 "Whenever an instance of a function, method or program is activated, the
control mechanisms for PERFORM statements contained in that instance of the
function, method or program are set to their initial states and the GO TO
statements referred to by ALTER statements are set to their initial states."

However, it true (I think in correction to what I previously stated) that I
think WS items themselves are in "last-used state" while Local-Storage items are
in "initial state".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T04:16:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40aadaf8.125442205@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net> <a8zqc.810$Tn6.529@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Robert,
>  Check your CURRENT Micro Focus documentation before making such claims.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>implementing the RECURSIVE phrase.

I looked and see you're right.

>The statement
>  "You get a run-time error message if  you try to use recursion in a program
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>documenation. 

--- quote --

Each call of a recursive routine is called an instance. Each instance needs a
different set of the data items used by the routine; so this COBOL system
enables you to have a Local-Storage Section. Every time a new instance of the
routine starts, a new, initialized copy of this section is created in memory;
this is deleted when the instance finishes. You get a run-time error message if
you try to use recursion in a program that does not contain a Local-Storage
Section. 

Program Development, chapter 3, Server Express 4.0
No change from Server Express 2.2 at 
http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/sx22sp1/sx22indx.htm

> I found (in the current documentation) the topic
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>instances of a function, method or program may be active at once."

"Active" means 'have not exited'. It does not mean 'running in parallel'.
Recursive programs are single-threaded. 

>Also,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>think WS items themselves are in "last-used state" while Local-Storage items
are
>in "initial state".

As before, the initial state is random. 
As before, thread-local-storage IS initialized. 

--- quote ---
The VALUE clause is treated as documentary. 

Language Reference, chapter 7

--- history below ---
>-- 
>Bill Klein
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T05:26:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8f991$8j6$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>There is no RECURSIVE compiler option or "attribute". The compiler determines
>you are doing recursion by seeing the program call itself.

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  RECURSER.
...
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  WS-PROG PIC X(8).
01  WS-PARM-AREA.
    05  WS-PARMLEN PIC 9(4) COMP.
    05  WS-PARM-PROG PIC X(8).

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  PARMS.
    05 PARMLEN PIC 9(4) COMP.
    05 PARM-PROG PIC X(8).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARMS.

    MOVE PARM-PROG  TO  WS-PROG.
    CALL WS-PROG USING WS-PARM-AREA.

Now, Mr Wagner, I don't know all that much about recursion... so do tell, 
how would the compiler know that PARM-PROG = 'RECURSER' and the 
execution-libraries (or STEPLIBs) are using the program shown here?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net>,

>execution-libraries (or STEPLIBs) are using the program shown here?

I was wrong about detecting recursion by looking at the CALL.  Local-Storage
tells it the program is recursive. I think IBM introduced the feature with LE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T19:43:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405181843.1596ef9a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote 

> NOTE WELL:
>    Once you get into making the calls RECURSIVE - remember that you will
> (always/autmatically) get WS in "initial state" each time you do the call - and
> when you GoBack or EXIT program you will "pop" the state that you "pushed" when
> you did the call.

I think that you meant LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION for "WS".

> This certainly MAY have been documented as a "way" to do recurssion in MF COBOL
> 2.5 - but it was certainly NOT something that I personally ever remember doing
> or seeing.  I think it is even LESS "visible" in the current MF product.

I have a set of MF 2.5 (and 2.1, 2.4, 3.2 and 3.4) manuals and
recursion is also discussed in Operating Guide where an example is
given, and in Ch.33 where a warning is given of 'indirect recursion'
by way of a callback.
```

- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>From two of my "usually reliable sources" (at Micro Focus),
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I *wonder* if this might be what your 'reliable sources' were thinking 
of  - one of the old DOS V 3 programs :-

      $set ans85 noosvs mf

      ****************************************************************
      * Copyright Micro Focus Limited 1989.    All Rights Reserved.  *
      * This demonstration program is provided for use by users of   *
      * Micro Focus products and may be used, modified and           *
      * distributed as part of your application provided that you    *
      * properly acknowledge the copyright of Micro Focus in this    *
      * material.                                                    *
      ****************************************************************

        PROGRAM-ID. DIOPHANT.
      ******************************************************************
      *
      *                     DIOPHANT.CBL
      *
      * DIOPHANTINE - solve linear equation Ax + By = C
      *                 for integers x and y.
      *
      * Method:
      *         if A > B
      *             swap A and B
      *         fi
      *
      *         when A = 0
      *             set x = 0, y = C/B as solution, and fail if non-integer
      *         when A = 1
      *             set x = C, y = 0 as solution
      *         otherwise
      *             let D = largest integer < (B/A)
      *             let E = largets integer < (C/A)
      *             let F = B - A*D
      *             let G = C - A*E
      *             then Ax + By = C becomes
      *                 Ax + (F + A*D)y = (G + A*E)
      *             so   x + (F/A + D)y = (G/A + E)
      *             and (F/A)y + v = G/A   (since everything else is 
integral)
      *             so solve
      *                 Fy + Av = G for integers y and v
      *
      *             in COBOL terms:
      *
      *             divide B by A giving D remainder F
      *             divide C by A giving E remainder G
      *             solve Av + Fw = G for integers v and w
      *             set x = E - Dw + v, y = w as solution
      *
      *         if swapped
      *             swap x and y
      *         fi
      *
      *
      ******************************************************************
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01  InitA   PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  InitB   PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  InitC   PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  SolvX   PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  SolvY   PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  FailFg  PIC X.
            88      OK  VALUE 'Y'.
            88      BAD VALUE 'N'.
            88      TRY VALUE '?'.
        LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION.
        01  D       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  E       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  F       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  G       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  V       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  TEMP    PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        LINKAGE SECTION.
        01  A       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  B       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  C       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  X       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        01  Y       PIC s9(9) comp-5.
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        MAIN SECTION.
            DISPLAY "Solve Ax + By = C for integers x and y"
            DISPLAY "Enter value for A: " with no advancing
            ACCEPT InitA
            DISPLAY "Enter value for B: " with no advancing
            ACCEPT InitB
            DISPLAY "Enter value for C: " with no advancing
            ACCEPT InitC
            SET TRY TO TRUE
            CALL 'SOLVE' USING BY VALUE InitA InitB InitC
                               BY REFERENCE SolvX SolvY

            IF OK
                DISPLAY "Solution is: x = " SolvX ", y = " SolvY
            ELSE
                DISPLAY "No Solution exists."
            END-IF
            STOP RUN.

        SOLVE-DIOPHANTINE SECTION.
        ENTRY 'SOLVE' USING BY VALUE A B C BY REFERENCE X Y.

            IF A > B
      *     Use TEMP as a flag to indicate swapped or not.
                MOVE 1 TO TEMP
                CALL 'SWAP2' USING A B
            ELSE
                MOVE 0 TO TEMP
            END-IF

            EVALUATE A
                WHEN 0
                    DIVIDE C BY B GIVING D REMAINDER E
                    IF E = 0
                        MOVE 0 TO X
                        MOVE D TO Y
                        SET OK TO TRUE
                    ELSE
      *     No solution exists.
                        SET BAD TO TRUE
                        MOVE 0 TO X, Y
                    END-IF

                WHEN 1
                    MOVE C TO X
                    MOVE 0 TO Y
                    SET OK TO TRUE

                WHEN OTHER
      *     We must delve deeper to find a solution.
                    DIVIDE B BY A GIVING D REMAINDER F
                    DIVIDE C BY A GIVING E REMAINDER G

                    CALL 'SOLVE' USING BY VALUE A F G BY REFERENCE v Y

                    COMPUTE X = E - ( D * Y ) + v

            END-EVALUATE

            IF TEMP = 1
                CALL 'SWAP2' USING X Y
            END-IF

            EXIT PROGRAM.


      * Second level program to swap 2 variables using local temp variable.
        SWAPPER SECTION.
        ENTRY 'SWAP2' USING X Y.
            MOVE X    TO TEMP
            MOVE Y    TO X
            MOVE TEMP TO Y
            EXIT PROGRAM.
---------------------------------------------------

Going through the Animator it sure looks like it's recursive - but I 
don't have a clue (what does Diophant mean to a rocket scientist like 
me). I keyed in the values A = 12, B = 14 and C = 36 getting the answer :-

"Solution is x = 3 y = 0"

Now if you want to get recursive/iterative take the following. Not 
written by me - solution was provided by David Sands at Newbury.  Last 
trip to my GP (the computer freak), I noticed he had ComboBox Droplists 
in his medical application which were coloured - "dead ugly" - each 
elemeent was a different colour - the same basic set of colours used 
with Screen Section. Thought I'd like to do that with Droplists but go 
for one pastel colour per droplist using RGB. David was storing the set 
of base colours, but I've adapated it to store a pastel RGB Colour per 
individual droplist, as there are occasions where you could have more 
than one per dialog.

       copy "windows.cpy".
       *>-----------------dlgdropl.cbl --------------------------------

       Class-id.             DropListDialog
                             data is protected
                             inherits from TabbedDialog with data.

       copy "callconv.cpy".

       Class-control.        DropListDialog   is class "dlgdropl"
                             TabbedDialog     is class "dlgtabbd"

                             Behavior         is class "behavior"
                             Brush            is class "brush"
                             Color            is class "color"
                             DrawingSurface   is class "graphwin"
                              .
       *> ------------------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY.
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
 *> It needs above dummy entry - to do with inheriting data from super - 
a so-called Micro Focus extension, though it            existed long 
before COBOL 2002 was dreamed up and put to bed !
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END FACTORY.
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT.
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 ComboCount                        pic x(4) comp-5.
       78 ComboBoxMax                       value 6.
       01 os-color      occurs ComboBoxMax  object reference value null.
       01 os-ComboBox   occurs ComboBoxMax  object reference value null.
       01 ws-ComboID    occurs ComboBoxMax  pic x(4) comp-5.

       *>  SimpleComboItem
       *>  DropComboItem
       *>  DropListComboItem

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       *>
       *>  Following relate soley to Coloured ComboBox DropDown Lists
       *>
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "finalizeObjects".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       copy "\copylib\sqlresult.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 ls==.
       01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-Object                     object reference.

       Procedure Division.

       *>01 os-color      occurs ComboBoxMax  object reference.
       *>01 os-ComboBox   occurs ComboBoxMax  object reference.

        perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ComboCount
            set ls-Object to os-Color(n)
            perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
        End-perform

        perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ComboCount
            set ls-Object to os-ComboBox(n)
            perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
        End-perform

        invoke super "finalizeObjects"

        if self <> null
           invoke self "finalize" returning self
        End-if

       EXIT METHOD.

        copy "\copylib\finalize.cpy".

       End Method "finalizeObjects".
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "passComboCount".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-count                        pic x(4) comp-5.
       Procedure Division using lnk-Count.
         move lnk-Count to ComboCount
       End Method "passComboCount".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "passDroplistRGB".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-Background.
          05 RedB                  pic x(4) comp-5.
          05 GreenB                pic x(4) comp-5.
          05 BlueB                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-n                    pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Background, lnk-n.

         invoke Color "RGB"
                using RedB, GreenB, BlueB returning os-Color(lnk-n)

       End Method "passDroplistRGB".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "setDroplistContents".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       copy "\copylib\dlgparams.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
       01 lnk-n                        pic x(4) comp-5.
       Procedure Division using lnk-Params, lnk-n.
          invoke os-ComboBox(lnk-n) "clear"
          invoke os-ComboBox(lnk-n) "setcontents" using lnk-Object
       End Method "setDroplistContents".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       *>    Here we trap the Paint notifications to the Drop Down
       *>    ComboBox on the Dialog. Its up to us how it's drawn !
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       method-id. "wmDrawItem".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       local-storage section.
       01 lsObject         usage object reference.
       01 lsText           usage object reference.
       01 drawsurface      usage object reference.
       01 myBrush          usage object reference.
       01 myFont           usage object reference.
       01 state-flag       uint.
       01 lsPixelHeight    pic 9(9) comp-5.
       01 ls-x             pic s9(9) comp-5.
       01 ls-y             pic s9(9) comp-5.
       01 ls-width         pic s9(9) comp-5.
       01 ls-height        pic s9(9) comp-5.
       01 lsindex          PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
       01 n2               pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 n3               pic x(4) comp-5.

       linkage section.
       01 lnkWnd       HWND.
       01 lnkMsg       INT.
       01 lnkwParam    INT.
       01 lnklParam    POINTER.
       01 mResult      LRESULT.
       01 drawItem     DRAWITEMSTRUCT.

       procedure division using lnkWnd lnkMsg lnkwParam lnklParam
          returning mResult.

       *>01  tagDRAWITEMSTRUCT is typedef.
       *> 02 ctltype           usage uns-int.
       *> 02 ctlid             usage uns-int.
       *> 02 itemid            usage uns-int.
       *> 02 itemaction        usage uns-int.
       *> 02 itemstate         usage uns-int.
       *> 02 hwnditem          usage data-pointer.
       *> 02 1hdc              usage data-pointer.
       *> 02 rcitem            usage rect.
       *> 02 itemdata          usage uns-long.

       set address of drawitem to lnklparam

       move zeroes to n3
       Perform varying n2 from 1 by 1 until n2 > Combocount

              if ctlid = ws-ComboID(n2)
                 move n2 to n3
                 EXIT PERFORM
              End-if
       End-perform

       Evaluate true
          when  n3 = zeroes
          when  itemid = -1
                continue
          when  itemAction = ODA-DRAWENTIRE
          when  itemAction = ODA-SELECT
                perform DRAW-ENTIRE
       End-evaluate

       EXIT METHOD.

       DRAW-ENTIRE.

        invoke DrawingSurface "fromHandle" using 1hdc
            returning drawsurface

        invoke brush       "new"                returning mybrush
        invoke mybrush     "solid"
        invoke mybrush     "setcolor"           using os-color(n3)
        invoke drawsurface "setBackGroundColor" using os-color(n3)

        move 1left       to ls-x
        move 1top        to ls-y
        compute ls-height = 1bottom - 1top
        compute ls-width = 1right - 1left
        invoke drawsurface "fillrectangle"
            using ls-x ls-y ls-width ls-height mybrush
        compute lsindex = itemid + 1

        invoke os-ComboBox(n3) "GetItemText"
               using lsindex returning lstext

        invoke drawsurface   "getFont" returning myFont
        invoke myFont        "getPixelHeight" returning lsPixelHeight
        compute ls-y = (1bottom + 1top - lsPixelHeight) /2
        invoke drawsurface   "drawTextAt" using lstext ls-x ls-y

       *> If this is the Selected Item then draw a Focus
       *> rectangle around the item to give user feedback on what
       *> is current in the list

           move ods-selected to state-flag
           call "cbl_and" using itemstate
                                state-flag
                                by value 4
           if state-flag > 0
               call wapi "DrawFocusRect"
                using by value 1hdc by reference rcitem
           end-if

       *>---- Clean Up

           invoke mybrush     "finalize"     returning mybrush
           invoke lstext      "finalize"     returning lstext
           invoke myFont      "destroyproxy" returning myFont
           invoke drawsurface "destroyproxy" returning drawsurface
           .

       End method "wmDrawItem".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "zCreateDroplist".    *> COLOURED DROPLISTS - this is 
one of my additons
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       *> This method is for coloured droplists and overrides the same
       *> method in 'super' TabbedDialog (tdialog.cbl). 'OwnerDraw' and
       *> 'HasStrings' MUST be set in the Dialog Editor when creating
       *> the ComboBox

       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-event                      pic x(4) comp-5.
       Linkage section.
       copy "\copylib\dlgParams.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
       01 lnk-n                         pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Params, lnk-n.

       *> Note : My collection is already sorted, so I don't want the
       *> Dialog sorting the contents before display

        move lnk-ID to ws-ComboID(lnk-n)
        initialize ws-MethodRec
        invoke self "getObjectFromId" using lnk-ID
               returning os-ComboBox(lnk-n)
        invoke os-ComboBox(lnk-n) "MonoSpacedFont"
        invoke os-ComboBox(lnk-n) "NotSorted"
        move p2ce-select to ls-event
        invoke os-ComboBox(lnk-n) "setEventTo" using
                    ls-event self "ReturnDropList "
                    lnk-Field, lnk-ReturnType
        invoke os-DialogCollection(1) "add" using  os-ComboBox(lnk-n)
        move lnk-MethodName to ws-MethodName
        invoke os-DialogCollection(2) "add" using  ws-MethodRec

       End Method "zCreateDroplist".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

       End OBJECT.
       End CLASS DropListDialog.

       *>---------------------------------------------------------------

The interesting thing is that nowhere do I invoke  "wmDrawItem" - it has 
to be triggered when I go to add the collection to the Droplist. And 
"wmDrawItem" automatically repeats itself (recursive/iterative) until it 
has created a particular droplist. The thing that really fascinated me 
when I ran it and stepped through the code with the Animator - as it 
goes through "wmDrawItem" the Animator visually shows what's happening 
per element in the collection (1) draws the rectangle, (2) colours it 
and (3)
.sets the text. I felt like a little boy with a lollipop ;-)

Yeah I know - some smart ass is going to come up with a slicker answer 
for doing Diophants - but I ain't interested !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T01:28:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Fdqc.211674$M3.172450@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
For code that I do, paragraphs or CALLs to separately compiled routines are
it. No ENTRY nor nested programs. For the record, I tend to like nested
programs for the basic reason of  "locality of reference" - not lumping
every WS field together in one place, and having clearly delineated
parameters.

So, why don't I use nested programs? First, our build process does not
support their use. We know how to fix it but it just isn't important. (Site
note - the majority of our source is still COBOL 74 and most of the
developers could care less about upgrading, even when it is simple.) Second,
if it was possible to utilize nested programs, there is the familiarity
factor for staff. Many of them only know COBOL 74 and have never dealt with
more structured languages so the concepts would have to be explained as well
as the syntax. (I know too many staff that find it preferable to duplicate
code rather than create a copybook or subroutine as it is. Why would they
embrace this?) Third, my own personal bias, is that although I like the
parameter passing, it causes me a little grief. Our general standard is that
subroutine parameters should be in a copybook to ensure that the caller and
called program have matching descriptions. I think this is a good thing.
With nested programs, do I now create copybooks for their parameters and
therefore have more source elements that need to be managed? Since the
source may all be contained in a single file, is keeping the parameter
descriptions consistent without a copybook going to be acceptable?

Perhaps this is a platform dependent issue, but what value is there in
placing a nested program into a copybook versus making it a separately
compiled program? I'm on Unisys 2200 Series hardware and all of the
executable programs are statically linked. I can't image that there is any
discernible difference in performance between the two methods.

Finally, ENTRY. I'm not even sure I'm thinking the same syntax as you on
this one. If I am, I would consider it something to stay away from in most
instances. As you mentioned, there are alternatives that I think should be
preferred. However, I have used it in a couple of special situations where
it was convenient and I felt that readability would actually be reduced by
employing a different approach.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T12:03:44+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<AYmqc.2389$k%2.330@newssvr32.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <0Fdqc.211674$M3.172450@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
"Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net> wrote in message
news:0Fdqc.211674$M3.172450@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
> For code that I do, paragraphs or CALLs to separately compiled routines
are
> it....

> Second, if it was possible to utilize nested programs, there is the
familiarity
> factor for staff. Many of them only know COBOL 74 and have never dealt
with
> more structured languages so the concepts would have to be explained as
well
> as the syntax.

I think you underestimate the value of an hour invested in a training
session; more importantly, you underestimate - or demean - the ability of
human beings to learn new things.

For crying out loud, if someone can write COBOL programs, and has been doing
so since COBOL 74 (in which they certainly did not receive any formal
instruction very recently), surely they can handle it.

If your firm is unwilling to invest an hour or so in an employee, well, that
simply confirms the patronizing attitude expressed above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T01:14:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5yyqc.217978$M3.146760@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <0Fdqc.211674$M3.172450@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <AYmqc.2389$k%2.330@newssvr32.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I have no problem investing some time in existing staff. I have done some
overviews/training on different topics myself. However, many of the staff
don't care to learn (which was my main point or at least intent) and there
is little organizational impetus to do any training. Existing permanent
staff is dwindling due to retirements and nearly all new COBOL staff are
contractors who are expected to "know" whatever we hired them for and we
refuse to train them in most cases.

As an aside, moving from COBOL 74 to COBOL 85 is non-trivial in many cases
on our system. New compiler, new linker, new run-time environment,
transaction programs have to be substantially modified, etc.which has
contributed to the resistance.
```
