###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T04:48:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40aae224.127278750@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <0Fdqc.211674$M3.172450@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <AYmqc.2389$k%2.330@newssvr32.news.prodigy.com> <5yyqc.217978$M3.146760@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
"Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net> wrote:

>I have no problem investing some time in existing staff. I have done some
>overviews/training on different topics myself. However, many of the staff
>don't care to learn (which was my main point or at least intent) and there
>is little organizational impetus to do any training.

I hate the word 'train'. Plants and animals are trained; people should be
educated. The difference is respect for their cognition.

>Existing permanent
>staff is dwindling due to retirements and nearly all new COBOL staff are
>contractors who are expected to "know" whatever we hired them for and we
>refuse to train them in most cases.

That's what it's like in the contracting world, in my experience. No tools, no
manuals, no text editor except whatever came with the operating system, no
support, no pride, no respect. They call us 'resources' rather than people.

What school of Business Administration teaches this style of management?

>As an aside, moving from COBOL 74 to COBOL 85 is non-trivial in many cases
>on our system. New compiler, new linker, new run-time environment,
>transaction programs have to be substantially modified, etc.which has
>contributed to the resistance.

New? Excuse me .. 1985 was TWENTY YEARS AGO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T00:30:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40ad46f3.284181593@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40aae224.127278750@news.optonline.net> <c8f9mp$8t5$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ac3706.214557647@news.optonline.net> <c8i0oo$k55$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>You get to use one of those at *work*, Mr Wagner?  I know of folks who 
>have worked on sites where consultants are not allowed Internet access... 
>ain't it a Silly Ol' World?

I've had six clients in six years who all provided internet access. Perhaps
that's because they were very large organizations that made the decision at
Levels much higher than department manager. 

When I started at Merrill Lynch in 2000, only Authorized Persons with a Need To
Know (as deemed by their manager) had access. A month later, Top Management gave
it to everyone. There was much grumbling about Lost Productivity. Miraculously,
the company survived.
```

Depending on how a Unisys 2200 ACOB program was written, it -can- be

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T00:43:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Agcrc.225445$M3.179106@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <0Fdqc.211674$M3.172450@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <AYmqc.2389$k%2.330@newssvr32.news.prodigy.com> <5yyqc.217978$M3.146760@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <40aae224.127278750@news.optonline.net> <10ao5b92e5o0o33@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Trust me, I'm *very* familiar with the conversion aspects. I've been pushing
people in the UCOB direction since about 1995 when the COBOL 85 standard was
a bit newer. Our biggest headache is with TIP transactions. We still run
COMPOOL instead of MCB and none (and I mean NONE) of the existing
transactions were coded using DPS, just the COMPOOL primitives and hardcoded
screens. Those are the programs that require significant code changes. Many
batch programs can be converted simply by changing compilers and  making a
couple of of minor syntax changes (and using COMPAT/FULL) but I still get
solid refusals to do it. For me, getting END-IF and EVALUATE were enough of
an inducement!


"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:10ao5b92e5o0o33@corp.supernews.com...
> Robert Wagner wrote:
>
> > "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net> wrote:
> >
> >>As an aside, moving from COBOL 74 to COBOL 85 is non-trivial in many
cases
> >>on our system. New compiler, new linker, new run-time environment,
> >>transaction programs have to be substantially modified, etc.which has
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T19:43:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10at8hvfm3g7124@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Agcrc.225445$M3.179106@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <0Fdqc.211674$M3.172450@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <AYmqc.2389$k%2.330@newssvr32.news.prodigy.com> <5yyqc.217978$M3.146760@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <40aae224.127278750@news.optonline.net> <10ao5b92e5o0o33@corp.supernews.com> <Agcrc.225445$M3.179106@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Douglas Gallant wrote:

> Trust me, I'm *very* familiar with the conversion aspects. I've been pushing
> people in the UCOB direction since about 1995 when the COBOL 85 standard was
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> screens. 

Ouch - Is it really old code, or was that just how the people who wrote 
it chose (or were directed) to write it?  It's been a while since I read 
about COMPOOL, and then I just skimmed it since we didn't use it.  Is it 
that they require different parameters, or that the name of the call is 
different?

> Those are the programs that require significant code changes. Many
> batch programs can be converted simply by changing compilers and  making a
> couple of of minor syntax changes (and using COMPAT/FULL) but I still get
> solid refusals to do it. For me, getting END-IF and EVALUATE were enough of
> an inducement!

:)  That and inline PERFORMs were all it took to sell my office on it. 
Plus, with UCOB being extended-mode, we could use PCFP (Program-Callable 
FURPUR) to sling files around - stuff you couldn't do with ERACSF$ in 
basic mode.
```

cases

with the net effects either stated or implied in prior posts.

Right you are...  I don't generally do it that way, though...  :)

>>PARAGRAPH statement - it's a shame our compiler doesn't interpret it
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T14:38:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8d75f$lke$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <c82eb3$ali$1@panix5.panix.com> <4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com> <c8534n$lah$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ad4hhq68jdv01@corp.supernews.com> <c8amo4$3q2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ainrt34as1hf0@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On 17-May-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> > It is perfectly acceptable to have
> >     IF MY-SWITCH
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Right you are...  I don't generally do it that way, though...  :)

Where I might do this is with a debug "D" in column 7 of the display statement.
 When I compile with debug on, the display works.   When I compile with the
debug off, it is a comment.

Replacing EXIT with CONTINUE allows the following to compile either way.

 EXIT-PARAGRAPH.
       CONTINUE.
D     DISPLAY "MYDEBUG Reached EXIT-PARAGRAPH".
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>Where I might do this is with a debug "D" in column 7 of the display statement.
> When I compile with debug on, the display works.   When I compile with the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>D     DISPLAY "MYDEBUG Reached EXIT-PARAGRAPH".

Why not say READY TRACE to have it display all paragraph names?

If you make READY TRACE conditional on a PARM, there's no need to check the
program out or recompile it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Logic Error?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T03:51:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QQAqc.1909$be.84@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<rgWoc.249467$e17.171934@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <XeOdneIwrMeCLjndRVn-vw@giganews.com> <c82eb3$ali$1@panix5.panix.com> <4eydnSQ7cdsmmTvdRVn-jA@giganews.com> <c8534n$lah$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ad4hhq68jdv01@corp.supernews.com> <c8amo4$3q2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ainrt34as1hf0@corp.supernews.com> <c8d75f$lke$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40aad589.124051143@news.optonline.net>`

```
Besides the fact that READY TRACE is only supported by a relatively few
compilers, it is worth noting it is NOT supported by any IBM compiler introduced
since 1984 !!!

Howard (I believe) works on IBM mainframes - where READY TRACE is an
"historical" memory - but certainly NOT current technology.
```

>Besides the fact that READY TRACE is only supported by a relatively few
>compilers, it is worth noting it is NOT supported by any IBM compiler
introduced
>since 1984 !!!

It is supported by Micro Focus, and probably still supported by Realia.

## Thread: COBOL source required for research — 1 msgs

### COBOL source required for research

- **From:** a.mohan@postgrad.umist.ac.uk (Andrew Mohan a.mohan at postgrad.umist.ac.uk)
- **Date:** 2004-05-18T08:46:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3371cd00.0405180746.20833da8@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone help? I'm currently undertaking some research, as part of
my PhD, into determining the maintainability of COBOL programs.
However I need some COBOL programs to undertake some evalution of my
method. In particular, I'm after COBOL programs with all (or some) of
their versions and (the important bit) some indication of the effort
involved in applying maintenance to each version.

If you can help me with this, or would like to know some more about my
research then please contact me.

Regards,
Andrew.
```

#### ↳ Re: *LARGE* COBOL programs - new SHARE requirement

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T04:56:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bOBqc.501401$Pk3.37699@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Hxxqc.627$Tn6.513@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<E1BQD4J-0006Hy-00@granger.mail.mindspring.net> <Hxxqc.627$Tn6.513@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>I thought my note to IBM-MAIN might be of interest to some in other newsgroups.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>

>>In this day and age when a growing number of COBOL programs are created by
>>"tools" which generate COBOL source code (and, hence, the code may not be
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
I must chide you, even if somewhat gently, on your above statement. 
Although you've never been negative about the Standard including OO 
syntax, as you've indicated many times, you haven't bought into OO. Well 
if you Big Boys on the Big Iron want LARGE programs so be it, with the 
attendant LARGE MAINTENANCE..

I'm not clear if the quote about large OO progrms is fro m you or from 
the IBM quote, earliert in the text - nevertheless whoever wrote it -  
But you(tehy)  are totally, totally wrong about needing LARGE OO 
programs (classes). It's still my contention the major buzz word for OO 
is *REUSE* - and essentially you only obtain that by (1) fragmenting  a 
LARGE program into a set of specific classes to do specific functions, 
and (2) For each separate class, where possible, segment it into small 
discrete, (and very often 'bland')  methods.

Example # 1 - in response to your message about Nested Programs - 
re-entrant/recursive/iterative - whatever......

(a) Class TabbedDialog - a generic template that does all the donkey 
work for setting up controls in any Dialog
(b) I showed the code for DropListDialog, (where I want to colour the 
droplists),  which inherits from  (a)

Using the Large program concept why not include (b) in (a) - after all 
(a) currently only contains just over 86 methods and the top limit is 
1,000. Reasons for not doing it (1) I might want to fine-tune (b) and 
could screw myself if it was included in (a).
Reason # 2 - not every dialog has droplists, or again I might not want 
to colour them - that gives me two choices :-

- may or may not contain droplists but NO colouring :-

         invoke TabbedDialog (which inherits from Modal or Modeless) 
"start" using .......... returning os-Dialog,    OR

- I want a dialog with a coloured DropList :-

          invoke DropListgDialog (which inherits from TabbedDialog) 
"start"using ....... returning os-Dialog

I should mention that initializing GUIs in M/F is the exception. You 
can't invoke "new" - it goes belly up inheriting from Modal or Modeless. 
So you call your "new" whatever you want - invoke "startIt" "BeginIt" 
"makeIt", whatever .......

Example # 2 :

I didn't show the code for TabbedDialog, but it does contain 'bland' 
methods, (if we want to get academic, polymorphic methods). I have left 
in the code for two of them, so that you get the idea what the rest are 
doing  :-

       Method-id. "checkChangedFlag".
       Method-id. "ElementChanged".
       Method-id. "finalizeObjects".
       Method-id. "getCoordinates".
       Method-id. "returnObjectFromField".
       Method-id. "setAsEmpty".
       Method-id. "setClear".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "setDisable".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       copy "\copylib\dlgparams.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

         invoke os-DialogCollection(1) "at"
                using lnk-field returning lnk-object
         invoke lnk-object "disable"

       End Method "setDisable".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "setDisableClear".    *> used for RadioButtons
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       copy "\copylib\dlgparams.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

         invoke os-DialogCollection(1) "at"
            using lnk-field returning lnk-object
         invoke lnk-object "disable"
         invoke lnk-object "clear"

       End Method "setDisableClear".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "setDisableEmpty".
       Method-id. "setEnable".
       Method-id. "setEnableEmpty".
       Method-id. "setFocusDefault".
       Method-id. "SetGainedFocus".
       Method-id. "setHide".
       Method-id. "setHideDisable".
       Method-id. "setNextFocusClear".    *> for DropLists/Radiobuttons
       Method-id. "setNextFocusEmpty".
       Method-id. "setNextFocusInsert".
       Method-id. "setRecordFlags".
       Method-id. "setRGBColors".
       Method-id. "setSelectedUnselected".
       Method-id. "SetTheColor".
       Method-id. "setTheFocus".
       Method-id. "SetTheLength".
       Method-id. "showControl".

So out of my total methods, some 86, (which include creating controls 
and event callback methods), there are 26 which fit into the 'bland' 
category and for the most part are applicable to every GUI control created.

Recall all those "includes..." you see in C programs - which of course 
are paralleled in OO by M/F's Class-Control or 2002 Repository 
(Repository *IS* in N/E V 4.0) - I have all of the necessary GUI classes 
listed in TabbedDialog - and that's the only place they appear in an 
application.

Example # 3 - I illustrated to Daniel (X-Li) about using a super to 
"getIndexFromKey" or "getKeyFromIndex" in a droplist collection. It 
didn't start out that way. Softly, softly, catchee monkee. Each 
individual DBI (Database Interface) had its own version of the two 
methods above, plus the initialization code to create 
collections/dictionaries.  As I worked my way through there came an 
awareness that so much was 'common routines' - that's when I hit on the 
idea of a super class DbiMain with appropriate sub-classes DbiThis or, 
DbiThat inheriting the resources in DbiMain.

Granted, splitting a problem into many classes, you still have a fair 
sized system, but with stringent testing of each inherited class, (i.e. 
the class deasignated as the SUPER) you have reduced your testing when 
making future invocations for new classes/objects. Once you start 
'inheriting', particularly 'bland/polymorphic'  methods - I have no way 
of guaging, but would suggest a saving of something like 20% on 
repetitive code.

No way ! - if you are using big iron or small iron - you do NOT need 
LARGE OO programs. If they are LARGE, I'll put my money on it - your 
thinking/design  is dead wrong.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: *LARGE* COBOL programs - new SHARE requirement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T14:23:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T5Kqc.1356$Tn6.1178@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<E1BQD4J-0006Hy-00@granger.mail.mindspring.net> <Hxxqc.627$Tn6.513@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bOBqc.501401$Pk3.37699@pd7tw1no>`

```
I agree with you Jimmy that an OO "application" can (and POSSIBLY should) be
made up of "relatively" small classes.  However, I also believe that some -
especially any created by "high-level" design interfaces are NOT.

The same is true for applications for handling XML.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *LARGE* COBOL programs - new SHARE requirement

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T18:12:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2sNqc.506863$Pk3.105915@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<T5Kqc.1356$Tn6.1178@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<E1BQD4J-0006Hy-00@granger.mail.mindspring.net> <Hxxqc.627$Tn6.513@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bOBqc.501401$Pk3.37699@pd7tw1no> <T5Kqc.1356$Tn6.1178@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>I agree with you Jimmy that an OO "application" can (and POSSIBLY should) be
>made up of "relatively" small classes.  However, I also believe that some -
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Are you here referring to program generators, for want of a name, (SAS, 
SAP or whatever) ?  Being omnibus, it's natural they would generate a 
lot of code - but in the case of SAP I recall  an early statement from 
Donald that it was a proprietary language. (That fits in with when it 
was being implemented at one of his former clients, they had to type him 
a cheque for his 'services' because the SAP application wasn't ready  
:-)  ).  Perhaps with your Share background you are aware of program 
generators specifically written in COBOL ? (Just a thought - they could 
always go back to using Segmentation !).

>The same is true for applications for handling XML.
>
>  
>
As yet I know nothing of XML, other than looking at RM/Liant's manual - 
they did  a pretty good job.  I think I'm correct, but I believe it was 
Richard who wrote that XML was no big deal, the XML standard has a fixed 
template layout. Design your application so that you can fill in the 
pieces. From an OO perspective the prime template is in a class of its 
own, (or perhaps more accurately a 'template' file),  and the 'pieces' 
are, as necessary, in their own separate classes or more likely, 
retrieved from files or DB Tables. If I get there, subject to viability, 
I'd do my damnedest to take that approach.. Again such an approach 
allows you to 'fragment' into smaller classes/programs.

You still come up with a caveat,  "(and POSSIBLY should)". I wrote some 
time back that there was a Net Express developer who came unstuck 
because he wanted to add method # 1,001 onwards. I haven't seen his code 
- but it's a fair bet he took the  basic OO syntax template and wrapped 
it around a procedural program turning PERFORMS into methods. I wouldn't 
be at all surprised to find out that he was also using SECTIONS in 
methods as well !!!  Surprisingly, Simon who is a real whiz at the 
graphical interface stuff, produces fairly large methods and uses 
SECTIONS. That's in part attributable to the techniques he learned from 
procedural COBOL.  (You may recall that having adapted very early on to 
the SECTION concept, Pete still prefers to have SECTIONS in OO). I've 
never questioned him, (Simon), but probably the following is relevant. 
Being in a development shop, (I'd guess there might be 6 or 12 
programmers), the operative  phrase is "get it out now" - and unlike 
myself, that just doesn't allow time for them to ruminate and think 
about shortcut techniques, i.e.'supers' with sub-classes inheriting..

BTW - back to that DroplistDialog - another very good reason I hived it 
off as a  separate class. Take a look at the very first line "copy 
windows.cpy" - that is an absolute monster containing 28,215 lines of 
source and includes every typedef or hex value you would want to use 
with calls to Win APIs. Boy, does that slow down compilation of the 
DroplistDialog class. Included in the 'super' TabbedDialog, it would 
drive me nuts on re-compilations. (Because those windows.cpy routines 
are so integrated I doubt there's much M/F could do to segment into a 
hierarchical set of copyfiles).

The following is pure conjecture because I don't know the mechanism, but 
you may well have some insight from your M/F days. N/E has a neat 
feature I really like - it obviously puts a date/time stamp on source, 
including copyfiles.

1 - I change a comment line and re-compile - BLIP - it's done in 
seconds. The compiler doesn't really give a damn about comment lines.
2 - I change a source program - I can either 'compile this' or "compile 
project' - done in a very short time - seconds.
3 - I want a change in a copyfile used with this particular program - 
'compile project' - picks up and recompiles all programs affected by the 
copyfile change.

One would have thought that windows.cpy fits into # 3 - apart from which 
it is NEVER changed. I can only guess, because of its size, the compiler 
does a thorough check on the copyfile syntax, and perhaps somewhat like 
the REPOSITORY concept, forward checks your source code to see which 
variables etc you need from windows.cpy ?????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *LARGE* COBOL programs - new SHARE requirement

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T17:00:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405191600.2ccbcfa1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<E1BQD4J-0006Hy-00@granger.mail.mindspring.net> <Hxxqc.627$Tn6.513@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bOBqc.501401$Pk3.37699@pd7tw1no> <T5Kqc.1356$Tn6.1178@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <2sNqc.506863$Pk3.105915@pd7tw1no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote

> (or perhaps more accurately a 'template' file),  

I do a lot of stuff using template files.  Then I don't care whether
the output is XML, HTML, EDIFAC, or simple text.  One of the
advantages of having OO for this is that I can create two template
objects at the same time.

The latest project that I did was picking up EMail from a POP3 server,
extracting the attachments which are Excel .xls files, processing the
rows in each sheet by matching up on the headings and outputting the
delivery orders (by change of order number) into templated EDIFAC
files in various directories for the warehouse management systems to
collect.

Because the templating is a class I can also create a summary reply
EMail at the same time using a different template file and then at the
end of the xls file SMTP this to a list of receivers (including
warehouse managers, IT Manager and originating client) as
confirmation.

(it was actually done in Python, but same thing).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *LARGE* COBOL programs - new SHARE requirement

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T07:42:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wjZqc.540899$oR5.225491@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0405191600.2ccbcfa1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<E1BQD4J-0006Hy-00@granger.mail.mindspring.net> <Hxxqc.627$Tn6.513@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bOBqc.501401$Pk3.37699@pd7tw1no> <T5Kqc.1356$Tn6.1178@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <2sNqc.506863$Pk3.105915@pd7tw1no> <217e491a.0405191600.2ccbcfa1@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Whoa there Richard, that's the first time you've used the magic word 
'OO'  to my knowledge. I know you have a complete box of tricks down 
there, somewhat like Aladdin and his forty jars - specifically which 
languages are you using for OO - is Python in fact OO ?

On the 'template' bit one thing I have done, but by no means clean and 
comprehensive as yet, was to generate a source template for "Edit x" x = 
Customer, Chart-of-Accounts etc.

I have no doubt, you follow a similar approach - the majority of your 
'edit' programs follow the same pattern - mine of course is geared to a 
class approach

- (1)  Having determined my own particular pattern as to the sequencing 
of an Edit program, I have a 'generic' source of that program with 
'markers' and areas left blank - much like you have written in the past 
that you weren't happy with Screen Sections and you have arrived at your 
own design tool.

 - (2) The next step is to do a line sequential read against the Micro 
Focus generated Resource File from Dialog Editor information (see 
footnote) picking up on all the controls and generating (2a) another 
class source - DialogParameters which lists the controls in order by 
their ID's (Level 88's) and (2b) a separate copyfile of those Level 
88's. Bearing in mind I was unaware of the concept of Dialog System when 
I started with VISOC - to my surprise I found I had re-invented the 
wheel - Dialog System uses the same approach, giving each field a 
numbered identity and name (which you would know from your familiarity 
with M/F products).

- (3) Now a sequential read of the Edit template, using the information 
generated in (2)  is used to include method names in the generated 
'Editxxxx' class. These same 'return' method names, also included in 
DialogParameters are passed as parameters to create a dialog instance 
from the generic Dialog template class.

Now to the Edit program's sequence :-

- (4) File/DB Table housekeeping - identify external names and either 
OPEN or CONNECT
- (5) create any lists (collections) needed for Droplists, Litboxes, 
Treeviews, Listviews etc.
- (6) Pass parameters to the generic Dialog as required - see (3) above.

The Edit class - having included methods with  'header and footer', plus 
reference to a standard copy file in the return methods, gives you a 
pretty good, typo-free  checklist. Naturally you have to go in and hand 
code to make things specific - such as validity checks. for dialog 
return values. While the technique can be generalised, in both our cases 
our output is to our personal liking. That's where those BIG program 
generators fail, they are so generalised, trying to be everybody's 
friend, of necessity they generate a lot of superfluous  code, or 
possibly take you down a pretty convoluted route of condition/decision 
tables to generate what you are really after

I'm not demeaning your approach on inventory above, which I grasp, but 
that is a fairly standard one you can take a template approach to. (You 
*naughty man* - I see you used the word Excel - Jerry will be pleased  
=-O   )  Have you attempted templates/decision tables for things like 
Payroll etc. I get the impression that for the small guy to explore and 
design program generators for applications like that, all the time you 
put into the generator, and it is probably never finite - you might just 
as well be writing the intended resultant code. Most likely you got that 
finished and thoroughly tested before a decision generator has even 
started to flap its wings, let alone get off the ground !

What's your take on storage ?. I don't consider BIG is BEAUTIFUL; many 
have taken the approach, what the hell, storage is cheap just spread the 
stuff around guys ! I try to be circumspect on storage, keeping things 
as compact as possible I'm using Win 98 with 14 GBs - not at all big 
compared with current disk options, but when I use Norton to do a disk 
clean-up the resulting graphic picture shows that I use less than 30 % 
of disk space.

BTW - how's your female PM making out down there with the Maoris - have 
you 'nationalised' the seashore yet . It appeared here as a brief 
paragraph and nothing since.

Footnote : From time to time I have made reference to the M/F Dialog 
Editor as opposed to the Dialog System, a separate M/F tool which a 
number of people here are familiar with from their former M/F days.. If 
curious at all about Dialog Editor,  search the Windows directory for 
\mstools\bin\dlgedit.exe - the M/F version is an extension of what you 
see in the MS product.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *LARGE* COBOL programs - new SHARE requirement

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T12:50:18-07:00

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> Whoa there Richard, that's the first time you've used the magic word 
> 'OO'  to my knowledge.

I can't disagree about what your knowledge is   ;-)

> is Python in fact OO ?

Yes.

> I'm not demeaning your approach on inventory above, which I grasp, but 
> that is a fairly standard one you can take a template approach to. (You 

It seems that you are using templating to generate code, which I have
done, but isn't what I took your 'templating' as.

>> I think I'm correct, but I believe it was 
>> Richard who wrote that XML was no big deal, the XML standard has a
fixed
>> template layout. Design your application so that you can fill in
the
>> pieces. From an OO perspective the prime template is in a class of
its
>> own, (or perhaps more accurately a 'template' file),

Rereading this it seems that you are building the 'template' into the
code (or generating code from the template) such that it becomes a
fixed layout until the program is recreated and recompiled.

I don't do that.  A class handles text templates to create the output.
 It will handle most any templates.  A template may be:

:header
UNA:+.? '
UNB+UNOB:2+DRINKCO:ZZ+94108810LFXNA:ZZ+<!%datenow%>:<!%timenow%>+<!%number%>'
UNH+<!%number%>+INSDES:D:97A:UN'
BGM+220::6:INSDES+<!%orderno%>+9'
DTM+2:20<!%reqdate%>:102'
DTM+4:20<!%orddate%>:102'
RFF+VN:<!%orderno%>'
RFF+UO:<!%custref%>'
NAD+ST+<!%custid%>::91+<!%address1%>:<!%address2%>:<!%address3%>+<!%custname%>++++<!%zone%>'
FTX+ADU++1+<!%instruct%>'
:lineitem
LIN+<!%lineno%>+1'
PIA+1+<!%product%>:::91'
IMD+++<!%unit%>:::<!%descrip%>'
QTY+21:<!%quantity%>'
RFF+IL:<!%orderno%>?:<!%line%>'
RFF+BT:<!%batch%>'
:trailer
UNS+S'
CNT+2:<!%lineno%>'
UNT+<!%segcount%>+<!%number%>'
UNZ+1+<!%number%>'

Or it may be 

:header
To: <!%sendto%>
From: <!%username%>@<!%pop3%>
Subject: File <!%filename%>
<!%%>
This file is being processed by <!%system%>, version <!%version%>
<!%%>
.   DC    OrderNum Line Product Quantity
.   ------------------------------------
:lineitem
.   <!%location%>  <!%orderno%>  <!%line%>  <!%product%> 
<!%quantity%>
:trailer
<!%%>
Disclaimer:
The information in this electronic mail message together with any
attachments is confidential. It is intended solely for the addressee.
Access to this Internet electronic mail message by anyone else is
unauthorised. If you are not the intended recipient, any disclosure,
copying, distribution or any action taken or omitted to be taken in
reliance on it is prohibited and may be unlawful.
<!%%>
<!%company%> accepts no responsibility for any effects this email
message or
attachments has on the recipient network or computer system.
<!%%>
End of message 20<!%datenow%> <!%timenow%>
------------------------------------------------------

In fact these are two that are used.

If the user wants to change the output then they use a text editor and
change it and, well, it will just work.  If they want XML or HTML or
plain text they can do that too.  In fact I can do postscript
templates too, but that is a two stage process.

The advantages of having an OO Class do this, over say called
programs, is that the program can output both of the templates above
concurrently _without_ worrying about re-entrancy problems.

> *naughty man* - I see you used the word Excel - Jerry will be pleased  

Actually I had no control over what was used, I think SAP is
generating the file then the client fiddles it and EMails it.  I would
prefer they used gnumeric or OpenOffice, but its not a big deal.  At
the warehouse end it is all automatic being triggered by the arrival
of data in the inbox the program is monitoring.

> =-O   )  Have you attempted templates/decision tables for things like 

I did use Decision Tables on the ICL 1900 in the early 70s.

> BTW - how's your female PM making out down there with the Maoris - have 
> you 'nationalised' the seashore yet . It appeared here as a brief 
> paragraph and nothing since.

Still arguing, back biting, threatening.  SNAFU.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *LARGE* COBOL programs - new SHARE requirement

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T20:29:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3z8rc.546521$Ig.396004@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0405201150.628a3395@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<E1BQD4J-0006Hy-00@granger.mail.mindspring.net> <Hxxqc.627$Tn6.513@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bOBqc.501401$Pk3.37699@pd7tw1no> <T5Kqc.1356$Tn6.1178@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <2sNqc.506863$Pk3.105915@pd7tw1no> <217e491a.0405191600.2ccbcfa1@posting.google.com> <wjZqc.540899$oR5.225491@pd7tw3no> <217e491a.0405201150.628a3395@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>It seems that you are using templating to generate code, which I have
>done, but isn't what I took your 'templating' as.
>  
>
You are right. But template is such a generic word. Remember the ICL 
flowchart template - I've still got one kicking around.

No I don't currently use a template as you are which is part of your 
inter-active application. I thought it over, and although intrigued, I 
really can't think of a  requirement for that approach at the moment - 
but I can see it is bloody useful. Taking a cursory look at that RPV 
report writer, it appears to be using a template approach where you feed 
it with a block of data in a pre-defined format.

My approach to templates is twofold :-

- the program generator, where if you like I have a 'mask' of a source 
file to be re-written as a new output source, (repeating the 'chunks' 
that don't need to be changed) and using 'markers' to insert  new method 
syntax.  Strictly speaking it is not a 'program generator' but a 
'program copier', updated with the methods applicable to the current 
problem.

- the other which I described to Bill in this thread is a template or 
general purpose/polymorphic class for dialoging. An all-purpose class 
accepting input and returning event results, regardless of the dialog's 
shape or controls involved. Similarly I have templates for handling the 
four COBOL file types, generating as many instances as are currently 
required..

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-e84acf8960"></a>
## Thread: Training — 3 msgs

### Training

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2004-05-19T11:54:18+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8ekva$rfs$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`

```
This comment was made in another thread.

I think you underestimate the value of an hour invested in a training
session; more importantly, you underestimate - or demean - the ability of
human beings to learn new things.

For crying out loud, if someone can write COBOL programs, and has been doing
so since COBOL 74 (in which they certainly did not receive any formal
instruction very recently), surely they can handle it.

If your firm is unwilling to invest an hour or so in an employee, well, that
simply confirms the patronizing attitude expressed above.


Anyone who has lead a sizable maintenence effort will have had to deal with
the problem, "How the hell do we get the programmers to do it this way!!"

Anyone can be taught how to adopt a new method, but once they start dealing
with existing code that still does it the old way how do you stop them from
back-sliding into old habits?

The only method I have found that works is to have a systematic program in
place that forces individual staff to use the new methods several times.
All staff need to be forced to go through this process.

Having used the new methods quite a few times forces people to learn as
opposed to understand.

It also places the new methods in the existing base of software being
maintained,  Hence people keep coming across the new techniques repeatedly.
This acts as a refresher course.

But Jeez, it is such a battle.  At times you begin to understand all those
idiots that stand up and shout that its better to fire the old programmers
and hired new ones.
```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:loHqc.9237
> Um, I think this is called - and someone please correct me if I'm wrong
> here - "professional management."
>
You are wrong and I correct you:)
A professional manager is someone who deals with adults.  They set
objectives and so on.

What we are talking about here is "kindergarten teacher" or perhaps
"surrogate mother".

A manager recognises that there are problems in getting an orgnisation to
accept change and adopt new methodologies.  A manager will provide
motivation for the change, trainiing to support the change, introduce new
procedures to support the change.  But he is not prepared for programmers.

We have all introduced new software systems to "Users" and come up with
horror stories about how they resist change - however we only talk about
"Users" because no one could every believe the real stories about
programming teams.

Jim

> Many can lead the horses to water... but it takes a professional manager
> (which is not the same thing as a professional programmer!) to make them
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** prino@bigfoot.com (Robert AH Prins)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<ed6d5b69.0405190041.25e00670@posting.google.com>`

```
A while ago I posted a message to the mvshelp boards, see
<http://www.greenhousecafe.com/cgi-bin/ubb6/ultimatebb.cgi?ubb=get_topic;f=1;t=003181>
on this subject, noting that Gary Thompson's Quine page
<http://www.nyx.net/~gthompso/quine.htm> doesn't have quines in either
Cobol or PL/I. A bit later I added my own smallest(?) possible PL/I
quine, which was developed after an original Pascal one. Then
yesterday a Peter Brown added another first, a quine in Cobol! It's in
the same URL. Look and enjoy (and FWIW, see if you can reduce its
size)

Robert
```

### Re: GOBACK (was: Perform Thru/Go to vs. Perform - Compile Speed

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T04:41:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40ac2267.209277935@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<108r50eojhdo57@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0405131229.614f6cb0@posting.google.com> <40a55e57.325467891@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405151217.40a0de91@posting.google.com> <40a6b384.412821264@news.optonline.net> <c8anu7$4ct$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40a9176d.9829986@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405171838.3d7d72e2@posting.google.com> <40a997e8.42726445@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405181224.6051451e@posting.google.com> <40aab7c6.116430589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405182314.6d07d36d@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>each release. 

Dot Net fully supports VB6, including mixed-mode .NET programs calling VB6
programs.There is no requirement to rewrite older code. 

> In any case VB isn't BASIC, it is just one of dozens,
>or hundreds, of implementations.

VB and VBScript dominate the Basic marketplace.

>I wouldn't have the programmer do anything in particular, but then I'm
>not a control freak trying to impose my 'better ideas'.

There is truth in this statement. You don't offer many better ideas.

>Now you have invented a completely different situation which may or
>may not be better with a parameter, or may be perfectly usable with a
>shared data item.

I must add 'shared data item' to my lexicon. Definition: a temporary variable to
which data must be copied because the language is incapable of passing the
original variable.

>If you want to CALL using some parameters _and_ to use shared data in
>WS, then nested programs do that, especially if the code is of no use
>elsewhere.  Perhaps you should look up 'GLOBAL' as it applied to
>nested programs.

I dislike GLOBAL because it is variant of EXTERNAL and 'blank common'. It's a
mechanism to defeat 'data hiding' by passing data by stealth rather than
explicitly.

>Which is just silly.  There will be only one LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION and
>one LINKAGE SECTION, data will have to be distributed amongst these
>very carefully and care taken as to which items are valid at any
>particular time.

The ENTRY header lists the linkage section variables being passed. The compiler
_could_ diagnose errors. Local-Storage is setup complete for each instance,
therefore all references are valid.

>> >> Re-entrancy is for multi-threading. Windows callbacks are not an example
of
>> >> multi-threading.
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>> OS/2). It was not supported by Win9x and certainly not by Win3. MF support
for
>> reentrancy alone was not adequate. You needed a dispatcher and a way to lock
>> shared data.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>release stuff when 3.0 was just out.

Your argument and counter-example are based on a 'cooperative' (i.e. not
preemptive) operating system that was obsolete ten years ago. This is irrelevant
in a discussion about contemporary operating systems, where multitasking is the
norm.

>> >I doubt there is much call to make existing programs thread-safe.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>procedures.  These are specifically written for the task.

My client has many 'external procedures' written in Cobol and dispatched via a
primative 'listener' daemon talking through an 'Oracle pipe'. Procedures and the
pipe itself are single threaded. All users and procedures are blocked while a
procedure executes. The technology wasn't home-grown; it is and continues to be
delivered by SAP. 

Oracle provides an alternative 'listener' that IS multithreaded and assumes the
procedures are thread-safe, not just reentrant. The challenge is to make
existing Cobol procedures thread-safe.

IBM mainframers have a large volume of similar programs because DB2 makes it
easier. It supports Stored Procedures written in Cobol. It doesn't require them
to be External Procedures. It doesn't require the programmer do anything special
because it constructs a Working-Storage for each instance. In effect, it turns
Working-Storage into Local-Storage. CICS does the same.

>You should learn how machines with a single CPU do multi-tasking, it
>is called timeslicing. 

The operating system I wrote knew how to timeslice.

>> If you want to do more advanced things, such as parallel processing to make
>> your application SCALABLE, you can't do it with PERFORMed paragraphs. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>out-of-line.

Suppose the program needs to do process A, B and C. A traditional Cobol program
would say:
perform A
perform B
perform C
Execution time would be the sum of A+B+C. 

A multithreaded program would say:
start A
start B
start C
wait for A and B and C
Execution time would be the longest of the three.

>Your analogy is inappropriate, I may trust another dog's better ideas.

Quotation kept for its bile content.
```

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>

robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> The assumption is no dependence. For example:

You assume that at your peril.
 
> perform get-customer-information
> perform get-product-information

In my systems the customer type or class is used to set the price when
the product is retrieved.  An order usually has several products, so
that would follow on.

> perform get-order-form-template
> .. followed by ..
> perform present-order-form
> 
> Surely you've seen Cobol code like that. I have.

'Like that' maybe, without dependencies, no.

## Thread: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T04:41:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40ac36fe.214549585@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405182345.6c30d6e8@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Recursive Attributes in the chapter Language Fundamentals.""""

You are correct when you say Local-Storage tells the compiler to make the
program recursive, not by seeing that it calls itself. 

Bill Klein pointed out that 2002-compliant compilers expect you to say recursive
in the program-id clause. 

>> The compiler determines
>> you are doing recursion by seeing the program call itself.
>
>Recursion is not detected until run-time, the compiler does not
>determine this.

Wrong. The compiler must generate code to setup the stack on entry.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraphs, Nested Programs and/or ENTRY statements

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T04:11:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405200311.7afa5651@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<UYbqc.4901$SZ4.2596@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <10ainh95hjg06e5@corp.supernews.com> <szuqc.1127$be.96@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40aaaa7d.113029425@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0405182345.6c30d6e8@posting.google.com> <40ac36fe.214549585@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> You are correct when you say Local-Storage tells the compiler to make the
> program recursive, not by seeing that it calls itself. 

> >> The compiler determines
> >> you are doing recursion by seeing the program call itself.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Wrong. The compiler must generate code to setup the stack on entry.

The compiler notes that a program has Local-Storage and generates code
as required.  This allows for re-entrancy.  This is does whether the
program has any CALLs in it or not.

Recursion is 'self re-entering' and is only detectable from re-enter
by seeing that the caller and the called are the same, which is not
done by the compiler.  That is a program may be re-entered or it may
recurse.  L-S provides for both/either.
```

```
Hi there,
it's my first time here cause i'm italian vb developer.
Nevertheless i've got a question for you all (if you wish reply.)

I have a cobol archive that has 2.7 Mb size,but software that reads this
data
tells that if empty of data.

There is a way to repair broken database? A way to read this file using
another sistem?

Thanx
```

#### ↳ Re: Open a bad archive

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T09:50:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0c%qc.544247$Ig.29226@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9xZqc.298397$rM4.12494732@news4.tin.it>`
- **References:** `<9xZqc.298397$rM4.12494732@news4.tin.it>`

```
Sossio Li Boni wrote:

>Hi there,
>it's my first time here cause i'm italian vb developer.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Are you actually wanting to EXPORT the COBOL file data into CSV files 
and then IMPORT into a new database (DBMS/RDBMS)?

That's tricky, even if you know COBOL but don't have the compiler that 
the data files were originally created with. There are specific COBOL 
tools and some here, who wrote them, will tell you about them. But 
firstly you have to play Sherlock Holmes at your end so that we all know 
what we are talking about.  Have you got a COBOL compiler, which COBOL 
compiler and Version # ? I ask about the compiler because some vendors 
include a REBUILD tool. As regards your archive data files - a hint - 
can you pick out some files names, something like :-

Customer.dat and Customer.idx - same name different extensions. (This 
pair indicates an ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Mode), "DAT" indicates 
the data file and "IDX" or perhaps "INX" contains a table of the keys to 
the file "DAT". What other file extensions are there - or are there some 
with just Customer.dat but no Customer.idx ( or could be no "inx").

Which software were you trying to read the files with - if you tried to 
open a COBOL ISAM file with say, Word or Notepad - other than a few 
funny characters up front , you probably wont see anything else.

If you can't figure out the COBOL compiler - how old is the data - what 
is the date/time stamp for various files.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

ago
he told me that windows had advice him that periferal was bad or he had a
bad
support.I told him that probably was possible recover lost data in iomega
zip,
and using a recovery software i have recovered all files contained in zip
disk!
But in this group of files, there is one that has 2.6 Mb of size,
nevertheless
it results empty of data.
This is the question! Why? Are there data inside? It's possible to recover?

Thanks again (and excuse my bad bad english... ;-)
Johnny

- **References:** `<9xZqc.298397$rM4.12494732@news4.tin.it> <0c%qc.544247$Ig.29226@pd7tw2no> <Y5Crc.226994$hc5.9630026@news3.tin.it>`

```
On Sat, 22 May 2004 06:06:48 GMT, "Sossio Li Boni" <sossio@tin.it>
wrote:
Top posting corrected.

>
>
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As this is a file recovered from a damaged media then it is possible
that any data that the file had is now behind any salvation.

You should really look at the original files and see if they had any
data.

Also the fact that a file is "big" does not mean it has any data. If
this was for example a temporary "sort" file then it is possible that
all the records have been deleted by the COBOL program as part of the
normal run. Same could apply if this was "a" movements file and these
had just been archived to another file (before they had done the
backup).


And you still need to reply to Jimmy questions, e.g. what are the file
names like, and does your customer remember which COBOL was used!





Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

Hi there,
I urgently need to convert this code snippet to vb. Im not even positive it cobol...
Any help would be greatly appreciated.


IF  REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( W-COUNT ) NOT = SPACE
            SUBTRACT 1      FROM W-COUNT
      ELSE
      ADD  1  TO W-COUNT
      IF   REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( W-COUNT ) = 'T' OR 'V'
                  ADD  1  TO W-COUNT GIVING W-COUNT2
                  IF REGNO-DIGIT-DIV (W-COUNT2) IS NUMERIC
                              MOVE REGNO-DIGIT-DIV (W-COUNT) TO
                                    REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( 1 )
                              MOVE  SPACES TO REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( 2 )
                              MOVE  3      TO W-COUNT
                              PERFORM F000-FILL-IN-SPACE
                              UNTIL W-COUNT = W-COUNT2
                         MOVE 0  TO   W-COUNT
                  ELSE
                              MOVE 0  TO   W-COUNT
      ELSE
                        MOVE 0  TO   W-COUNT


COMPUTE W-TOTAL =  ( VALUE-DIGIT (1) +  VALUE-DIGIT (4) +  (
VALUE-DIGIT (2) + VALUE-DIGIT
                                          (5) ) * THREE + ( VALUE-DIGIT (3)
+  VALUE-DIGIT (6) ) * SEVEN )
         So our value will be . (22 + 3 +((36+2) * three) + ((32 + 8 ) *
seven )) = 419


DIVIDE W-TOTAL BY THIRTY-SEVEN GIVING W-DIV
ADD 1 TO W-REMAINDER

deks..

#### ↳ Re: Need to convert this code to VB

- **From:** Kramer <Kramer@seinfield.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T13:06:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40ACE59D.C97075FC@seinfield.com>`
- **References:** `<8d1a6405.0405200048.5467556a@posting.google.com>`

```
' *** Names cannot contain a minus so change it to an underscore.
IF  LEN( TRIM( REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( W-COUNT ))) > 0 THEN
              W-COUNT = W-COUNT - 1
        ELSE
              W-COUNT = W-COUNT + 1
        IF   REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( W-COUNT ) = "T" OR REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( W-COUNT ) = "V" THEN

                    W-COUNT2 =  W-COUNT + 1
                    IF IsNumeric(REGNO-DIGIT-DIV (W-COUNT2) ) THEN
                                REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( 1 )=  REGNO-DIGIT-DIV (W-COUNT)

                                REGNO-DIGIT-DIV ( 2 ) = " "
                                W-COUNT = 3
' *** You need to have a function or procedure for the next line of code.
' *** Names cannot contain a minus so make it an underscore :)
                                DO
                                F000_FILL_IN_SPACE
    LOOP UNTIL W-COUNT = W-COUNT2
                           W-COUNT = 0
                    ELSE
                                W-COUNT = 0
        ELSE
                          W-COUNT = 0
' *** SEVEN, THREE, THIRTY_SEVEN are not Keywords. Hence they must be defined as
variables.
  W-TOTAL =  ( VALUE-DIGIT (1) +  VALUE-DIGIT (4) +  (
  VALUE-DIGIT (2) + VALUE-DIGIT
                                            (5) ) * THREE + ( VALUE-DIGIT (3)
  +  VALUE-DIGIT (6) ) * SEVEN )
           So our value will be . (22 + 3 +((36+2) * three) + ((32 + 8 ) *
  seven )) = 419

  W-DIV =  W-TOTAL / THIRTY-SEVEN
  W-REMAINDER = W-TOTAL mod  THIRTY-SEVEN  + 1

#### ↳ Re: Need to convert this code to VB

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T19:30:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10at7q9akdhe0c4@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<8d1a6405.0405200048.5467556a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8d1a6405.0405200048.5467556a@posting.google.com>`

```
deks wrote:

This code is COBOL, but I don't think this does what it's supposed to, 
to begin with.  Some potential problems noted below...

> Hi there,
> I urgently need to convert this code snippet to vb. Im not even positive it cobol...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> seven )) = 419

Count the "IF"s and the "ELSE"s - the indentation of the COMPUTE verb 
would lead one to believe it's at the same nesting level as original IF, 
but it isn't.  If that is intentional, it's misleading.

> DIVIDE W-TOTAL BY THIRTY-SEVEN GIVING W-DIV
> ADD 1 TO W-REMAINDER

This looks like it's missing a remainder clause.  The DIVIDE verb, in 
and of itself, does not fill a remainder variable unless you tell it 
"REMAINDER W-REMAINDER" after the "GIVING W-DIV" and before the ADD 
statement.

> I can understand most of the syntax but there are a few keywords i am stuck with.

I can understand why you'd be stuck - this certainly isn't the most 
elegant COBOL I've ever seen.  It's far from the worst, though.  :) 
Below is a conversion of your code above.  I've turned the variables 
such as THREE and THIRTY-SEVEN into numbers...  I haven't compiled this, 
but it should be close.  I've also made the division fill the remainder 
before adding 1 to it, but I've commented that line out by using a 
single quote.  I also reversed the NOT logic in the code above, by 
making the positive condition stuff on the If leg instead of the Else leg.

(Of course, anything other than "If", "Trim", "Then", "Or", "isNumeric", 
"Do", "Until", "Loop", "End", and "Mod" is a variable you'll have to 
declare somewhere.)

-=-=-=-=- Begin VB Code -=-=-=-=-

If Trim(RegNo_Digit_Div(W_Count)) = "" Then
     W_Count = W_Count + 1
     If RegNo_Digit_Div(W_Count) = "T"
       Or RegNo_Digit_Div(W_Count) = "V" Then
         W_Count2 = W_Count + 1
         If isNumeric(RegNo_Digit_Div(W_Count2)) Then
             RegNo_Digit_Div(1) = RegNo_Digit_Div(W_Count)
             RegNo_Digit_Div(2) = ""
             W_Count = 3
             Do Until W_Count = W_Count2
'               You'll need to make this subroutine and pass the two
'               counter variables, unless they're global
                 Call F000_Fill_In_Space
             Loop
          End If
'       You've got this line on both the true and false legs of the
'       If statement - it only needs to be there once
         W_Count = 0
     Else
         W_Count = 0
'       If the compute should be at a different level, add "End If"s
'       as needed here, and remove the same number of "End If"s from
'       the bottom - also, correct the indentation for the sake of
'       future maintainers.  :)
         W_Total = (Value_Digit(1) + Value_Digit(4) + (Value_Digit(2) _
           + Value_Digit(5)) * 3 + (Value_Digit(3) + Value_Digit(6)) * 7)
         W_Div = W_Total / 37
'       Here's where the remainder would be filled
'       W_Remainder = W_Total Mod 37
         W_Remainder = W_Remainder + 1
     End If
End If

-=-=-=-=- End VB Code -=-=-=-=-

Use at your own risk...
```

<a id="t-1a56575ac2"></a>
## Thread: Local-Storage & Recursion — 9 msgs

### Local-Storage & Recursion

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-20T09:14:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LF_qc.2330$Tn6.438@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
To clarify things (a bit) from another thread,

1) The ISO 2002 Standard has a phrase "IS RECURSIVE" which may be added to the
Program-ID paragraph; when so coded the program has recursive attribute and may
be called recursively. This is supported in current Micro Focus and IBM (OS/390
& z/OS) compilers.

2) The ISO 2002 Standard has a Local-Storage Section which may be used by
recursive OR NON-RECURSIVE programs. This section is supported by both IBM and
Micro Focus. (When used by a "non-recursive" program it is similar - but not
quite the same as using WS for a program with the "IS INITIAL" attribute.)

3) Micro Focus has a documented extension, that WHEN a Local-Storage Section
appears in a program, that they "auto-magically" make the program have the
RECURSIVE attribute - regardless of what is coded in the Program-ID paragraph..
(This doesn't hurt - if you don't use it recursively)

4) IBM (mainframe) gets Local-Storage from "STACK" storage and Working-Storage
from HEAP storage.  (Different LE options apply to these)

5) In a recursive program, if you use Local-Storage with an ISO 2002 conforming
compiler it uses "push" and "pop" logic to get, save, and retrieve storage as
you go into and exit out of instances of the program.

   ***

To what extent other compilers have the RECURSIVE attribute, Local-Storage
Section, and/or use these in an ISO 2002 conforming manner, I cannot say.
```

#### ↳ Re: Local-Storage & Recursion

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T00:46:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40ad4e4d.286063391@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<LF_qc.2330$Tn6.438@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>2) The ISO 2002 Standard has a Local-Storage Section which may be used by
>recursive OR NON-RECURSIVE programs. This section is supported by both IBM and
>Micro Focus. (When used by a "non-recursive" program it is similar - but not
>quite the same as using WS for a program with the "IS INITIAL" attribute.)

Micro Focus does not initialize Local-Storage, no matter what attribute the
program has. VALUE clauses are ignored. 

Thread-Local-Storage _is_ initialized.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Local-Storage & Recursion

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T02:39:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Zdrc.519612$Pk3.519241@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<40ad4e4d.286063391@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<LF_qc.2330$Tn6.438@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40ad4e4d.286063391@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well "Yes" and 'No" - but note this is specifically LS used in OO :-

Example # 1 :
*>--------------------------------------------------
method-id. "displayNames".
*>--------------------------------------------------
Working-Storage Section.
01 Name1   pic x(xx) value "Hello Bill".
01 Name2  pic x(xx) value "Hello Robert".

*> Procedure Division - can be ignored here - no sending/receiving 
parameters.
display Name1
display Name2
End-method "displayNames".
*>----------------------------

Prior to J4 changing syntax rules above was acceptable and still is - a 
way of getting around LS not having values.
The reasoning for J4 disallowing Working-Storage in a method was that 
this could conflict with the same variable name if used in Global 
Working-Storage for the OO Instance. (you have a situation where, per 
instance, the method variables are not 'clean', i.e. in their initial 
state when a method is  invoked, and may be re-invoked many more 
times).  M/F's own rules still apply  if you use Variable A in both 
global W/S and a method; then the method Variable A overrides the global 
Variable A.  I can live with above, but I know somebody who still 
grumbles, grumbles about removing W/S, each time I correspond with him :-)

Example # 2 :
*>--------------------------------------------------
method-id. "displayNames2".
*>--------------------------------------------------
Local-Storage Section.
78  Name1   value "Hello Bill".
78  Name2   value "Hello Robert".
01 DisplayName    pic x(xx).

*> Procedure Division - can be ignored here - no sending/receiving 
parameters.
move Name1 to DisplayName
display DisplayName
move Name2 to DisplayName
display DisplayName
End-method "displayNames2".
*>----------------------------

Again of course, the nice shorthand Level 78 has been replaced by J4 by 
CONSTANT  - recent proposals have been made
by Chuck Stevens to accommodate the above with a CONSTANT structure. - 
due with COBOL 2008 - I await with bated breath !

In their usual pattern of backwards compatibility, Micro Focus will  no 
doubt support both the "old" and "new".

I've no idea what goes on at these J4 meetings - but sometimes I just 
can't help feeling that, "If M/F dreams it up, let's knock it down". 
Absolutely no evidence to support my suspicions - BUT  note in the case 
of disallowing W/S in a method the reasoning was legit - like Bill and 
some others, I was privy to non-J4 discussions.  The funny thing is we 
bitch about the '17' years to get to COBOL 2002 - but the 'Exclusion of 
W/S'  was handled with alacrity - it was up, running, discussed  and 
rubber-stamped by J4 before folks like myself had a chance to digest the 
ramifications of dealing with the above examples.
Who knows - maybe there is a case for taking 10 years to implement 
changes - seems like a reasonable period for everybody interested in a 
topic to give it some thought - the draft spec hopefully should be right 
at the end of a decade >:o               

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Local-Storage & Recursion

- **From:** stephen@gennard.net (Stephen Gennard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T02:03:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<735713a9.0405210103.1b7c6e87@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<LF_qc.2330$Tn6.438@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40ad4e4d.286063391@news.optonline.net>`

```
> Micro Focus does not initialize Local-Storage, no matter what attribute the
> program has. VALUE clauses are ignored. 
> 
> Thread-Local-Storage _is_ initialized.

Micro Focus Net Express v4.0 does initialize Local-Storage.

For example:

D:\>type a.cbl
        working-storage section.
        01 ws-count pic 99 value 0.
        local-storage section.
        01 ls-hi pic x(40) value "Hello world".
        procedure division.
                if ws-count equals 5
                   stop run
                end-if
                display ls-hi " - " ws-count
                move "some old crud" to ls-hi
                add 1 to ws-count
                call "a".

D:\>cobol a.cbl int();
Micro Focus Net Express V4
Version 4.0.38 Copyright (C) 1984-2004 Micro Focus International Ltd.
URN AXCGG/AA0/00000
* Checking complete with no errors - starting code generation
* Generating a
* Data:         592     Code:         648     Literals:         156

D:\>run a.int
Micro Focus Net Express V4.0.38
RUN TIME ENVIRONMENT Copyright (C) 1984-2004 Micro Focus International Ltd.
URN AXCGG/AA0/00000
Hello world                              - 00
Hello world                              - 01
Hello world                              - 02
Hello world                              - 03
Hello world                              - 04
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Local-Storage & Recursion

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-21T22:14:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Savrc.3998$Tn6.3910@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
I think it is pretty clear that as releases are provided (on the different
platforms) Micro Focus moves more and more toward conforming to the ISO 2002
Standard.  (They often/usually provide "downwardly compatible" compiler
directives and/or run-time switches for the "old" behavior - but it stops being
the "default".)
```

Hello world                              - 00

Hello world                              - 02
Hello world                              - 03
Hello world                              - 04

        working-storage section.
        01 ws-count pic 99 value 0.

        procedure division.
                if ws-count equals 5
                   stop run
                end-if
                display ls-hi " - " ws-count
                move "some old crud" to ls-hi
                add 1 to ws-count
                call "a".

[whale-hub-cobol]uname
AIX
[whale-hub-cobol]oslevel
5.2.0.0
[whale-hub-cobol]
