<a id="t-070b29c69b"></a>
## Thread: Programs expecting "initial values" — 1 this month / 57 total

### Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-05-31T20:59:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
IMHO ...

Any program that is expecting (requires) initial values (rather than "last used
state") for all Working-Storage items should have the "IS INITIAL" phrase in the
Program-ID paragraph.  (Those still using '74 Standard compilers need not reply
<G>)

Although each "instance" of a program will get Local-Storage in "initial state"
when activated for the first time, they WILL get Local-Storage in "last used
state" when that instance is "popped" back on the stack (in the case of
recursive programs) - if the compiler conforms to the ISO 2002 Standard.

If a program expects (requires) initial values for some data items and last-used
state for others, then they should NOT use "IS INITIAL" - but should use the
INITIALIZE statement (or equivalent) at the beginning of the application logic.

  ***

Obviously there are application specific (and/or compiler specific) reasons for
using other approaches, but "in general" it would seem that above should provide
"safe" programming techniques for programs that may or may NOT be called by
"unsafe" calling (activating) programs.
```

---

<a id="t-321b88b19e"></a>
## Thread: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program — 1 this month / 8 total

### Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-05-31T22:18:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0405312118.6c36def2@posting.google.com>`

```
>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>>If 'someone else calls your .dll' and gets your data values then
that
>>would be a serious issue, but what systems would this occur with ?

> Windows and Unix systems. 

The question was "_and_gets_your_data_".

You seem to have some strange ideas about what happens in a Cobol
run-unit.  While the .dll or .so contains the code and the initial
values for W-S the actual W-S and L-S is created in the run-unit's
address space.  If it abends then the data areas disappear too.

If two separate run units on the same machine both call the same .dll
or .so each run-unit has a new freshly created W-S (and L-S) etc in
its own address space.  If one abends (call it 'A') then the other
won't get A's data, nor will any new run-unit.

Now there is a problem with dlls that when a new version is compiled
or installed the old one may still be in memory and so calls will not
load the new one.  _That_ is why Windows may be rebooted.  It has
nothing to do with Cobol Working-Storage as you claim.

>>One assumes that you only use binary types for these variables. 
>>Packed and display variables may be just as poorly initialised as
>>occurs with ascii zero characters on packed.

> Packed-decimal, as used in Cobol, is a type defined by mainframe 
> hardware. 

Or by a software emulation of that hardware.

> To an Intel assembly language programmer, Packed means ...

Which is quite irrelevant.

> I use display numbers for DISPLAY on screens, reports and files. 
> There's no reason to use that format for temporary variables in 
> memory.

It is not particularly relevant what you do.  You made a suggestion
about what should be done by others.  I was pointing out that this was
useful only in circumstances where there were restricted usage types,
as you have in your programs.

Specifically the 'solution' may be just as bad as the 'problem'
depending entirely on what types are used.

> Pic 1 was an extension before 2002, so it means whatever the 
> vendor says. 

I recall using it in the 60s on ICL Cobol.

> It seems pointless to make it default to display. 

You should try reading the standard:

"If the USAGE clause is not specified .. the USAGE is implicitly
DISPLAY".

But then you never seem to care what the actual ANS' standards say.

> They already had pic 9. Why invent a new type to do the same thing?

PIC 1 may be 'boolean' and, just like in other languages, it is not
the same as PIC 9.

If PIC 1 is computational in nature then a MOVE [PIC 1(8) COMP] 
TO [PIC 1(8) DISPLAY] may unpack the bits to separate bytes.  PIC 9
won't do this.
```
